title: Clinical Examples
text: Publication-style meta-analyses and trial figures. Each example pairs a viz_forest() column with a textual col_interval() summary, then swaps the theme to match a target journal.

title: Clinical Examples
text:
JAMA-Style Forest Plot
Classic medical-journal style with clean typography:


Code
data(glp1_trials)

tabviz(
  glp1_trials,
  label = "study",
  group = "group",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    col_events("events", "n", "Events"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
  ),
  row_type = "row_type",
  row_bold = "row_bold",
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "GLP-1 Agonist Cardiovascular Outcomes",
  subtitle = "Major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE)"
)












TipTheme variations



The same data structure works with any theme. Try web_theme_nejm(), web_theme_nejm(), or web_theme_aurora() for different journal styles, or one of the LOTR presets (web_theme_dwarven() / web_theme_dwarven() / web_theme_dwarven()) for non-journal contexts. See Feature Showcases for a visual comparison of all seven built-in themes.

title: Clinical Examples
text:
Multi-Arm Dose-Response
Compare three treatment arms across multiple endpoints using col_group to organize results:


Code
dose_response <- data.frame(
  endpoint = c("Overall Survival", "Progression-Free Survival",
               "Objective Response", "Quality of Life"),
  low_hr  = c(0.88, 0.82, 0.85, 0.92),
  low_lo  = c(0.74, 0.68, 0.70, 0.78),
  low_hi  = c(1.05, 0.99, 1.03, 1.08),
  mid_hr  = c(0.76, 0.71, 0.74, 0.83),
  mid_lo  = c(0.62, 0.58, 0.60, 0.69),
  mid_hi  = c(0.93, 0.87, 0.91, 1.00),
  high_hr = c(0.65, 0.60, 0.62, 0.75),
  high_lo = c(0.51, 0.46, 0.48, 0.61),
  high_hi = c(0.83, 0.78, 0.80, 0.92)
)

tabviz(dose_response, label = "endpoint",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(
      effects = list(
        effect_forest("low_hr", "low_lo", "low_hi", label = "Low Dose"),
        effect_forest("mid_hr", "mid_lo", "mid_hi", label = "Standard"),
        effect_forest("high_hr", "high_lo", "high_hi", label = "High Dose")
      ),
      scale = "log", null_value = 1,
      axis_label = "Hazard Ratio"
    ),
    col_group("Low Dose",
      col_interval("low_hr", "low_lo", "low_hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    col_group("Standard",
      col_interval("mid_hr", "mid_lo", "mid_hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    col_group("High Dose",
      col_interval("high_hr", "high_lo", "high_hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    )
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Dose-Response Analysis",
  subtitle = "Three-arm randomized trial across key endpoints"
)

title: Clinical Examples
text:
Annotated Forest Plot
Combine reference lines with annotations, direction-coded marker colors, and significance-based styling in a single comprehensive example:


Code
annotated_data <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data") |>
  mutate(
    sig_color = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
      lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
      TRUE ~ "#64748b"
    ),
    sig_shape = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "circle",
      lower > 1 ~ "diamond",
      TRUE ~ "square"
    )
  )

tabviz(annotated_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n", "N"),
    col_events("events", "n", "Events"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_gridlines = TRUE,
               annotations = list(
                 refline(1, label = "No effect", style = "solid"),
                 refline(0.85, label = "Pooled estimate", style = "dashed", color = "#2563eb")
               )),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE)
  ),
  marker_color = "sig_color",
  marker_shape = "sig_shape",
  row_bold = ~ pvalue < 0.05,
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Cardiovascular Outcomes with Annotations",
  subtitle = "Direction-coded markers: green = significant benefit, gray = non-significant"
)

title: Clinical Examples
text:
Large-Scale Meta-Analysis
A stress test: 150 synthetic studies split into four regional panels via split_by. With shared_axis = TRUE, all panels share a single x-axis range, and the sidebar becomes a navigable tree — no endless scroll.


Code
set.seed(42)
n_studies <- 150
regions <- c("North America", "Europe", "Asia-Pacific", "Latin America")
region_pool <- sample(regions, n_studies,
                      replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.32, 0.30, 0.25, 0.13))

large_meta <- data.frame(
  study = sprintf("Study-%03d", seq_len(n_studies)),
  region = region_pool,
  n = round(pmax(60, rlnorm(n_studies, meanlog = 6.2, sdlog = 0.7))),
  hr = round(exp(rnorm(n_studies, mean = -0.18, sd = 0.22)), 3)
) |>
  mutate(
    se = 0.08 + 0.18 / sqrt(n / 200),
    lower = round(exp(log(hr) - 1.96 * se), 3),
    upper = round(exp(log(hr) + 1.96 * se), 3),
    weight = round(1 / se^2, 1),
    pvalue = round(2 * pnorm(-abs(log(hr) / se)), 4)
  ) |>
  select(-se)

tabviz(large_meta,
  label = "study",
  split_by = "region",
  shared_axis = TRUE,
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_bar("weight", "Weight"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_range = c(0.3, 2.5),
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)",
               annotations = list(
                 refline(1, label = "No effect", style = "solid"),
                 refline(0.83, label = "Pooled", style = "dashed", color = "#2563eb")
               )),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE)
  ),
  marker_color = ~ case_when(
    upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
    lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
    TRUE ~ "#64748b"
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Large-Scale Regional Meta-Analysis",
  subtitle = "150 synthetic studies across 4 regions, shared axis for cross-panel comparison",
  footnote = "Use the sidebar tree to navigate regions. Pooled estimate shown as blue dashed reference line."
)

title: Clinical Examples
text:
Nested Hierarchical Groups
Three levels of nesting (Region > Country > Site) give multi-center trial programs a compact, collapsible structure. Click any group header to collapse an entire branch and all its children.


Code
nested_data <- tibble(
  site = c(
    "Boston General", "Mass Eye & Ear", "Johns Hopkins", "Cleveland Clinic",
    "UCL Hospital", "Imperial College", "Charite Berlin", "LMU Munich",
    "Tokyo University", "Osaka Medical", "Peking Union", "Shanghai Ruijin"
  ),
  region = c(
    rep("americas", 4),
    rep("europe", 4),
    rep("asia_pacific", 4)
  ),
  country = c(
    "usa", "usa", "usa", "usa",
    "uk", "uk", "germany", "germany",
    "japan", "japan", "china", "china"
  ),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.68, 0.75, 0.71, 0.78, 0.82, 0.69, 0.74, 0.65, 0.70, 0.67, 0.72),
  lower = c(0.58, 0.52, 0.61, 0.56, 0.64, 0.68, 0.54, 0.59, 0.50, 0.55, 0.52, 0.57),
  upper = c(0.89, 0.89, 0.92, 0.90, 0.95, 0.99, 0.88, 0.93, 0.85, 0.89, 0.86, 0.91),
  n = c(245, 189, 312, 278, 156, 134, 298, 267, 445, 389, 512, 478)
)

tabviz(
  nested_data,
  label = "site",
  group = c("region", "country"),  # Hierarchical: region > country
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Hazard Ratio")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Nested Hierarchical Groups",
  subtitle = "Region > Country > Site (3 levels)",
  caption = "Click any group header to collapse that branch and all children"
)

title: Bilingual Dashboard (R + TS)
text: The Genetic Association example showcased the bilingual API on a forest plot. This page proves the same R ↔︎ TypeScript parity for the visual column family: col_bar / col_sparkline / col_badge / col_ring / col_stars. The R chunk renders the live widget; the OJS chunk loads @tabviz/core from esm.sh and mounts the equivalent TS-built spec — both shown on the same page.

title: Bilingual Dashboard (R + TS)
text:
Data: regional sales KPIs


Code
set.seed(2026)

regions <- c("North", "South", "East", "West", "Central")
revenue <- c(2400000, 1900000, 3200000, 1650000, 2750000)
growth  <- c(0.12, -0.04, 0.18, 0.03, 0.09)

# Per-region 12-month revenue sparkline (slight growth ± monthly noise).
sales_trend <- lapply(seq_along(regions), function(i) {
  base <- revenue[i] / 12
  trend <- seq(0, growth[i], length.out = 12)
  jitter <- rnorm(12, sd = 0.08)
  round(base * (1 + trend + jitter))
})

kpi <- data.frame(
  region        = regions,
  revenue       = revenue,
  growth        = growth,
  health        = c("green", "amber", "green", "red", "green"),
  satisfaction  = c(4.5, 3.2, 4.8, 2.9, 4.1),
  pipeline_pct  = c(0.78, 0.45, 0.92, 0.38, 0.71)
)
kpi$trend <- I(sales_trend)

# Expose to OJS (plus the locked Svelte version + @tabviz/core version,
# same pattern as the genetic-association page).
svelte_version <- jsonlite::fromJSON(
  "../../srcjs/package-lock.json"
)$packages[["node_modules/svelte"]]$version
tabviz_core_version <- jsonlite::fromJSON(
  "../../srcjs/package.json"
)$version

ojs_define(kpi_in = kpi,
           svelte_version = svelte_version,
           tabviz_core_version = tabviz_core_version)

title: Bilingual Dashboard (R + TS)
section: R: tabviz(...) rendering the live widget
text:
R: tabviz(...) rendering the live widget


Code
tabviz(
  kpi,
  label = "region",
  label_header = "Region",
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  columns = list(
    col_currency("revenue", header = "Revenue", symbol = "$", decimals = 0,
                 thousands_sep = ",", abbreviate = TRUE),
    col_bar("growth", header = "YoY growth", scale = "linear"),
    col_sparkline("trend", header = "12-month trend", type = "area"),
    col_badge("health", header = "Health"),
    col_stars("satisfaction", header = "CSAT", max_stars = 5),
    col_ring("pipeline_pct", header = "Pipeline", min_value = 0, max_value = 1)
  ),
  title = "Regional sales KPIs",
  caption = "Quarter-end snapshot. Revenue abbreviated; sparkline shows last 12 months."
)

title: Bilingual Dashboard (R + TS)
section: TypeScript via @tabviz/core, rendered in the browser

title: Bilingual Dashboard (R + TS)
text:
What’s the same, what’s different?
Same as the genetic-association page — argument names + defaults map snake_case ↔︎ camelCase, positional R args become named TS args, and theme = web_theme_nejm() becomes theme: "cochrane". The visual column family (bars, sparklines, badges, rings, stars) has the same mechanical translation as the forest-plot family.

title: The Preset Gallery
text:
The fastest road to a publication-ready table is not building a theme from inputs — it’s picking the preset whose identity matches your document, then swapping in your brand color:
Code
tabviz(df, ..., theme = set_brand(web_theme_ledger(), "#1F3A5F"))
Every preset below renders the same five-row meta-analysis, so the only thing changing is the theme. Identity is more than color: presets pin typography, density, corner radius, shell texture, mark shapes, effects (glow / gradient / glass), and status palettes — and a CI gate enforces that no two presets converge.
Code
demo <- data.frame(
  study = c("Alpha 2024", "Beta 2023", "Gamma 2022", "Delta 2021", "Epsilon 2024"),
  n     = c(245, 189, 312, 278, 478),
  hr    = c(0.72, 0.81, 0.66, 0.74, 0.91),
  lo    = c(0.58, 0.65, 0.50, 0.59, 0.78),
  hi    = c(0.89, 1.01, 0.86, 0.93, 1.06),
  p     = c(0.004, 0.062, 0.0008, 0.011, 0.23)
)

show <- function(theme, tag = NULL) {
  tabviz(demo,
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      col_n("n"),
      col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
      col_pvalue("p", "P", stars = TRUE),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Hazard ratio")
    ),
    theme = theme,
    title = theme@name,
    tag = tag
  )
}
The roster is the four registers package_themes() groups by: one clinical default, two editorial voices, two design movements, and four expressive moods. Each preset owns a distinct expressive axis.

title: The Preset Gallery
text:
Clinical
Restrained clinical authority — the package default.


Code
show(web_theme_nejm())

title: The Preset Gallery
text:
Editorial
Typographic register: a warm-grain newsprint and a Cinzel-display fantasy serif.


Code
show(web_theme_newsprint())








Code
show(web_theme_dwarven(), tag = "Table 1")

title: The Preset Gallery
text:
Design
Design-movement identities — color discipline and hard geometry.


Code
show(web_theme_ledger())








Code
show(web_theme_brutalist())

title: The Preset Gallery
text:
Expressive
The expressivity ceiling: glow, glass, neon, phosphor aliasing, and cyanotype grid — the moods that exercise the full surface.


Code
show(web_theme_aurora())








Code
show(web_theme_terminal())








Code
