Code
show(web_theme_synthwave())

title: The Preset Gallery
text:
Make it yours
Three moves cover most customization, in order of reach:


Code
# 1. Swap the brand — everything identity re-derives from one seed.
my_theme <- set_brand(web_theme_ledger(), "#1F3A5F")

# 2. Pull the supporting hues toward the brand (subtle / medium / vivid).
my_theme <- tint_from_brand(my_theme, "subtle")

# 3. Reach for a focused setter when one axis needs attention:
my_theme <- my_theme |>
  set_density("compact", factor = 0.95) |>
  set_effects(glow_intensity = "subtle", title_style = "bar") |>
  set_status_palette(list(negative = "#D42320")) |>
  set_pin("--tv-ink2", "#862721")             # rubrication ink (pinnable token)


The full input model — anchors, polarity, monochrome, geometry, marks, effects — is the Themes guide; every argument is in the Themes reference. For an end-to-end worked example, see Compose Your Own Brand.

title: Genetic Association (R + TS)
text:
When tabviz was just an R package, “the JS code” meant “the bundled htmlwidget runtime” — opaque to consumers, only useful as a black box behind the R API. As of 0.2.0, the JS side is a first-class authoring target: the same tabviz() / col_*() / viz_*() / theme_*() API mirrored in TypeScript, available from npm as @tabviz/core.
This page renders the same forest plot twice on the same page — once via R, once via TypeScript loaded over the wire from esm.sh. The pixels should match.

title: Genetic Association (R + TS)
section: Data: genetic associations with cardiovascular events
text:
Data: genetic associations with cardiovascular events
A small GWAS-style result table — SNP-level effect estimates from a polygenic-risk-score study. Rows are SNPs; columns carry the locus name, allele frequency, effect size + CI, sample size, and a p-value. A forest plot on the right shows the effect estimates aligned with the table.


Code
snps <- data.frame(
  snp         = c("rs7412",   "rs429358",  "rs2230806", "rs5174",   "rs1801133",
                  "rs5882",   "rs7041",    "rs662",     "rs6025",   "rs1799963"),
  gene        = c("APOE",     "APOE",      "ABCA1",     "LRP1",     "MTHFR",
                  "CETP",     "GC",        "PON1",      "F5",       "F2"),
  maf         = c(0.07,       0.14,        0.22,        0.41,       0.30,
                  0.35,       0.40,        0.31,        0.04,       0.02),
  effect      = c(-0.18,      0.28,        -0.11,       0.07,       0.09,
                  -0.13,      0.05,        0.10,        0.34,       0.41),
  lower       = c(-0.27,      0.19,        -0.18,       0.02,       0.02,
                  -0.20,     -0.01,       0.03,        0.18,        0.21),
  upper       = c(-0.09,      0.37,        -0.04,       0.12,       0.16,
                  -0.06,      0.11,       0.17,        0.50,        0.61),
  n_studies   = c(34,         38,          12,          9,          21,
                  17,         8,           14,          22,          11),
  p_value     = c(3.2e-5,     1.4e-9,      4.7e-3,      0.018,      0.014,
                  2.6e-4,     0.067,       7.3e-3,      1.1e-5,      2.1e-5)
)

# Expose the data + the Svelte version @tabviz/core was compiled against
# to the OJS runtime below, so the esm.sh import can lockstep-pin and
# avoid the "validate_each_keys is not a function" peer-resolution drift.
# Read the *locked* version from package-lock.json — devDeps in
# package.json is a range spec (e.g. "^5.0.0") which esm.sh would resolve
# to whatever 5.x is current.
svelte_version <- jsonlite::fromJSON(
  "../../srcjs/package-lock.json"
)$packages[["node_modules/svelte"]]$version

# Read the @tabviz/core version from srcjs/package.json so esm.sh
# imports stay in lockstep with whatever's published. Future version
# bumps auto-propagate to the docs on the next quarto render.
tabviz_core_version <- jsonlite::fromJSON(
  "../../srcjs/package.json"
)$version

ojs_define(snps_in = snps,
           svelte_version = svelte_version,
           tabviz_core_version = tabviz_core_version)

title: Genetic Association (R + TS)
section: R: tabviz(...) rendering the live widget
text:
R: tabviz(...) rendering the live widget


Code
tabviz(
  snps,
  label = "snp",
  label_header = "SNP",
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  columns = list(
    col_text("gene", header = "Gene"),
    col_numeric("maf", header = "MAF", decimals = 2),
    col_interval("effect", "lower", "upper", header = "Effect (95% CI)"),
    col_n("n_studies", header = "Studies"),
    col_pvalue("p_value"),
    viz_forest(
      point = "effect", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
      axis_label = "Effect estimate",
      scale = "linear",
      null_value = 0
    )
  ),
  title = "Genetic associations with cardiovascular events",
  caption = "Effect = log odds ratio per allele; CI = 95%."
)

title: Genetic Association (R + TS)
section: TypeScript via @tabviz/core, rendered in the browser

title: Genetic Association (R + TS)
text:
What’s the same, what’s different?
The argument names, defaults, and shape of the returned spec are identical:

R: col_interval("effect", "lower", "upper") ↔︎ TS: colInterval({ point: "effect", lower: "lower", upper: "upper" })
R: snake_case kwargs ↔︎ TS: camelCase keys
R: theme = web_theme_nejm() ↔︎ TS: theme: "lancet" (or theme: themeLancet())

R is positional-friendly (col_* helpers take field as the first positional arg); TS is purely named-argument because it’s a single object literal. Beyond that, the helpers are mechanical 1:1 translations — change one to fit the other by remembering snake-case ↔︎ camelCase and keyword-vs-positional.

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
The V4 theme system is built so authoring a branded theme is a sequence of single-input edits — each one obviously about one thing — rather than a big monolithic web_theme_acme() wrapper to bisect when something goes wrong. This page walks the build end-to-end for a fictional brand, Acme Cardio Research, with the rendered table at every step so you can see exactly which input controls what.
Each step is one named input on web_theme() (or one set_*() call). At the end, we wrap the result as a one-shot constructor.

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
The shared dataset
Every step renders the same eight-row meta-analysis so the only thing changing across the renders is the theme:


Code
acme_data <- data.frame(
  study  = c("ACME-101", "PROVE-IT", "CADENCE", "VANGUARD-2",
             "STELLAR", "ENDEAVOUR", "SUMMIT", "Pooled"),
  cohort = c("Phase III", "Phase III", "Phase III",
             "Real-world", "Real-world", "Real-world",
             "Registry",   "Pooled"),
  n      = c(2400, 1850, 3120, 980,  4200, 2180, 1640,  16370),
  hr     = c(0.78, 0.82, 0.74, 0.85, 0.79, 0.81, 0.88,  0.79),
  lower  = c(0.65, 0.71, 0.62, 0.69, 0.69, 0.70, 0.74,  0.74),
  upper  = c(0.93, 0.95, 0.88, 1.05, 0.91, 0.94, 1.05,  0.85),
  pvalue = c(0.005, 0.008, 0.0001, 0.13, 0.0002, 0.005, 0.18, NA),
  row_type = c(rep("data", 7), "summary"),
  is_pooled = c(rep(FALSE, 7), TRUE)
)

render_acme <- function(theme, title = NULL, subtitle = NULL) {
  tabviz(acme_data,
    label = "study",
    group = "cohort",
    columns = list(
      col_n("n"),
      col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1,
                 axis_label = "Hazard ratio (95% CI)"),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    row_type = "row_type",
    row_fill = "is_pooled",
    theme = theme,
    title = title %||% "Acme Cardio Research — pooled meta-analysis",
    subtitle = subtitle
  )
}

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 0 — start from web_theme_nejm()
The package default is the cleanest, broadest-use preset; everything that follows is a delta from this baseline.


Code
render_acme(
  theme    = web_theme_nejm(),
  subtitle = "Step 0 — package default"
)

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 1 — pick the brand identity
Acme’s brand seed is #1E3A5F — a deep editorial navy. Everything identity (marker fills, title color, the brand ramp behind headers and gradients) derives from this one V4 anchor.


Code
acme_t1 <- web_theme(brand = "#1E3A5F")

render_acme(acme_t1, subtitle = "Step 1 — navy brand")

title: Compose Your Own Brand
section: Step 2 — tint the neutrals toward the brand
text:
Step 2 — tint the neutrals toward the brand
The neutral ramp (paper → ink) drives every surface tone, divider, and the chrome body-text color. Rather than hand-picking greys, nudge the paper/ink anchors’ hues toward the brand in one move with [tint_from_brand()] — "subtle" travels 30% of the angular hue distance, leaving lightness untouched. Acme wants a quietly cool, editorial chrome.


Code
acme_t2 <- tint_from_brand(acme_t1, "subtle")

render_acme(acme_t2, subtitle = "Step 2 — brand-tinted neutrals")






Notice the marker fill, the title color, and the cell-hairline tint all picked up the new identity at once. The chrome cascade does this automatically — there’s no per-component knob to flip.

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 3 — pick the accent (engagement layer)
accent is reserved for engagement: hover, selected rows, semantic row callouts (row_accent), the selection edge, and the status.info fallback. Never identity — keep it visually distinct from brand. Acme picks a warm amber to counterpoint the cool navy.


Code
acme_t3 <- web_theme(
  brand  = "#1E3A5F",
  accent = "#C8893F"           # amber engagement
) |>
  tint_from_brand("subtle")

render_acme(acme_t3, subtitle = "Step 3 — amber accent")

title: Compose Your Own Brand
section: Step 4 — series colors for multi-effect plots
text:
Step 4 — series colors for multi-effect plots
Per-effect colors (for multi-arm forest, grouped bars, multiple boxplots per row) are derived — the resolved series slot bundles come from the brand identity plus the categorical data scheme; the first slot also drives the pooled-effect diamond. You don’t pin them as an input. To choose the scheme, set categorical; to override a single resolved slot fill, reach for set_theme_field(c("series", i, "fill"), …).


Code
acme_t4 <- web_theme(
  brand  = "#1E3A5F",
  accent = "#C8893F",
  categorical = "okabe_ito"    # the per-effect data palette
) |>
  tint_from_brand("subtle") |>
  # pin the first series slot to the brand navy (drives the pooled diamond)
  set_theme_field(c("series", 1L, "fill"), "#1E3A5F")

render_acme(acme_t4, subtitle = "Step 4 — derived series palette (single-effect here)")






The single-effect forest in this example only consumes the first slot; switch to a multi-effect effect_forest() list and the rest cycle in from the categorical scheme.

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 5 — fonts
Acme picks an editorial serif for display copy and a humanist sans for body. fonts_display falls back to fonts_body when unset, so we name both explicitly.


Code
acme_t5 <- web_theme(
  brand        = "#1E3A5F",
  accent       = "#C8893F",
  categorical  = "okabe_ito",
  fonts_body    = "'IBM Plex Sans', system-ui, sans-serif",
  fonts_display = "'IBM Plex Serif', Georgia, serif",
  web_fonts = list(
    web_font("IBM Plex Sans",
             "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Sans:wght@400;500;600&display=swap"),
    web_font("IBM Plex Serif",
             "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Serif:wght@500;600&display=swap")
  )
) |>
  tint_from_brand("subtle")

render_acme(acme_t5, subtitle = "Step 5 — IBM Plex Sans + Serif via web_fonts")






The widget injects <link rel="stylesheet"> tags on mount for any web_fonts declared on the theme; SVG / PNG / PDF export embeds the @font-face rules into the SVG so the static output carries its glyphs (when a fallback is needed, the browser substitutes — see Themes guide for details).

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 6 — pick variants
Variants are the per-table toggles that don’t change identity. Acme picks comfortable density and a tint header — a brand-tinted band that sits halfway between the default light header and the maximum-chrome bold header.


Code
acme_t6 <- web_theme(
  brand        = "#1E3A5F",
  accent       = "#C8893F",
  categorical  = "okabe_ito",
  fonts_body    = "'IBM Plex Sans', system-ui, sans-serif",
  fonts_display = "'IBM Plex Serif', Georgia, serif",
  density            = "comfortable",
  header_style       = "tint",
  first_column_style = "default",
  web_fonts = list(
    web_font("IBM Plex Sans",
             "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Sans:wght@400;500;600&display=swap"),
    web_font("IBM Plex Serif",
             "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Serif:wght@500;600&display=swap")
  )
) |>
  tint_from_brand("subtle") |>
  set_theme_field(c("series", 1L, "fill"), "#1E3A5F")

render_acme(acme_t6, subtitle = "Step 6 — comfortable density, tint header")

title: Compose Your Own Brand
section: Step 7 — pin a single field with set_theme_field()
text:
Step 7 — pin a single field with set_theme_field()
Almost every theme can be expressed entirely through Tier 1 inputs. The one place you reach for set_theme_field() is when you want to override a single derived field without disturbing the rest of the cascade — e.g., turning gridlines on, forcing a specific axis tick color, or pinning the row-group L1 background to a brand-specific shade.
Here, Acme wants visible axis gridlines (default off) and a slightly stronger tick label:


Code
acme_t7 <- acme_t6 |>
  set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE) |>
  set_theme_field(c("plot", "tick_label", "weight"), 500)

render_acme(acme_t7, subtitle = "Step 7 — gridlines on, slightly stronger tick label")






These overrides survive subsequent set_inputs() calls — the resolver fills NA-valued fields and leaves explicit pins alone.

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
Step 8 — wrap as a function
Once you’re happy, wrap the whole theme in a named function for team-wide use. Anyone on your team gets a one-line theme without learning the input model.


Code
web_theme_acme <- function() {
  web_theme(
    name         = "acme",
    brand        = "#1E3A5F",
    accent       = "#C8893F",
    categorical  = "okabe_ito",
    fonts_body    = "'IBM Plex Sans', system-ui, sans-serif",
    fonts_display = "'IBM Plex Serif', Georgia, serif",
    density            = "comfortable",
    header_style       = "tint",
    first_column_style = "default",
    web_fonts = list(
      web_font("IBM Plex Sans",
               "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Sans:wght@400;500;600&display=swap"),
      web_font("IBM Plex Serif",
               "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Serif:wght@500;600&display=swap")
    )
  ) |>
    tint_from_brand("subtle") |>
    set_theme_field(c("series", 1L, "fill"), "#1E3A5F") |>
    set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE) |>
    set_theme_field(c("plot", "tick_label", "weight"), 500)
}

# Now the whole team writes:
render_acme(web_theme_acme(), subtitle = "Step 8 — wrapped as web_theme_acme()")

title: Compose Your Own Brand
section: Putting it on a different table
text:
Putting it on a different table
Same theme, different data — column types and content shift, the brand stays consistent:


Code
sales <- data.frame(
  region   = c("Americas", "Europe", "Asia-Pac", "MENA"),
  q1       = c(2.4, 1.9, 2.1, 0.7),
  q2       = c(2.7, 2.0, 2.3, 0.8),
  q3       = c(3.1, 2.2, 2.5, 0.9),
  q4       = c(3.4, 2.4, 2.8, 1.0),
  yoy      = c(0.18, 0.11, 0.16, 0.22)
)

tabviz(sales, label = "region",
  columns = list(
    col_currency("q1", "Q1", decimals = 1),
    col_currency("q2", "Q2", decimals = 1),
    col_currency("q3", "Q3", decimals = 1),
    col_currency("q4", "Q4", decimals = 1),
    col_percent("yoy", "YoY"),
    viz_bar(effect_bar("q4"), header = "Q4 ($B)", axis_range = c(0, 4))
  ),
  theme = web_theme_acme(),
  title = "Acme Cardio — regional revenue",
  subtitle = "Same theme function, different schema."
)

title: Compose Your Own Brand
text:
See also

Themes — Guide — the conceptual model of inputs / variants / overrides.
Themes — Reference — every web_theme() argument and set_*() modifier.
Theming Manifesto — why the cascade is shaped this way (two-knob principle, three-axis contract, anti-patterns).
LOTR (Easter Egg) — three real shipped examples of theme functions wrapped this same way.

title: Domain Dashboards
text: tabviz() works as well outside the journal as inside. This page collects dense, information-rich dashboards from six domains — sports analytics, customer feedback, portfolio performance, product metrics, airline ops, climate science — built with the same columns = list(...) API. Each example leans into the column types that fit the domain (rings for percentages, pictograms for counts and ratings, sparklines for trends, viz_forest for confidence intervals on any interval-shaped metric).

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
Sports analytics — NBA player efficiency
Three dimensions in one row: shooting efficiency (rings — FG% / 3P% / FT%), accumulated honors (pictograms in rating mode for All-Star appearances), and a focal forest plot for Player Efficiency Rating with 95% CIs. Row groups by conference; row accent on All-Stars.


Code
data(nba_efficiency)

# Synthesize a few realistic-feeling secondary stats so we can exercise
# col_ring / col_pictogram / viz_bar on top of the per-CI forest.
set.seed(2024)
nba_dash <- nba_efficiency |>
  mutate(
    fg_pct      = round(runif(n(), 0.42, 0.58), 3),
    three_pct   = round(runif(n(), 0.30, 0.42), 3),
    ft_pct      = round(runif(n(), 0.70, 0.92), 3),
    all_star_n  = pmin(15L, sample(0:9, n(), replace = TRUE) +
                            ifelse(all_star, 1L, 0L)),
    is_mvp      = award == "MVP"
  )

tabviz(nba_dash,
  label = "player",
  group = "conference",
  columns = list(
    col_group("Player",
      col_text("team", "Team", width = 50),
      col_text("position", "Pos", width = 40)
    ),
    col_group("Volume",
      col_numeric("games", "GP", decimals = 0),
      col_numeric("ppg", "PPG", decimals = 1)
    ),
    col_group("Shooting",
      col_ring("fg_pct",    header = "FG%",
               thresholds = c(0.45, 0.52)),
      col_ring("three_pct", header = "3P%",
               thresholds = c(0.34, 0.39)),
      col_ring("ft_pct",    header = "FT%",
               thresholds = c(0.75, 0.85))
    ),
    col_pictogram("all_star_n", header = "All-Star apps",
                  glyph = "star", max_glyphs = 15,
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer"),
    viz_forest(point = "per", lower = "per_lower", upper = "per_upper",
               null_value = 15,
               axis_label = "Player Efficiency Rating (95% CI)"),
    col_badge("award", "Award",
      variants = list(
        MVP                = "success",
        `All-NBA 1st`      = "info",
        `All-NBA 2nd`      = "warning",
        `All-NBA 3rd`      = "muted",
        `Defensive POY`    = "info",
        `6th Man`          = "muted",
        `Most Improved`    = "muted",
        `Rookie of Year`   = "muted",
        `All-Star`         = "muted"
      ))
  ),
  row_accent = "all_star",       # bold + theme accent on All-Stars
  row_fill   = "is_mvp",         # strong fill on MVP row
  theme = web_theme_nejm() |>
    set_inputs(brand = "#1d428a", accent = "#c8102e"),  # NBA blue + red
  title    = "NBA Player Efficiency Dashboard",
  subtitle = "PER = 15 is league average. Rings show shooting efficiency by typical-range thresholds.",
  caption  = "All-Star rows accented; MVP row gets the row.fill token."
)












TipWhat this exercises




col_ring() with thresholds: each shooting-percentage column flips color at a typical-range cutoff (e.g. FG% green ≥ 0.52, amber 0.45–0.52, red < 0.45). No hex literals — the cascade resolves to the theme’s status palette.
col_pictogram() rating mode: max_glyphs = 15 with value_label = "trailing" shows accumulated All-Star appearances visually + numerically.
row_accent and row_fill together: the 26 All-Stars take the accent token (bold + accent color), and the single MVP row layers the fill token on top — strongest visual hierarchy reserved for the rarest case.
Custom-tinted theme via set_inputs() — NBA blue + red without rebuilding from scratch.

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
Customer satisfaction survey
Likert-style satisfaction scores as rings (with auto-defaulted positive / warning / negative palette), detractor counts as pictograms, response progress as bars. Row fill flags the segment with the strongest result; row accent flags any with concerning scores.


Code
survey <- data.frame(
  segment      = c("Enterprise — strategic", "Enterprise — mid-market",
                   "SMB — paid",             "SMB — self-serve",
                   "Education",              "Public sector"),
  responses    = c(412, 287, 1820, 4560, 198, 142),
  response_rate= c(0.71, 0.58, 0.32, 0.18, 0.62, 0.49),
  csat         = c(0.87, 0.82, 0.74, 0.66, 0.89, 0.78),         # 0–1
  nps          = c( 62,  48,  31,   8,  74,  44),                # -100 to 100
  ces          = c(0.82, 0.76, 0.68, 0.55, 0.85, 0.71),
  detractors   = c(  2,   3,   8,  14,   1,   4),
  promoters    = c(  9,   8,   6,   3,  11,   7),
  trend        = I(list(
    c(78, 80, 82, 85, 87), c(72, 75, 78, 80, 82),
    c(65, 68, 70, 72, 74), c(60, 62, 64, 65, 66),
    c(82, 85, 87, 88, 89), c(70, 73, 75, 77, 78)
  )),
  is_top       = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE),     # row_fill
  is_concern   = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE,  FALSE, FALSE)     # row_accent
)

tabviz(survey,
  label = "segment",
  columns = list(
    col_n("responses", "N"),
    col_progress("response_rate", "Response rate", max_value = 1,
                 show_label = TRUE),
    col_group("Satisfaction",
      col_ring("csat", header = "CSAT", thresholds = c(0.70, 0.85)),
      col_ring("ces",  header = "CES",  thresholds = c(0.65, 0.80))
    ),
    col_numeric("nps", "NPS", decimals = 0),
    col_pictogram("detractors", header = "Detractors",
                  glyph = "person", layout = "row"),
    col_pictogram("promoters",  header = "Promoters",
                  glyph = "person", layout = "row"),
    col_sparkline("trend", "5q trend")
  ),
  row_fill   = "is_top",
  row_accent = "is_concern",
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title    = "Customer satisfaction by segment",
  subtitle = "CSAT and CES bands set per the program's typical-range thresholds; trend on the right.",
  caption  = "SMB self-serve flagged for response-rate concern; Education leads on every metric."
)












NoteWhen to reach for what




Use col_ring() when the metric has a natural percentage / fraction shape and falls into typical “good / OK / concern” bands. The threshold-driven palette tells the story at a glance.
Use col_pictogram() when the metric is a count that benefits from visual heft (one icon per detractor, one star per appearance) — it reads faster than a bare number for small counts.
Use col_sparkline() when you need direction without precision; for axis-comparable trends across rows reach for viz_bar() or viz_forest() instead.

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
Portfolio performance
Twelve-month return with a 95% CI as the focal forest column, allocation share as a ring, position counts as a pictogram, and a Sharpe-ratio bar. Row-level accent on assets that beat their benchmark; row fill on the overall portfolio summary.


Code
portfolio <- data.frame(
  asset        = c("US Large Cap", "US Small Cap", "International Dev",
                   "Emerging Mkts", "US IG Bonds",  "TIPS",
                   "Gold",         "Real Estate",   "Cash",
                   "PORTFOLIO"),
  category     = c("Equity", "Equity", "Equity", "Equity",
                   "Fixed Income", "Fixed Income",
                   "Alternative", "Alternative", "Cash",
                   "Total"),
  allocation   = c(0.32, 0.10, 0.12, 0.06, 0.22, 0.05,
                   0.04, 0.06, 0.03, 1.00),
  positions    = c(  4,    3,    5,    3,    6,    1,
                     1,    2,    0,   25L),
  return_pct   = c(0.183, 0.122, 0.094, 0.072, 0.041, 0.025,
                   0.158, 0.108, 0.043, 0.118),
  return_lo    = c(0.142, 0.062, 0.038, 0.005, 0.020, 0.008,
                   0.090, 0.058, 0.041, 0.092),
  return_hi    = c(0.224, 0.182, 0.150, 0.139, 0.062, 0.042,
                   0.226, 0.158, 0.045, 0.144),
  sharpe       = c(1.45, 0.86, 0.72, 0.41, 1.02, 0.62,
                   0.94, 0.81, NA,   1.18),
  beats_bench  = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE,
                   FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE),
  row_type     = c(rep("data", 9), "summary"),
  is_total     = c(rep(FALSE, 9), TRUE)
)

tabviz(portfolio,
  label = "asset",
  group = "category",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("allocation", header = "Alloc",
             thresholds = c(0.05, 0.20)),     # tiny / typical / heavy
    col_pictogram("positions", header = "Positions",
                  glyph = "person",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 6),
    viz_forest(point = "return_pct", lower = "return_lo", upper = "return_hi",
               null_value = 0,
               axis_label = "12-month return (95% CI)"),
    col_percent("return_pct", "Return", multiply = TRUE),
    col_numeric("sharpe",     "Sharpe", decimals = 2),
    col_badge("beats_bench", "vs Benchmark",
      variants = list(`TRUE` = "success", `FALSE` = "muted"))
  ),
  row_type   = "row_type",
  row_accent = "beats_bench",     # bold + accent on outperformers
  row_fill   = "is_total",        # strong fill on overall portfolio row
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title    = "Portfolio performance — trailing twelve months",
  subtitle = "Allocation share, position count, return CI, and Sharpe ratio per holding category.",
  caption  = "Outperforming holdings highlighted with the accent token; overall portfolio row uses the fill token."
)

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
Product metrics
Five product variants tracked across revenue, growth, margin, units sold, and a 12-month trend:


Code
product_metrics <- data.frame(
  product = c("Widget Pro", "Widget Lite", "Widget Plus", "Widget Max", "Widget Mini"),
  revenue = c(2450000, 1890000, 3200000, 1560000, 980000),
  growth = c(0.12, -0.05, 0.28, 0.08, 0.15),
  margin = c(0.35, 0.42, 0.28, 0.38, 0.45),
  units_sold = c(12500, 9800, 18200, 7800, 15600),
  satisfaction = c(4.2, 3.8, 4.5, 4.1, 4.3),
  trend = I(list(
    c(180, 195, 210, 205, 225, 240, 235, 250, 260, 275, 290, 305),
    c(220, 215, 205, 210, 200, 195, 190, 185, 188, 182, 180, 178),
    c(200, 220, 240, 260, 290, 320, 340, 360, 380, 400, 420, 440),
    c(120, 125, 130, 128, 135, 140, 138, 145, 150, 148, 155, 160),
    c(60, 68, 75, 82, 88, 95, 100, 108, 115, 122, 128, 135)
  ))
)

tabviz(product_metrics, label = "product",
  columns = list(
    col_currency("revenue", "Revenue", decimals = 0),
    col_percent("growth", "YoY"),
    col_percent("margin", "Margin"),
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("units_sold"),
      header = "Units Sold",
      axis_range = c(0, 20000)
    ),
    col_sparkline("trend", "12M Trend"),
    col_stars("satisfaction", "Rating", max_stars = 5)
  ),
  row_color = ~ ifelse(growth < 0, "#dc2626", "#64748b"),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Product Performance Dashboard"
)

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
A/B test results
Conversion lift with a 95% CI per variant, p-value, and a verdict badge. The control row is a row_type = "summary" baseline.


Code
ab_test_results <- data.frame(
  variant = c("Control", "Variant A", "Variant B", "Variant C"),
  visitors = c(50000, 48500, 51200, 47800),
  conversions = c(2500, 2910, 2816, 2629),
  rate = c(0.050, 0.060, 0.055, 0.055),
  lift = c(0, 0.20, 0.10, 0.10),
  lower = c(NA, 0.08, -0.02, -0.03),
  upper = c(NA, 0.32, 0.22, 0.23),
  pvalue = c(NA, 0.002, 0.12, 0.15),
  verdict = c("Baseline", "Winner", "Inconclusive", "Inconclusive"),
  row_type = c("summary", "data", "data", "data"),
  row_bold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE)
)

tabviz(ab_test_results, label = "variant",
  columns = list(
    col_n("visitors", "Visitors", abbreviate = TRUE),
    col_n("conversions", "Conv"),
    col_percent("rate", "CVR"),
    viz_forest(
      point = "lift", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
      null_value = 0,
      axis_label = "Lift vs Control"
    ),
    col_percent("lift", "Lift"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE),
    col_badge("verdict", "Verdict",
      variants = list(
        Baseline = "info",
        Winner = "success",
        Inconclusive = "warning"
      ))
  ),
  row_type = "row_type",
  row_bold = "row_bold",
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(is.na(lower), "#64748b",
                          ifelse(lower > 0, "#16a34a", "#64748b")),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Homepage Redesign A/B Test"
)

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
Airline performance
Carrier delays with a heatmap on on-time percentage, a progress bar for satisfaction, and a 12-month delay trend:


Code
data(airline_delays)

airline_summary <- airline_delays |>
  filter(month == "Apr") |>
  select(carrier, carrier_type, on_time_pct, delay_vs_avg, satisfaction, flights, trend)

tabviz(airline_summary, label = "carrier",
  columns = list(
    col_badge("carrier_type", "Type",
      variants = list(Legacy = "info", `Low-Cost` = "success",
                      `Ultra Low-Cost` = "warning")),
    col_n("flights", "Flights", abbreviate = TRUE),
    col_heatmap("on_time_pct", "On-Time",
      palette = c("#fee2e2", "#dcfce7"),
      min_value = 0.65, max_value = 0.95, decimals = 2, show_value = TRUE),
    col_progress("satisfaction", "Satisfaction", max_value = 5),
    col_numeric("delay_vs_avg", "Delay vs Avg", decimals = 1),
    col_sparkline("trend", "12-Month Trend")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Airline Performance — April"
)

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
Climate — regional temperature anomalies
The same interval-estimate structure that runs clinical forest plots maps cleanly onto temperature anomalies vs. a pre-industrial baseline. Marker shape encodes confidence; marker color signals severity:


Code
data(climate_temps)

tabviz(climate_temps,
  label = "region",
  group = "category",
  columns = list(
    col_text("period", "Period"),
    col_text("certainty", "Confidence"),
    col_interval("anomaly", "lower", "upper", header = "Anomaly °C (95% CI)"),
    viz_forest(point = "anomaly", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 0, axis_label = "Temperature Anomaly (°C vs. pre-industrial)")
  ),
  marker_shape = ~ ifelse(certainty == "High", "circle", "square"),
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(anomaly > 1.5, "#dc2626", ifelse(anomaly > 1.0, "#f59e0b", "#3b82f6")),
  theme = web_theme_nejm() |>
    set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE),
  title    = "Regional Temperature Anomalies",
  subtitle = "Grouped by geographic category, shaped by confidence level",
  caption  = "Red ≥ 1.5 °C, amber 1.0–1.5 °C, blue < 1.0 °C; circle = high confidence."
)

title: Domain Dashboards
text:
See also

Long Meta-Analysis — pooled summaries + pagination across 70 studies.
Pictogram & Ring Deep Dive — every mode of col_pictogram() and col_ring().
Compose Your Own Brand — building the NBA / Lancet / journal themes from scratch.
Visualization Styling — the four-layer marker-color cascade across viz_* columns.
Reference → Columns — every col_* and viz_* argument.

title: Shiny Integration
text: Functions for embedding tabviz plots in Shiny applications and updating them in place from server code via proxies. The proxy pattern lets every fluent verb dispatch transparently — the same sort_rows() call works on a WebSpec, an htmlwidget, or a live tabviz_proxy.

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Output and Render

tabvizOutput()
Create a Shiny output element for a tabviz plot.
tabvizOutput(outputId, width = "100%", height = "400px")









Argument
Type
Default
Description




outputId
character(1)
required
Output variable name.


width
character/numeric
"100%"
Widget width (CSS units).


height
character/numeric
"400px"
Widget height (CSS units).





renderTabviz()
renderTabviz(expr, env = parent.frame(), quoted = FALSE)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




expr
expression
required
Returns a widget from tabviz().


env
environment
parent.frame()
Evaluation environment.


quoted
logical(1)
FALSE
Whether expr is already quoted.

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Proxy Updates

tabviz_proxy()
Create a proxy to update a running tabviz widget without re-rendering it. Pass the proxy to any fluent verb (sort_rows(), filter_rows(), add_column(), set_theme(), …) to dispatch an update message to the browser.
tabviz_proxy(id, session = shiny::getDefaultReactiveDomain())









Argument
Type
Default
Description




id
character(1)
required
Widget (output) id.


session
Shiny session
getDefaultReactiveDomain()
Current reactive session.



Returns a tabviz_proxy object. Always create proxies inside a reactive context (observe, observeEvent, …), never at the top of server.


Verbs that accept a proxy
All fluent verbs dispatch on input type: static WebSpec / htmlwidget objects are mutated in R; tabviz_proxy objects send a custom message to the browser. See the full list in Styling and the Fluent API guide.







Category
Verbs




Columns
add_column(), remove_column(), move_column(), resize_column(), update_column()


Rows
sort_rows(), filter_rows(), clear_filters(), select_rows(), move_row()


Data
update_data() (proxy requires a WebSpec)


Groups
toggle_group()


Cells
set_cell(), set_row_label(), clear_edits() (proxy-only)


Global
set_theme(), set_zoom(), paginate(), styling set_* verbs


Labels & watermark
set_title(), set_subtitle(), set_caption(), set_footnote(), set_watermark()


Painter
paint_row(), paint_cell() (apply a semantic token)


Split
set_shared_column_widths() (align column widths across sub-plots)

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Reading widget state
Tabviz widgets emit per-dimension Shiny inputs as users interact (sort, filter, drag, edit, paginate, …). Two helpers wrap the boilerplate of pulling them all together:

tabviz_state()
Returns a named list of every observability dimension the widget exposes. The envelope (source, ts) is stripped — values only.
tabviz_state(input, id)








Argument
Type
Description




input
Shiny input object
Passed through from server


id
character(1)
Widget output id (the same one passed to tabvizOutput())



observe({
  state <- tabviz_state(input, "tbl")
  # state$sort, state$filters, state$collapsed_groups, ...
})


tabviz_state_envelope()
Like tabviz_state() but preserves each dimension’s envelope, so callers see source ("user" / "proxy") and ts alongside the value. Use this to ignore proxy-originated mutations and avoid feedback loops:
observe({
  sort <- input[["tbl_sort"]]
  req(sort, sort$source == "user")
  # react only to user-driven sort changes
})

# Or pull all dimensions at once:
env <- tabviz_state_envelope(input, "tbl")
env$sort$value      # the value
env$sort$source     # "user" or "proxy"
env$sort$ts         # timestamp

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Split-Table Bindings

splitTabvizOutput()
splitTabvizOutput(outputId, width = "100%", height = "600px")
Same arguments as tabvizOutput() with a taller default height suited to the split-view’s sidebar layout.


renderSplitTabviz()
renderSplitTabviz(expr, env = parent.frame(), quoted = FALSE)
expr should return a widget created from a split_table() result (or a forest_plot() / tabviz() call with split_by).


split_tabviz_proxy()
split_tabviz_proxy(id, session = shiny::getDefaultReactiveDomain())
Returns a split_tabviz_proxy object.


split_tabviz_select()
Select a sub-plot in a split-table tabviz.
split_tabviz_select(proxy, key)








Argument
Type
Description




proxy
split_tabviz_proxy
From split_tabviz_proxy().


key
character(1)
Sub-plot key (e.g. "Americas" or "Male__Young").

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Examples

Basic Shiny app


Code
library(shiny)
library(tabviz)

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Forest Plot Demo"),
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      selectInput("theme", "Theme:",
        choices = c("Default", "JAMA", "Lancet", "Modern")
      )
    ),
    mainPanel(
      tabvizOutput("plot", height = "500px")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  data <- data.frame(
    study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C"),
    hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91),
    lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75),
    upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10)
  )

  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    theme <- switch(input$theme,
      "Default" = web_theme_nejm(),
      "JAMA"    = web_theme_nejm(),
      "Lancet"  = web_theme_nejm(),
      "Modern"  = web_theme_nejm()
    )

    tabviz(data,
      label = "study",
      columns = list(
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1)
      ),
      theme = theme
    )
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)




Proxy updates (no re-render)


Code
library(shiny)
library(tabviz)

ui <- fluidPage(
  tabvizOutput("plot"),
  actionButton("filter_btn", "Filter HR < 1"),
  actionButton("clear_btn",  "Clear Filter"),
  actionButton("sort_btn",   "Sort HR desc")
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  data <- data.frame(
    study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D"),
    hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 1.15, 0.91),
    lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.90, 0.75),
    upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.45, 1.10)
  )

  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    tabviz(data,
      label = "study",
      columns = list(
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1)
      )
    )
  })

  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")

  observeEvent(input$filter_btn, filter_rows(proxy, "hr", "lt", 1))
  observeEvent(input$clear_btn,  clear_filters(proxy))
  observeEvent(input$sort_btn,   sort_rows(proxy, "hr", "desc"))
}




Replacing the dataset
update_data() on a proxy requires a WebSpec (not a raw data frame) so the new columns/types can be serialized. Build one with tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE).


Code
observeEvent(input$switch_dataset, {
  new_spec <- tabviz(
    get_dataset(input$dataset_name),
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    ),
    .spec_only = TRUE
  )
  update_data(tabviz_proxy("plot"), new_spec)
})

title: Shiny Integration
text:
See Also

Shiny guide — reactive patterns and a complete example.
Fluent API guide — full verb list and static/proxy dispatch.
tabviz() — main entry point.

title: forest_plot()
text: forest_plot() is a convenience wrapper around tabviz() that auto-builds a forest column from point / lower / upper (or an effects list) and forwards everything else to tabviz(). Use it for quick forest plots; reach for tabviz() directly when you want full control over column ordering, multiple viz columns, or column groups.

title: forest_plot()
text:
Signature
forest_plot(
  x,
  point = NULL, lower = NULL, upper = NULL,
  effects = NULL,
  label = NULL,
  group = NULL,
  columns = NULL,
  scale = NULL,            # "linear" or "log"
  null_value = NULL,       # 0 (linear) or 1 (log) by default
  axis_label = NULL,
  axis_range = NULL,
  axis_ticks = NULL,
  axis_gridlines = NULL,
  theme = NULL,
  ...,                     # forwarded to tabviz()
  split_by = NULL,
  shared_axis = FALSE,
  width = NULL, height = NULL, elementId = NULL
)

title: forest_plot()
text:
Arguments



Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
data.frame / WebSpec / SplitForest
required
Input. A data frame builds a fresh widget; a WebSpec is rendered directly; a SplitForest is rendered as a split widget.


point
character(1)
NULL
Column name for point estimates. Required when x is a data frame and effects is not provided.


lower
character(1)
NULL
Lower-bound column. Required with point / upper.


upper
character(1)
NULL
Upper-bound column.


effects
list of effect_forest()
NULL
Multi-effect spec; when provided, point / lower / upper are ignored.


label
character(1)
NULL
Column name for row labels.


group
character / list
NULL
Same shape as tabviz(group=).


columns
list
NULL
Extra col_*() / viz_*() columns added alongside the auto-built forest column.


scale
character(1)
"linear"
"linear" or "log".


null_value
numeric(1)
0 (linear) / 1 (log)
Reference value for the null line.


axis_label
character(1)
"Effect"
X-axis label.


axis_range
numeric(2)
NULL
Forwarded to the auto-built viz_forest().


axis_ticks
numeric
NULL
Forwarded to the auto-built viz_forest().


axis_gridlines
logical(1)
FALSE
Forwarded to the auto-built viz_forest().


theme
WebTheme
NULL
Theme; NULL falls through to tabviz()’s default.


...
—
—
Forwarded to tabviz(). All tabviz() row/marker styling, titles, and interactivity arguments are accepted.


split_by
character
NULL
Splits the result into a SplitForest.


shared_axis
logical(1)
FALSE
Share axis range across split sub-plots.


width, height, elementId
—
NULL
Standard htmlwidget passthroughs.









Noteaxis_range / axis_ticks / axis_gridlines are native here



In tabviz(), these arguments were deprecated in 0.9.0 and now live on viz_forest(). forest_plot() keeps them as native arguments and forwards them into the auto-built forest column — that’s the whole point of the wrapper.








TipPagination flows through



Pass paginate = paginate_letter() (or any other pagination shortcut) to break a long table across multiple PDF pages — forest_plot() forwards paginate to tabviz() via .... See the Export → Pagination guide for the full surface.

title: forest_plot()
text:
Return value
An htmlwidget. When split_by is set or x is a SplitForest, returns the split-table widget.

title: forest_plot()
text:
Examples


Code
data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D"),
  hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91, 0.68),
  lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75, 0.52),
  upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10, 0.89)
)

# Quick forest from a data frame
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study")








Code
# Log scale for ratios (HR / OR / RR)
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study",
            scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Hazard Ratio")








Code
# Add data columns alongside the auto-built forest column
data$n <- c(1250, 980, 1560, 1100)

forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
            label = "study",
            scale = "log", null_value = 1,
            columns = list(
              col_n("n"),
              col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
            ))








Code
# Multi-effect plot (e.g. ITT vs Per-Protocol)
multi <- data.frame(
  study  = c("TRIAL-1", "TRIAL-2", "TRIAL-3"),
  itt_hr = c(0.78, 0.82, 0.74),
  itt_lo = c(0.65, 0.68, 0.61),
  itt_hi = c(0.94, 0.99, 0.90),
  pp_hr  = c(0.72, 0.78, 0.70),
  pp_lo  = c(0.58, 0.63, 0.56),
  pp_hi  = c(0.89, 0.97, 0.88)
)

forest_plot(multi,
  label = "study",
  effects = list(
    effect_forest("itt_hr", "itt_lo", "itt_hi", label = "ITT",          color = "#2563eb"),
    effect_forest("pp_hr",  "pp_lo",  "pp_hi",  label = "Per-Protocol", color = "#16a34a")
  ),
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
)








Code
# Split view with shared axis
regional <- data.frame(
  study  = rep(c("Study 1", "Study 2", "Study 3"), 2),
  region = rep(c("North America", "Europe"), each = 3),
  hr     = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.78, 0.68, 0.82, 0.75),
  lo     = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.62, 0.52, 0.68, 0.58),
  hi     = c(0.95, 1.03, 0.98, 0.89, 0.99, 0.97)
)

forest_plot(regional, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
            label = "study",
            scale = "log", null_value = 1,
            split_by = "region", shared_axis = TRUE)

title: forest_plot()
text:
Equivalence with tabviz()
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
            label = "study", scale = "log", null_value = 1)

# Equivalent to:
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

title: forest_plot()
text:
Related

tabviz() — full control over columns and layout
viz_forest() — the underlying forest column helper
effect_forest() — define one effect for multi-effect plots
split_table() — build a SplitForest from a spec or widget
save_plot() — export to SVG/PNG/PDF

title: split_table()
text:
split_table() slices a spec or widget into a SplitForest — one sub-plot per unique value (or combination of values) of the splitting column(s). The resulting object renders with sidebar navigation in the browser, can be exported one file per sub-plot via save_split_table(), and supports a shared-axis option for cross-subgroup comparison.
The same effect is available inline on tabviz() and forest_plot() via the split_by / shared_axis arguments — split_table() is the explicit constructor used when you want a SplitForest object to inspect, modify, or hand to save_split_table().

title: split_table()
text:
Signature
split_table(x, by, shared_axis = FALSE, shared_column_widths = FALSE, ...)

title: split_table()
text:
Arguments









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget / data.frame
required
Source. A data frame is fed through web_spec() first.


by
character
required
Column name(s) to split on. A single string creates one plot per unique value; a vector creates a hierarchical navigation tree (outermost first).


shared_axis
logical(1)
FALSE
When TRUE, all sub-plots use the same x-axis range so subgroup effects can be compared at a glance.


shared_column_widths
logical(1)
FALSE
When TRUE, stamp identical per-column widths across every sub-plot so columns line up tile-to-tile when screenshots are stacked in slides / PowerPoint. Widths are computed once from the combined data. Added in v0.16.


...
—
—
Forwarded to web_spec() when x is a data frame.

title: split_table()
text:
Return value
A SplitForest containing one WebSpec per split combination, the navigation tree, the split variables, and (when shared_axis = TRUE) the shared axis range.

title: split_table()
text:
tabviz() / forest_plot() shortcut
Both top-level entry points accept split_by and shared_axis directly. They build a WebSpec first, call split_table() internally, then render the result.
# Equivalent
tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")),
       split_by = "region", shared_axis = TRUE)

tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")),
       .spec_only = TRUE) |>
  split_table(by = "region", shared_axis = TRUE) |>
  forest_plot()

title: split_table()
text:
Pagination cascades through splits
Set paginate = ... on the underlying tabviz() call (or pipe paginate() after split_table()) and the spec is propagated into every subview. Each subview paginates independently with its own row-count budget; PDF export emits one combined multi-page PDF per split leaf:
sf <- tabviz(data, label = "study",
             columns = list(viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper")),
             paginate = paginate_letter(),
             .spec_only = TRUE) |>
  split_table(by = "region")

save_plot(sf, "out/region.pdf")  # writes out/Americas.pdf, out/Europe.pdf, ...

title: split_table()
text:
Single-subview export with save_plot(which = ...)
save_plot() accepts a SplitForest directly. Without which =, it dispatches to save_split_table() and writes one file per leaf into a directory. With which =, it extracts a single subview by key (e.g. "Male__Young") or 1-based integer index and writes to a single file:
save_plot(sf, "out/europe.svg", which = "Europe")   # named subview
save_plot(sf, "out/first.svg",  which = 1L)         # by index

title: split_table()
text:
Examples


Code
set.seed(42)
data <- data.frame(
  study  = paste0("Study ", 1:30),
  region = sample(c("Americas", "Europe", "Asia"), 30, replace = TRUE),
  or     = exp(rnorm(30, log(0.8), 0.3))
)
data$lower <- data$or * exp(-1.96 * 0.25)
data$upper <- data$or * exp( 1.96 * 0.25)

forest_plot(data,
  point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study",
  split_by = "region",
  scale = "log", null_value = 1,
  theme = web_theme_nejm()
)








Code
# Hierarchical split with nested sidebar
forest_plot(data,
  point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study",
  split_by = c("sex", "age_group"),
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
)




Code
# Build the SplitForest explicitly to export
sf <- tabviz(data, label = "study",
             columns = list(viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper")),
             .spec_only = TRUE) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group"), shared_axis = TRUE)

sf                              # in a knitr chunk, renders as the live widget
save_split_table(sf, "out/")    # one file per sub-plot








NoteSplitForest renders as a widget in knitr chunks



A SplitForest carries a registered knit_print method (since this audit cycle), so a chunk whose last value is sf renders as the interactive split widget rather than dumping the S7 object’s internal structure as raw text. At an interactive prompt outside knitr, print(sf) still prints a one-line summary.

title: split_table()
text:
Related

tabviz() and forest_plot() — both accept split_by directly
save_split_table() — export every sub-plot to a directory tree
Shiny bindings — splitTabvizOutput(), renderSplitTabviz(), split_tabviz_proxy()

title: Interaction
text: web_interaction() builds an InteractionSpec controlling what users can do with a tabviz widget — sorting, filtering, reordering, editing, exporting, theme switching. Three presets cover common cases (_full, _minimal, _publication).

title: Interaction
text:
web_interaction()
web_interaction(
  show_legend = TRUE,
  enable_sort = TRUE,
  enable_collapse = TRUE,
  enable_select = TRUE,
  enable_hover = TRUE,
  enable_resize = TRUE,
  enable_export = TRUE,
  enable_filters = TRUE,
  enable_reorder_rows = TRUE,
  enable_reorder_columns = TRUE,
  enable_edit = TRUE,
  show_group_counts = FALSE,
  tooltip_fields = NULL,
  enable_themes = getOption("tabviz.enable_themes", "default")
)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




show_legend
logical(1)
TRUE
Render the legend (multi-effect plots).


enable_sort
logical(1)
TRUE
Click headers to sort.


enable_collapse
logical(1)
TRUE
Click row-group headers to collapse / expand.


enable_select
logical(1)
TRUE
Click rows to select.


enable_hover
logical(1)
TRUE
Highlight rows on hover.


enable_resize
logical(1)
TRUE
Drag column borders to resize.


enable_export
logical(1)
TRUE
Show download / export button on hover.


enable_filters
logical(1)
TRUE
Per-column filter popovers in headers.


enable_reorder_rows
logical(1)
TRUE
Drag rows within a group, and row-groups among siblings.


enable_reorder_columns
logical(1)
TRUE
Drag columns within a group, and column-groups among top-level siblings.


enable_edit
logical(1)
TRUE
Double-click cells to edit; popover editor for forest-cell numerics. Also enables inline editing of title / subtitle / caption / footnote (v0.15+).


show_group_counts
logical(1)
FALSE
Append (n) after each row-group label.


tooltip_fields
character
NULL
Column names shown in the hover tooltip. NULL disables tooltips.


enable_theme_edit
logical(1)
TRUE
Settings cog + panel. FALSE = author freeze — viewers can’t restyle a published figure live; the theme switcher stays governed by enable_themes.


enable_themes
"default" / NULL / list of WebTheme
getOption("tabviz.enable_themes", "default")
Curate the theme switcher (see below).



Returns: an InteractionSpec.






Importantshow_filters was renamed in 0.9.0



The argument is now enable_filters — the column-filter UI is a user capability, not a static visibility toggle. The old show_filters is still accepted for one cycle and emits lifecycle::deprecate_warn().



enable_themes
Curates the in-widget theme switcher. Four shapes are accepted; the runtime detects which one you passed and renders the switcher accordingly:







Value
Effect




"default"
Show every theme returned by package_themes() (a tabbed picker — Journals + LOTR — since package_themes() is itself 2-level).


NULL
Hide the switcher entirely.


list(WebTheme, ...) (flat)
Show exactly those themes as a single column of choices. Named entries override the displayed name (e.g. list(Classical = web_theme_nejm())). The spec’s active theme is auto-included so users can always revert.


list(<category> = list(WebTheme, ...), ...) (2-level)
A categorized switcher: each top-level key becomes a tab, each second-level entry is a choice within that tab. If the active theme isn’t in any category, a Current tab is prepended automatically.



# Flat — two-theme curated menu, custom display names
web_interaction(enable_themes = list(
  Classical = web_theme_nejm(),
  Modern    = web_theme_nejm()
))

# Categorized — three tabs, themes nested under each
web_interaction(enable_themes = list(
  Editorial = list(
    Cochrane = web_theme_nejm(),
    Lancet   = web_theme_nejm(),
    JAMA     = web_theme_nejm()
  ),
  LOTR = list(
    Dwarven = web_theme_dwarven(),
    Elvish  = web_theme_dwarven(),
    Hobbit  = web_theme_dwarven()
  ),
  Other = list(Dark = web_theme_aurora())
))
The fluent equivalent on a built spec is selectable_themes(), which accepts the same four shapes.

title: Interaction
text:
web_interaction_full()
All capabilities on. This is what default_interaction_for_theme() returns, and what tabviz() falls back to when interaction = NULL.
web_interaction_full()

title: Interaction
text:
web_interaction_minimal()
Read-only with hover highlighting only.
web_interaction_minimal()
# Equivalent to web_interaction() with every enable_* set to FALSE
# except enable_hover = TRUE; show_legend kept TRUE.

title: Interaction
text:
web_interaction_publication()
Print-clean: nothing is interactive, no legend chrome.
web_interaction_publication()
# Every show_* and enable_* set to FALSE.
Use when:

Exporting screenshots for print publications.
Embedding in static documents where any UI noise is unwelcome.

title: Interaction
text:
default_interaction_for_theme()
default_interaction_for_theme(theme)
Returns web_interaction_full() regardless of theme — every shipped theme defaults to full interactivity. The function is exported for use in custom widget builders. Pass interaction = web_interaction_publication() (or _minimal()) explicitly for a quieter widget.

title: Interaction
text:
Examples


Code
data(glp1_trials)

# Hover-only preset
glp1_trials |>
  dplyr::filter(row_type == "data") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(label = "study",
    interaction = web_interaction_minimal(),
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    ))








Code
# Hover tooltips with selected fields
glp1_trials |>
  dplyr::filter(row_type == "data") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(label = "study",
    interaction = web_interaction(tooltip_fields = c("drug", "n", "events", "pvalue")),
    footnote = "Hover over markers to see details",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ))






# Disable just one capability
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  interaction = web_interaction(enable_sort = FALSE),
  columns = list(...))

# Curated theme menu via the constructor
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  interaction = web_interaction(enable_themes = list(
    web_theme_nejm(), web_theme_nejm()
  )),
  columns = list(...))

title: Interaction
text:
Related

selectable_themes() — fluent equivalent for the enable_themes argument
set_theme() — change the active theme
Themes — package_themes() and the web_theme_*() builders
tabviz() — the interaction argument

title: Annotations
text: Annotations decorate a viz_forest() column with reference lines and custom shapes. They are passed via the annotations = list(...) argument of viz_forest() (and the equivalent shorthand in forest_plot()).

title: Annotations
text:
refline()
Vertical reference line at a specific x-axis position. Most often used to mark the null effect (x = 0 linear, x = 1 log) or a clinically meaningful threshold.
refline(x,
        label = NULL,
        style = c("dashed", "solid", "dotted"),
        color = NULL,
        width = 1,
        opacity = 0.6)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
numeric(1)
required
X-axis position.


label
character(1)
NULL
Text rendered near the line.


style
choice
"dashed"
"dashed", "solid", or "dotted".


color
character(1)
NULL
CSS color. NULL uses the theme default.


width
numeric(1)
1
Line width in px.


opacity
numeric(1)
0.6
0–1.



Returns: a ReferenceLine object.


Code
data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C"),
  hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 1.15),
  lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.90),
  upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.45)
)

tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               annotations = list(
                 refline(1,    label = "No effect",            style = "solid"),
                 refline(0.75, label = "Clinically meaningful", style = "dashed")
               ))
  )
)

title: Annotations
text:
forest_annotation()
Custom shape attached to a specific study row.
forest_annotation(
  study_id,
  shape = c("circle", "square", "triangle", "star"),
  position = c("after", "before", "overlay"),
  color = "#8b5cf6",
  size = 1.0
)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




study_id
character(1)
required
Identifier of the study to annotate.


shape
choice
"circle"
One of "circle", "square", "triangle", "star".


position
choice
"after"
Position relative to the point: "before", "after", or "overlay".


color
character(1)
"#8b5cf6"
CSS color.


size
numeric(1)
1.0
Size multiplier.



Returns: a CustomAnnotation object.






WarningFrontend rendering not implemented



forest_annotation() is currently a placeholder — the spec is built but the frontend has no renderer for it yet. Use refline() for visual annotations until this lands.

title: Annotations
text:
Related

viz_forest() — the annotations argument
forest_plot() — quick wrapper that forwards an annotations list

title: tabviz()
text: tabviz() is the main entry point for building interactive table visualizations. It returns an htmlwidget that renders in R Markdown, Quarto, Shiny, or the RStudio Viewer. Use tabviz() whenever you want full control over which columns appear (text, numeric, badges, forest plots, sparklines, etc.). For a quick forest-only plot driven by point / lower / upper arguments, see forest_plot().

title: tabviz()
text:
Signature
tabviz(
  data,
  label = NULL,
  label_header = "Study",
  group = NULL,
  columns = NULL,
  extra_columns = NULL,
  available_exclude = NULL,
  title = NULL, subtitle = NULL, caption = NULL, footnote = NULL,
  # Row styling (column name or formula)
  row_bold = NULL, row_italic = NULL,
  row_color = NULL, row_bg = NULL,
  row_badge = NULL, row_icon = NULL,
  row_indent = NULL, row_type = NULL,
  row_emphasis = NULL, row_muted = NULL, row_accent = NULL,
  # Marker styling (column name or formula)
  marker_color = NULL, marker_shape = NULL,
  marker_opacity = NULL, marker_size = NULL,
  # Theme & interaction
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  interaction = NULL,
  plot_position = NULL,
  row_height = NULL,
  banding = NULL,
  width = NULL, height = NULL, elementId = NULL,
  # Splitting
  split_by = NULL, shared_axis = FALSE, shared_column_widths = FALSE,
  # Pagination
  paginate = NULL,
  .spec_only = FALSE
)

title: tabviz()
text:
Arguments

Data



Argument
Type
Default
Description




data
data.frame
required
A data.frame, data.table, or tibble.


label
character(1)
NULL
Column name for row labels. When NULL, row numbers are used.


label_header
character(1)
"Study"
Header text for the label column. The literal default "Study" is auto-prettified from the label field name when applicable.


group
character / list
NULL
Grouping spec. A single column name (flat), a vector (hierarchical), or a list of web_group() objects. See Grouping.


columns
list
NULL
List of column specs from col_*() / viz_*() helpers.


extra_columns
list
NULL
Pre-configured columns hidden by default but available in the interactive column picker.


available_exclude
character
NULL
Data columns to omit from the column picker (e.g. internal/sensitive fields).





Titles









Argument
Type
Default
Description




title
character(1)
NULL
Main title (above the plot).


subtitle
character(1)
NULL
Subtitle (below title).


caption
character(1)
NULL
Caption (below plot).


footnote
character(1)
NULL
Footnote (below caption, italicized).





Row styling
All row-styling arguments accept either a column name (string) that holds the per-row value, or a formula evaluated against data (e.g. ~ pvalue < 0.05).









Argument
Value type
Default
Description




row_bold
logical
NULL
Bold whole row.


row_italic
logical
NULL
Italicize row.


row_color
CSS color
NULL
Row text color.


row_bg
CSS color
NULL
Row background color.


row_badge
character
NULL
Badge text on the label cell.


row_icon
emoji/unicode
NULL
Icon on the label cell.


row_indent
numeric
NULL
Indentation level.


row_type
character
NULL
One of "data", "header", "summary", "spacer".


row_emphasis
logical
NULL
Bold + theme primary color.


row_muted
logical
NULL
Lighter, reduced prominence.


row_accent
logical
NULL
Bold + theme accent color (most common).


row_fill
logical
NULL
Bold + a strong row fill (auto-contrasting fg).





Marker styling
Same column-name-or-formula pattern as row styling:









Argument
Value type
Default
Description




marker_color
CSS color
NULL
Marker fill color.


marker_shape
character
NULL
One of "square", "circle", "diamond", "triangle".


marker_opacity
numeric [0,1]
NULL
Marker opacity.


marker_size
numeric
NULL
Driver for marker size — pass raw weights (e.g. study weights 0–100). The renderer normalizes via 0.5 + sqrt(w/100) * 1.5.





Theme, interaction, layout









Argument
Type
Default
Description




theme
WebTheme
web_theme_nejm()
Theme object (use a web_theme_*() builder).


interaction
InteractionSpec
NULL
Use web_interaction(). When NULL, default_interaction_for_theme(theme) is used (full interactivity).


plot_position
character(1)
NULL
 No effect. Column order in columns = list(...) controls placement.


row_height
numeric(1)
NULL
Row height in pixels.


banding
character(1)
NULL
Row banding mode: "none", "row", "group" (deepest level, default), or "group-n" (e.g. "group-1" for outermost). Overrides the banding on theme. Use the viewer’s settings panel (gear icon) to adjust interactively.


width
character/numeric
NULL
Widget width (auto).


height
character/numeric
NULL
Widget height (auto).


elementId
character(1)
NULL
HTML element id.





Splitting









Argument
Type
Default
Description




split_by
character
NULL
Column name(s) to split into one plot per unique value/combination, returning a SplitForest widget.


shared_axis
logical(1)
FALSE
When split_by is set, share an axis range across all sub-plots.


shared_column_widths
logical(1)
FALSE
When split_by is set, stamp identical per-column widths across every sub-plot so columns line up when screenshots are stacked. Added in v0.16.





Pagination



Argument
Type
Default
Description




paginate
PaginateSpec / shortcut
NULL
Break a long table across multiple pages. Accepts NULL (off), TRUE (default paginate_spec()), a positive integer (rows-per-page shorthand), or a PaginateSpec from paginate_spec() / paginate_letter() / paginate_a4() / paginate_slide(). Cascades into every split_by subview. See Export → Pagination.





Other









Argument
Type
Default
Description




.spec_only
logical(1)
FALSE
When TRUE, returns the WebSpec object instead of an htmlwidget — useful for programmatic manipulation before rendering.

title: tabviz()
text:
Deprecated arguments






ImportantRemoved from the tabviz() surface in 0.9.0



The arguments below are still accepted for one cycle but emit lifecycle::deprecate_warn(). They have moved to dedicated, more discoverable homes.





Old argument
Replacement




tabviz(axis_range = ...)
Pass axis_range to viz_forest() on the forest column.


tabviz(axis_ticks = ...)
Pass axis_ticks to viz_forest() on the forest column.


tabviz(axis_gridlines = ...)
Pass axis_gridlines to viz_forest() on the forest column.


tabviz(zoom = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(zoom = ...).


tabviz(auto_fit = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(auto_fit = ...).


tabviz(max_width = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(max_width = ...).


tabviz(max_height = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(max_height = ...).


tabviz(show_zoom_controls = ...)
Pipe to set_zoom(show_controls = ...).


tabviz(weight = ...)
Use marker_size = ....

title: tabviz()
text:
Return value
An htmlwidget. When .spec_only = TRUE, returns a WebSpec instead. When split_by is set, returns a split-table widget backed by a SplitForest.

title: tabviz()
text:
Grouping
Three modes of group:
# Flat grouping
tabviz(data, group = "category", ...)

# Hierarchical grouping
tabviz(data, group = c("region", "country"), ...)

# Explicit group definitions
tabviz(data,
  group = list(
    web_group("cardio", label = "Cardiovascular"),
    web_group("renal",  label = "Renal Outcomes", collapsed = TRUE)
  ),
  ...)

title: tabviz()
text:
Examples


Code
meta <- data.frame(
  study = c("Smith 2020", "Jones 2021", "Lee 2022"),
  drug  = c("A", "A", "B"),
  hr    = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91),
  lo    = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75),
  hi    = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10),
  n     = c(245, 180, 320)
)

# Minimal: forest column + a couple of data columns
tabviz(meta,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Hazard Ratio"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  )
)








Code
# Row styling: bold significant rows, color the favourable ones green
tabviz(meta,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  row_bold  = ~ hi < 1,
  row_color = ~ ifelse(hi < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b")
)








Code
# Spec-only: build, mutate fluently, then render
tabviz(meta,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")),
  .spec_only = TRUE
) |>
  set_theme("nejm") |>
  set_marker_style(color = "drug") |>
  forest_plot()

title: tabviz()
text:
Related

forest_plot() — convenience wrapper around tabviz() + viz_forest()
Columns — every col_*() and viz_*() helper
Styling modifiers — set_row_style(), set_marker_style(), set_zoom()
Themes — web_theme_*() builders and set_*() modifiers
Interaction — web_interaction()
Export — save_plot() for SVG / PNG / PDF, plus pagination for long tables

title: Troubleshooting
text: This guide covers common issues and their solutions when using tabviz.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Quick Diagnostic Checklist
When something isn’t working, check:

Column names — Are they spelled correctly and exist in the data?
Data types — Are numeric columns actually numeric?
NAs — Are there unexpected missing values?
Scale — Using scale = "log" with non-positive values?
Dependencies — Is V8 installed (always required) and rsvg (for PNG/PDF)?
Deprecated arguments — tabviz(axis_range / axis_ticks / axis_gridlines / zoom / auto_fit / weight) were deprecated in 0.9.0; pass them to viz_forest() (axis_*) or pipe through set_zoom() instead. forest_plot() keeps the axis args native.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Data Issues

“Column not found in data”
Symptom: Error message about missing column.
Cause: Column name typo or the column doesn’t exist.
Solution:


Code
# Check what columns exist
names(your_data)

# Common mistake: using wrong case
forest_plot(data, point = "HR", ...)  # Wrong: "HR"
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", ...)  # Correct: "hr"




Unexpected NAs in Plot
Symptom: Rows appear without intervals or with missing values.
Cause: Columns contain NA values or wrong data types.
Solution:


Code
# Check for NAs
summary(your_data)

# Check column types - character columns won't work for numeric fields
sapply(your_data, class)

# Fix character columns that should be numeric
your_data$hr <- as.numeric(your_data$hr)




Groups Not Displaying Correctly
Symptom: Hierarchical groups appear flat or have wrong nesting.
Cause: Group column contains unexpected values or ID collisions.
Solution:


Code
# Check unique group values
table(your_data$region)
table(your_data$subregion)

# Ensure nested groups have consistent parent values
your_data |>
  group_by(region, subregion) |>
  count()




Data Rows Mixed with Headers
Symptom: Header rows show intervals, or data rows appear as headers.
Cause: row_type column has incorrect values.
Solution:


Code
# Valid row_type values
valid_types <- c("header", "data", "summary", "spacer")

# Check your values
your_data |>
  count(row_type)

# Ensure header/spacer rows have NA for estimates
your_data <- your_data |>
  mutate(
    hr = if_else(row_type %in% c("header", "spacer"), NA_real_, hr),
    lower = if_else(row_type %in% c("header", "spacer"), NA_real_, lower),
    upper = if_else(row_type %in% c("header", "spacer"), NA_real_, upper)
  )

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Scale and Axis Issues

Log Scale with Non-Positive Values
Symptom: Error or unexpected axis range when using scale = "log".
Cause: Data contains zero or negative values, which are undefined on log scale.
Solution:


Code
# Find problematic rows
your_data |>
  filter(hr <= 0 | lower <= 0 | upper <= 0)

# Option 1: Filter out invalid rows
valid_data <- your_data |>
  filter(hr > 0, lower > 0, upper > 0)

# Option 2: Use linear scale instead
forest_plot(your_data, ..., scale = "linear", null_value = 0)




Axis Range Too Wide
Symptom: Data points are compressed into a small portion of the axis.
Cause: Outliers or automatic axis calculation including extreme values.
Solution:


Code
# Check data range
range(your_data$hr, na.rm = TRUE)
range(your_data$lower, na.rm = TRUE)
range(your_data$upper, na.rm = TRUE)

# Option 1: Override axis range
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  axis_range = c(0.5, 2.0)  # Focus on relevant range
)

# Option 2: Filter outliers
filtered <- your_data |>
  filter(between(hr, 0.3, 3))




Axis Range Too Narrow
Symptom: Confidence intervals extend beyond axis bounds.
Cause: Manual axis range doesn’t accommodate all data.
Solution:


Code
# Find the actual data extent
min_val <- min(your_data$lower, na.rm = TRUE)
max_val <- max(your_data$upper, na.rm = TRUE)
message("Data range: ", round(min_val, 2), " to ", round(max_val, 2))

# Adjust axis_range to include all data
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  axis_range = c(min_val * 0.9, max_val * 1.1)
)




Tick Marks Not Appearing
Symptom: Axis shows but has no tick marks or unexpected tick positions.
Solution:


Code
# Specify explicit tick positions
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  scale = "log",
  axis_ticks = c(0.5, 0.7, 1, 1.5, 2)  # Explicit ticks
)

# Or use theme settings
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  theme = web_theme_nejm() |>
    set_theme_field(c("axis", "tick_count"), 5) |>
    set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE)
)








NoteOn tabviz() (vs forest_plot())



tabviz() no longer takes axis_range, axis_ticks, axis_gridlines (deprecated in 0.9.0). Pass them to viz_forest() on the forest column instead:
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_range = c(0.5, 2.0),
               axis_ticks = c(0.5, 0.7, 1, 1.5, 2))
  )
)
forest_plot() keeps these as native top-level arguments and forwards them to the auto-built forest column.

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Export Failures

V8 Not Installed
Symptom: Error when calling save_plot(): “Package V8 is required”
Solution:


Code
# Install V8
install.packages("V8")

# On some systems, you may need system libraries first:
# macOS: brew install v8
# Ubuntu: sudo apt-get install libv8-dev




rsvg Issues
Symptom: PDF/PNG export fails with rsvg errors.
Solution:


Code
# Install rsvg
install.packages("rsvg")

# On macOS, you may need:
# brew install librsvg

# On Ubuntu:
# sudo apt-get install librsvg2-dev




“SVG generator JavaScript file not found”
Symptom: Export fails with message about missing JS file.
Cause: Package installation is incomplete.
Solution:


Code
# Reinstall the package
pak::pak("kaskarn/tabviz", force = TRUE)

# Verify installation
system.file("js/svg-generator.js", package = "tabviz")
# Should return a path, not ""




Colors Differ Between Display and Export
Symptom: Exported SVG/PNG has different colors than the interactive widget.
Cause: Theme not applied consistently to both.
Solution:


Code
# Use the same theme for display and export
my_theme <- web_theme_nejm()

# Interactive
forest_plot(data, ..., theme = my_theme)

# Export
spec <- web_spec(data, ..., theme = my_theme)
save_plot(spec, "output.svg")

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Widget Rendering Issues

Blank Output in RStudio Viewer
Symptom: Plot doesn’t appear in RStudio’s Viewer pane.
Cause: Viewer pane size or rendering issues.
Solution:


Code
# Try in browser instead
plot <- forest_plot(your_data, ...)
htmltools::browsable(plot)

# Or save to file and open
htmlwidgets::saveWidget(plot, "temp.html")
browseURL("temp.html")




Widget Not Sizing Correctly
Symptom: Plot is too small, cut off, or doesn’t fill container.
Solution:


Code
# Specify explicit dimensions
forest_plot(your_data, ...,
  width = 900,
  height = 600
)

# For Shiny, use CSS units
tabvizOutput("plot", width = "100%", height = "600px")

# Constrain max size via set_zoom() (replaces deprecated tabviz(max_width=)/(max_height=))
tabviz(your_data, ...) |>
  set_zoom(max_width = 1000, auto_fit = TRUE)




Shiny: Proxy Not Working
Symptom: tabviz_proxy() calls don’t update the plot.
Cause: Proxy created outside reactive context.
Solution:


Code
# Wrong: proxy at top level
# proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")  # This will fail!

# Correct: proxy inside reactive context
observeEvent(input$button, {
  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")
  sort_rows(proxy, "hr", "asc")
})

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Theme and Styling Issues

Styles Not Applying
Symptom: Bold, color, or other styles don’t appear.
Cause: Passing values directly instead of column names.
Solution:


Code
# Wrong: passing values directly
col_numeric("hr", bold = TRUE, color = "#ff0000")

# Correct: passing column names that contain the values
col_numeric("hr", bold = "is_bold", color = "hr_color")

# The data must have these columns:
data <- data |>
  mutate(
    is_bold = pval < 0.05,
    hr_color = if_else(hr < 1, "#16a34a", "#dc2626")
  )




Font Not Loading
Symptom: Text appears in wrong font.
Cause: Custom font not available on system.
Solution:


Code
# Use web-safe fallback fonts
web_theme_nejm() |>
  set_inputs(fonts_body = "Georgia, 'Times New Roman', serif")

# For exports, fonts must be installed on the rendering system

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Debug Patterns

Inspecting a WebSpec


Code
# Build a spec without rendering (use .spec_only = TRUE on tabviz())
spec <- tabviz(
  data.frame(
    study = c("A", "B"),
    hr = c(0.8, 1.2),
    n = c(100, 200)
  ),
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("hr", "HR"),
    col_n("n")
  ),
  .spec_only = TRUE
)

# Print summary
print(spec)

Minimal Reproducible Example

Code
# Minimal data that reproduces the problem
test_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Test 1", "Test 2"),
  hr = c(0.8, 1.2),
  lower = c(0.6, 0.9),
  upper = c(1.1, 1.6)
)

# Minimal code
forest_plot(test_data,
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
  label = "study"
)

# Include session info
sessionInfo()

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Pagination

“Why didn’t my paginate spec page my PNG?”
save_plot(<paginated spec>, "out.png") warns and flattens by default — single-image formats can’t represent multiple pages. To silence the warning when you want a flattened image:
save_plot(p, "out.png", paginate = NULL)  # explicit "flatten this render"
Or save to PDF for true multi-page output:
save_plot(p, "out.pdf")  # multi-page PDF via qpdf::pdf_combine


Multi-page PDF wrote a numbered series instead of one file
save_plot(*.pdf, paginate = ...) merges per-page PDFs via qpdf::pdf_combine(). When the qpdf package isn’t installed, it falls back to writing out_p01.pdf, out_p02.pdf, … and emits a warning. Install qpdf to merge into one file:
install.packages("qpdf")
qpdf is in Suggests (lightweight C++ binding, ~3 MB).


“My SplitForest paginate didn’t cascade”
Pagination cascades into every subview when set on the underlying tabviz() call. If you constructed the spec without paginate, then piped through split_table(), the subviews carry no pagination. Either set paginate on the original tabviz() call (recommended), or pipe paginate() after split:
sf <- tabviz(data, ..., paginate = paginate_letter(),
             split_by = "region")

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Widget rendering in knitr

“My split_table widget rendered as a wall of @ specs : List of 6 text”
Fixed in this audit cycle. SplitForest (the object returned by split_table() and by tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE) |> split_table(...)) now carries a registered knit_print method, so a chunk whose last value is one renders as the live interactive widget. Same for WebSpec from tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE).
If you still see the structure dump in your own project, you’re likely on an old install. Reinstall:
remotes::install_github("kaskarn/tabviz")

title: Troubleshooting
text:
Getting Help
If you’ve tried the above and still have issues:

Check GitHub Issues for similar problems
Create a minimal reproducible example
Include sessionInfo() output
Open a new issue with your example and session info

title: Schema System
text:
Every column type in tabviz is described by a schema — a single declarative object that owns its options, its inheritance chain, its renderers (one per runtime target), its behaviors (sort key, formatter, aggregate, …), and its contributions to widget-wide state (axes, footnotes, conditions). The schema is the single source of truth: the editor, the renderer, the source codegen, the R-side defaults all read from the same description.
This page covers the design principles. For the full type contracts and extension API, see srcjs/src/schema/ARCHITECTURE.md.

title: Schema System
text:
Producer / consumer contract
Every advanced feature splits into a side that produces typed state and a side that consumes it.








Feature
Producer
Consumer




Banks (footnotes / axes / legends / conditions)
tabviz(footnotes = …, conditions = …) + schema contributeBanks
renderer reads ctx.banks


Conditions
condition(name, rule) — evaluated at spec build, materialized as a boolean vector on banks.conditions
renderer looks up ctx.banks.conditions[name].values[ctx.rowIndex]


styleMapping (4-mode)
col_text(bold = cond("sig")) or col_text(bold = ~ p < 0.05)
a shared resolver runs at the cell-style site, looking up the per-row value through the right mode


Variants
col_interval(variant = "bracket_muted") — stays on the wire as authoring intent
compiled to primitive options at ingest; renderer reads expanded options + emits tags


Tags + nodeRules
