  rcolor = c("#2563eb", NA, NA, "#2563eb", NA),
  rbadge = c(NA, "Primary", NA, NA, NA),
  # Cell styling (overrides for specific cells)
  hr_color = c(NA, "#16a34a", "#16a34a", NA, "#71717a")
)

tabviz(combined,
  label = "label",
  row_type = "rtype", row_bold = "rbold", row_color = "rcolor", row_badge = "rbadge",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("hr", "HR", color = "hr_color"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

title: Cell Styling
text:
dplyr Workflow for Style Columns
A typical workflow computes style columns with dplyr before plotting:


Code
meta_results <- data.frame(
  study = c("SHIFT", "PARADIGM-HF", "DAPA-HF", "EMPEROR-Reduced"),
  drug_class = c("Ivabradine", "ARNI", "SGLT2i", "SGLT2i"),
  hr = c(0.82, 0.80, 0.74, 0.75),
  lower = c(0.75, 0.73, 0.65, 0.65),
  upper = c(0.90, 0.87, 0.85, 0.86),
  pval = c(0.0001, 0.0001, 0.0001, 0.0001)
)

styled_results <- meta_results |>
  mutate(
    # Significance styling
    is_sig = pval < 0.05,
    sig_stars = case_when(
      pval < 0.001 ~ "***",
      pval < 0.01 ~ "**",
      pval < 0.05 ~ "*",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    # Effect direction
    effect_color = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
      lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
      TRUE ~ "#71717a"
    ),
    # Drug class styling
    class_color = case_when(
      drug_class == "SGLT2i" ~ "#2563eb",
      drug_class == "ARNI" ~ "#7c3aed",
      TRUE ~ "#525252"
    )
  )

tabviz(styled_results,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug_class", "Class", color = "class_color"),
    col_numeric("hr", "HR", bold = "is_sig", color = "effect_color"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", color = "effect_color"),
    col_pvalue("pval", badge = "sig_stars"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

title: Cell Styling
text:
Style Column Naming Conventions
Keep your data organized by using consistent naming:



Style Type
Suggested Suffix
Example




Bold
_bold
hr_bold, is_bold


Color
_color
effect_color, pval_color


Background
_bg
sig_bg, row_bg


Badge
_badge
sig_badge, status_badge


Icon
_icon
status_icon



This makes it clear which columns are data vs styling, and helps when debugging formatting issues.

title: Cell Styling
text:
See Also

Styling Overview — Where this layer fits in the cascade
Row Styling — Whole-row formatting and semantic-token shortcuts
Themes — Theme-aware color tokens (accent, emphasis, etc.)
Columns — col_*() argument reference

title: Visualizations
text: tabviz embeds visualizations directly inside table columns. Four viz_*() column types cover the common patterns; each gets its own focused page.

title: Visualizations
text:
The Four viz_* Columns



Column
Use this when…
Guide




viz_forest()
You have point estimates with confidence intervals (meta-analyses, treatment effects)
Forest Plots


viz_bar()
You’re comparing single values across rows (counts, scores, percentages)
Bar Charts


viz_boxplot()
You care about median, spread, and outliers across distributions
Boxplots


viz_violin()
You need to see full distribution shapes (bimodality, skewness)
Violin Plots









Noteviz_* vs col_*



viz_*() columns are focal visualizations with their own axes and scales — designed for direct visual comparison across rows. The simpler col_*() family (col_bar(), col_sparkline()) is for inline cell content that fits in a cell without a dedicated axis.
If you want a quick weight indicator inside a row, reach for col_bar(). If you want a true comparable bar chart, reach for viz_bar().

title: Visualizations
text:
Common Features
All viz_*() columns share:

Axis configuration — axis_range, axis_ticks, axis_gridlines, axis_label
Pan and zoom — mouse wheel, drag, double-click reset (per column)
Theming — colors adapt to the active theme; multi-effect series use the theme’s effect_colors palette
Marker-styling cascade — row-level row_accent / row_emphasis / row_muted and per-row marker_color reach into viz cells. Single-effect viz: fill replacement. Multi-effect viz: outline augment. See Visualization Styling for the full 4-layer cascade with rendered examples across viz types.
Annotations — refline() for reference lines (and forest_annotation() on viz_forest). Use null_value as a shortcut for a single themed reference line.
Tooltips — opt in via web_interaction(tooltip_fields = ...)
Export — SVG / PNG / PDF via save_plot()







TipTheme Effect Colors



When you have multiple effects per row (multiple bar series, boxplot groups, or forest effects), colors come from the theme’s series_anchors input. Each anchor expands into a 7-field slot bundle (fill, stroke, fill_muted, etc.) at resolve time:
web_theme_nejm() |>
  set_inputs(series_anchors = c("#2563eb", "#dc2626", "#16a34a"))
Individual effects can still override the theme via their own color parameter.

title: Visualizations
Visualizations
text:
Exporting Visualizations
p <- tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "log", null_value = 1)
))

save_plot(p, "figure.svg")                           # vector, no deps
save_plot(p, "figure.png", width = 1200, scale = 2)  # raster, needs rsvg
save_plot(p, "figure.pdf", width = 800)              # vector, needs rsvg
See Export for details.

title: Visualizations
text:
See Also

Forest Plots — Confidence intervals with viz_forest()
Bar Charts — Magnitude comparisons with viz_bar()
Boxplots — Distribution summaries with viz_boxplot()
Violin Plots — Full distributions with viz_violin()
Visualization Styling — The 4-layer color cascade across all viz types
Columns — Inline columns including col_bar() and col_sparkline()

title: Forest Plots
text: Forest plots display point estimates with confidence intervals — the standard visualization for meta-analyses and clinical trial results. Build them with the viz_forest() column inside tabviz(), or use the forest_plot() shortcut for quick one-liners.

title: Forest Plots
text:
Data Mapping
viz_forest() uses a column-mapping pattern: name the columns that hold your data, not the values themselves.


Code
data(effect_sizes)

effect_sizes |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(label = "study", columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ))







Required Mappings








Argument
What it is
Example




point
Point estimate column
Hazard ratio, odds ratio, mean difference


lower
Lower CI bound column
95% CI lower


upper
Upper CI bound column
95% CI upper





Handling Missing Values
tabviz uses NA strategically for structured layouts:

Header rows: row_type = "header" with NA for effect estimates
Spacer rows: row_type = "spacer" for visual separation
Missing estimates: data rows where the effect couldn’t be calculated



Code
data(glp1_trials)

glp1_trials |>
  filter(group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5) |>
  tabviz(label = "study", row_type = "row_type", row_bold = "row_bold",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    )
  )

title: Forest Plots
text:
viz_forest() Arguments








Argument
Default
Purpose




point / lower / upper
required (single-effect mode)
Column names for the point estimate and CI bounds


effects
NULL
List of effect_forest() items for multi-effect mode


header
NULL
Column header text


width
auto
Column width in pixels


scale
"linear"
"linear" or "log"


null_value
NULL
Position of the dashed reference line (e.g. 1 for ratios, 0 for differences)


axis_range
auto
c(min, max) overriding the auto-calculated range


axis_ticks
auto
Custom tick positions


axis_gridlines
FALSE
Show vertical gridlines at tick positions


axis_label
"Effect"
X-axis label


annotations
NULL
List of refline() / forest_annotation() items


shared_axis
FALSE
Share axis range with sibling forest columns


show_axis
TRUE
Show the axis

title: Forest Plots
text:
Scale and Null Value

Log Scale
Use for ratios (hazard ratio, odds ratio, risk ratio) — effects are multiplicative around null_value = 1:


Code
effect_sizes |>
  filter(outcome == "Primary") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(label = "study", columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)")
  ))












WarningLog Scale Requires Positive Values



All values in point, lower, and upper must be positive. Zero or negative values will error.




Linear Scale
Use for differences (mean difference, risk difference, SMD) — effects are additive around null_value = 0:


Code
diff_data <- data.frame(
  comparison = c("Treatment A", "Treatment B", "Treatment C"),
  mean_diff = c(-2.5, 1.3, -0.8),
  lower = c(-4.1, -0.2, -2.1),
  upper = c(-0.9, 2.8, 0.5)
)

tabviz(diff_data, label = "comparison", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "mean_diff", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "linear", null_value = 0,
             axis_label = "Mean Difference (95% CI)")
))

title: Forest Plots
text:
Multiple Effects
Display multiple effect estimates per row using effects = list(effect_forest(...), ...). Useful for comparing analyses or sensitivity scenarios:


Code
multi_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("PIONEER", "SUMMIT", "HORIZON"),
  n = c(2450, 1890, 3200),
  itt_or = c(0.72, 0.78, 0.65),
  itt_lo = c(0.58, 0.64, 0.52),
  itt_hi = c(0.89, 0.95, 0.81),
  pp_or = c(0.68, 0.73, 0.61),
  pp_lo = c(0.53, 0.58, 0.47),
  pp_hi = c(0.87, 0.92, 0.79)
)

tabviz(multi_data, label = "study", title = "Sensitivity Analysis Comparison",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_interval("itt_or", "itt_lo", "itt_hi", header = "Primary OR"),
    viz_forest(
      scale = "log", null_value = 1,
      effects = list(
        effect_forest("itt_or", "itt_lo", "itt_hi", label = "ITT", color = "#2563eb"),
        effect_forest("pp_or", "pp_lo", "pp_hi", label = "Per-Protocol", color = "#16a34a")
      )
    )
  )
)







effect_forest() Arguments







Argument
Purpose




point
Column name for point estimates


lower
Column name for lower bounds


upper
Column name for upper bounds


label
Display label for legend


color
Override color for this effect


shape
Override shape: "square", "circle", "diamond", "triangle"





Side-by-Side Forest Columns
When comparing entirely different outcomes, separate viz_forest() columns are often clearer than overlaid effects:


Code
comparison_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Cardiovascular", "Mortality", "Hospitalization", "Composite"),
  na_hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.78, 0.76),
  na_lo = c(0.58, 0.71, 0.64, 0.65),
  na_hi = c(0.89, 1.02, 0.95, 0.89),
  eu_hr = c(0.68, 0.79, 0.82, 0.74),
  eu_lo = c(0.52, 0.62, 0.67, 0.61),
  eu_hi = c(0.89, 1.01, 1.00, 0.90)
)

tabviz(comparison_data, label = "study",
  title = "Regional Comparison: North America vs Europe",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "na_hr", lower = "na_lo", upper = "na_hi",
               header = "North America", scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 180),
    viz_forest(point = "eu_hr", lower = "eu_lo", upper = "eu_hi",
               header = "Europe", scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 180)
  )
)












TipSide-by-Side vs Multi-Effect




Side-by-side viz_forest() columns — comparing different outcomes or populations on potentially different scales.
Multi-effect overlays via effect_forest() — comparing the same outcome across analyses (sensitivity, ITT vs PP) on a single shared scale.





shared_axis
Set shared_axis = TRUE on multiple viz_forest() columns to lock them to a common axis range so the eye can compare across columns directly.

title: Forest Plots
text:
Marker Customization
Customize marker appearance based on data values using formula expressions on tabviz():


Code
effect_sizes |>
  filter(outcome == "Primary") |>
  head(6) |>
  tabviz(label = "study", title = "Marker Styling with Formulas",
    marker_shape = ~ ifelse(phase == "Phase 3", "circle", "square"),
    marker_color = ~ ifelse(significant, "#16a34a", "#94a3b8"),
    marker_opacity = ~ ifelse(n > 200, 1, 0.7),
    columns = list(
      col_text("phase", "Phase"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    )
  )














Argument
Type
Effect




marker_color
string / formula
CSS color for marker fill


marker_shape
string / formula
"square", "circle", "diamond", "triangle"


marker_opacity
numeric / formula
Transparency (0–1)


marker_size
numeric / formula / "col"
Size multiplier (1 = default), or column name for proportional sizing



All marker arguments accept either a column name or a formula expression (~ ...). Pass marker_size = "col_name" to scale markers by a column (e.g. study weight in a meta-analysis).

title: Forest Plots
text:
Axis Control
Axis arguments live on viz_forest() itself — they’re per-column.

Axis Range


Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_range = c(0.3, 2.0))
))








Custom Tick Values


Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "log", null_value = 1,
             axis_range = c(0.3, 2.0),
             axis_ticks = c(0.5, 0.7, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0))
))








Gridlines


Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
             scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_gridlines = TRUE)
))

title: Forest Plots
text:
Reference Lines and Annotations
null_value draws a single dashed reference line. For more, pass annotations = list(...) of refline() / forest_annotation() items.


Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", title = "With Custom Reference Line",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               annotations = list(
                 refline(0.8, label = "Threshold", style = "dashed", color = "#e11d48")
               ))
  )
)








Code
tabviz(axis_demo, label = "study", title = "Clinical Thresholds",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               annotations = list(
                 refline(0.5, label = "Strong", style = "solid", color = "#16a34a"),
                 refline(0.8, label = "Moderate", style = "dashed", color = "#f59e0b")
               ))
  )
)







refline() Arguments



Argument
Purpose




value
X-axis position


label
Optional label text


style
"solid", "dashed", "dotted"


color
Line color


width
Line width (default 1.5)


opacity
Line opacity 0–1 (default 0.6)



forest_annotation() adds richer annotations (regions, text callouts) — see the reference for the full surface.

title: Forest Plots
text:
Working with Meta-Analysis Results


Code
library(metafor)

res <- rma(yi = log_or, sei = se, data = studies, method = "REML")

forest_data <- studies |>
  mutate(
    or = exp(log_or),
    lower = exp(log_or - 1.96 * se),
    upper = exp(log_or + 1.96 * se)
  ) |>
  bind_rows(
    tibble(
      study = "Pooled Estimate",
      or = exp(res$b),
      lower = exp(res$ci.lb),
      upper = exp(res$ci.ub),
      row_type = "summary",
      row_bold = TRUE
    )
  )

tabviz(forest_data,
  label = "study", row_type = "row_type", row_bold = "row_bold",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)

title: Forest Plots
text:
See Also

Visualization Styling — How row_accent / marker_color cascade into marker colors
Visualizations Overview — Tooltips, export, common features
Titles & Labels — Adding context to your plots
Row Styling — Headers, summaries, indentation
Themes — Visual styling

title: Visualization Styling
text: Every viz_* column — viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin — participates in the same color cascade. Markers, bars, boxes, and violins all resolve their fill (and sometimes their outline) by walking four layers from general to specific. Most of the time you only touch the layer that matches your intent and let the rest stay defaulted.

title: Visualization Styling
text:
The four layers
Each glyph’s color is decided by the most-specific layer that has a value:








Layer
Source
Wins when…




4. Per-row literal
marker_color = "col" or ~ formula on tabviz()
Value is non-NA for that row.


3. Row semantic class
row_emphasis / row_muted / row_accent / row_bold / row_fill on tabviz()
Layer 4 is NA. Single-effect: replaces fill with the token’s theme color. Multi-effect: preserves per-effect fill, adds an outline in the token’s color.


2. Per-effect literal
effect_*(color = "#hex") inside viz_*()
Layers 3–4 don’t apply. Sets the fill for that effect on every row.


1. Theme palette
theme.shapes.effect_colors[i]
Nothing else is set. Effects cycle through the palette by index.



Two things to internalize before the examples:

NA falls through. A marker_color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.001, "darkred", NA) colors the strict-significant rows red and lets every other row drop to whatever Layer 3 or below would have done.
Single vs. multi-effect matters at Layer 3. A semantic class on a single-effect viz repaints the glyph; on a multi-effect viz it adds an outline so the per-effect color identity (a key visual encoding) survives.

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Layer 1 — Theme palette
When you don’t say anything about color, viz cells pick from the active theme’s effect_colors palette. Single-effect viz takes the first color; multi-effect viz cycles by effect index.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  title = "Layer 1: theme palette",
  subtitle = "No color set anywhere — the marker takes the theme's first effect color"
)






Switching themes restyles automatically:


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Same code, JAMA theme",
  subtitle = "The marker color tracks the active palette"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Layer 2 — Per-effect color
Set color = "#hex" inside an effect_*() to nail down a single series. Useful when the encoding is intrinsic to the metric (“ITT is blue, per-protocol is green”) rather than to a row.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(effects = list(
      effect_forest("itt_hr", "itt_lo", "itt_hi",
                    label = "ITT", color = "#2563eb"),
      effect_forest("pp_hr",  "pp_lo",  "pp_hi",
                    label = "Per-protocol", color = "#16a34a")
    ), scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  title = "Layer 2: per-effect colors",
  subtitle = "ITT and per-protocol get fixed identities across every row"
)






The same idea on viz_bar:


Code
sales <- data.frame(
  region = c("North", "South", "East", "West"),
  q1 = c(245, 189, 312, 98),
  q2 = c(278, 215, 345, 125),
  q3 = c(312, 242, 398, 156)
)

tabviz(sales, label = "region",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("q1", label = "Q1", color = "#3b82f6"),
      effect_bar("q2", label = "Q2", color = "#22c55e"),
      effect_bar("q3", label = "Q3", color = "#f59e0b"),
      header = "Sales ($K)"
    )
  ),
  title = "Per-effect colors on viz_bar",
  subtitle = "Each quarter holds its identity across regions"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Layer 3 — Row semantic cascade
The five semantic tokens — row_emphasis, row_muted, row_accent, row_bold, row_fill — already style row text (see Row Styling). They also reach into viz cells. The behavior depends on whether the viz column has one effect or several.

Single-effect: fill replacement
The marker fill is replaced with the theme color. Below, row_accent highlights strongly significant trials in the theme’s accent color, row_muted dims the non-significant ones.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_pvalue("pval"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  row_accent = ~ pval < 0.01,
  row_muted  = ~ pval >= 0.10,
  title = "Layer 3 (single-effect): fill replacement",
  subtitle = "Accented rows take the accent color; muted rows fade out"
)






The cascade is themable — the same code under web_theme_nejm() uses JAMA’s accent color instead.


Multi-effect: outline augment
If the column has more than one effect, replacing the fill would destroy the per-effect color encoding. The cascade preserves the fill and adds an outline in the theme color, so each accented row gets a visible “this row is special” cue without losing its ITT/per-protocol identity.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(effects = list(
      effect_forest("itt_hr", "itt_lo", "itt_hi",
                    label = "ITT", color = "#2563eb"),
      effect_forest("pp_hr",  "pp_lo",  "pp_hi",
                    label = "Per-protocol", color = "#16a34a")
    ), scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  row_accent = ~ pval < 0.01,
  title = "Layer 3 (multi-effect): outline augment",
  subtitle = "Per-effect colors preserved; accented rows get an outline"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Layer 4 — Per-row literal marker_color
For exact control — a categorical palette, a colorblind-safe encoding, or matching a primary color — pass a column or formula to marker_color. Returning NA falls through to the layers below.


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_pvalue("pval"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.001, "#b91c1c", NA),
  title = "Layer 4: per-row literal",
  subtitle = "p < 0.001 rows go dark red; the rest fall through to the theme"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
Combined precedence
When multiple layers are active, the most-specific one wins per row. Below: the strictest-significance rows are pinned to dark red (Layer 4), the still-significant rows take the theme accent (Layer 3), and the rest fall through to the theme palette (Layer 1).


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_pvalue("pval"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "HR")
  ),
  row_accent   = ~ pval < 0.05,                                    # Layer 3
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.001, "#b91c1c", NA_character_), # Layer 4
  title = "All layers together",
  subtitle = "p < 0.001 → red (L4), 0.001–0.05 → accent (L3), > 0.05 → palette (L1)"
)

title: Visualization Styling
section: The cascade is uniform across viz types
text:
The cascade is uniform across viz types
Everything above works identically on viz_bar, viz_boxplot, and viz_violin. A single example with a row_accent on each:

viz_bar


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("n"), header = "Enrollment", axis_label = "N enrolled")
  ),
  row_accent = ~ n > 1000,
  title = "row_accent on viz_bar",
  subtitle = "Bars for large trials take the accent color"
)








viz_violin


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_violin(effect_violin(data = "dist"),
               header = "Effect distribution",
               axis_label = "Posterior HR",
               show_quartiles = TRUE)
  ),
  row_accent = ~ pval < 0.01,
  title = "row_accent on viz_violin",
  subtitle = "Accented rows: the violin fill switches to the accent color"
)








viz_boxplot


Code
tabviz(trials, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_boxplot(effect_boxplot(data = "dist"),
                header = "Effect distribution",
                axis_label = "Posterior HR")
  ),
  row_accent = ~ pval < 0.01,
  title = "row_accent on viz_boxplot",
  subtitle = "Accented rows: the box fill switches to the accent color"
)

title: Visualization Styling
text:
When to reach for what

Stick with the theme when the viz is single-purpose and the row’s role isn’t categorical. The palette already does the right thing.
Use effect_*(color = ...) when the color identifies the metric (ITT vs. per-protocol, drug A vs. B). It belongs to the effect, not the row.
Use a row semantic token (row_accent / row_emphasis / row_muted / row_bold / row_fill) when the color identifies the row (“this is the primary outcome”, “this is exploratory”). Themable, single-source: the row text and its viz both update.
Use marker_color (column or formula) when you need exact-pixel control — categorical row palettes, primary alignment, colorblind-safe encodings. Use NA to fall through to the cascade for rows you don’t want to override.

title: Visualization Styling
text:
See also

Row Styling — Text-side semantic classes that drive the same cascade
Styling Cascade — Higher-level overview of all four cascade layers across the package
Themes — Where effect_colors, accent, foreground, and muted live
Forest Plots, Bar Charts, Boxplots, Violin Plots — Per-viz feature reference

title: The Fluent API
text: tabviz exposes a single, unified fluent API for every kind of modification you’d want to make — styling, column structure, row ordering, data, theme, zoom, and cell edits. Every verb returns the modified object and dispatches on input type, so the same call works on a static WebSpec, a rendered htmlwidget, or a live Shiny proxy.

title: The Fluent API
text:
Two ways to style
Styling can live on the tabviz() call or come from fluent modifiers chained after it. Both are fully supported and produce identical output. Pick based on intent:







Use top-level args (tabviz(...))
Use fluent modifiers (|> set_*())




One-shot construction
Post-hoc updates to an existing plot


Everything known upfront
Building variants from a single base


Concise static plots
Live updates inside a Shiny tabviz_proxy()





Code
# Top-level: everything in one call
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  row_type = "rtype",
  row_bold = "rbold",
  marker_color = "effect_color",
  theme = web_theme_nejm()
)

# Fluent: same result, modifier-driven
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
) |>
  set_row_style(type = "rtype", bold = "rbold") |>
  set_marker_style(color = "effect_color") |>
  set_theme("nejm")


For Shiny proxies the choice is made for you — incremental updates must go through fluent verbs.

title: The Fluent API
text:
Static vs Proxy Dispatch
Every fluent verb accepts three kinds of input and behaves accordingly:







Input type
What happens




WebSpec (from tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE))
Returns a new WebSpec with the change applied in R.


htmlwidget (from tabviz(...))
Returns a new widget with the change baked into its spec.


tabviz_proxy (from tabviz_proxy("id"))
Sends a custom message to the running widget in Shiny — no re-render.



# Static WebSpec
spec <- tabviz(data, label = "study", .spec_only = TRUE) |>
  sort_rows("hr", "desc") |>
  add_column(col_numeric("n", "N"), after = "study")

# htmlwidget — same verbs
widget <- tabviz(data, ...) |>
  set_theme("nejm") |>
  resize_column("hr", width = 140)

# Shiny proxy — same verbs
observeEvent(input$sort_hr, {
  tabviz_proxy("plot") |>
    sort_rows("hr", "desc")
})
A handful of verbs are proxy-only because they depend on row identity post-render (set_cell, set_row_label, clear_edits). On a static input they emit a warning and leave the object unchanged.

title: The Fluent API
text:
The Fluent Pattern
Every verb returns the modified object, so calls chain:
tabviz(data, ...) |>     # Create plot
  set_row_style(...) |>  # Modify rows
  add_column(...) |>     # Add a column
  sort_rows(...) |>      # Reorder rows
  set_theme(...)         # Apply theme






NoteImmutability



Each verb returns a new object; the input is unchanged. Safe to derive multiple variants from a single base:
base <- tabviz(data, ...)
clinical <- base |> set_theme("nejm")
neon     <- base |> set_theme("synthwave")

title: The Fluent API
text:
Styling Verbs
All styling verbs accept either column names (strings) or formula expressions (~ ...):
# Using column names (pre-computed)
plot |> set_row_style(bold = "is_significant")

# Using formula expressions (computed on the fly)
plot |> set_row_style(bold = ~ pvalue < 0.05)

# Mix both
plot |> set_marker_style(
  color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b"),
  shape = "marker_shape_column"
)
See Row Styling and Cell Styling for formula details.

set_row_style()
Row-level styling. Your primary tool for visual hierarchy — distinguishing headers from data rows, highlighting summaries, showing nested structure.
plot |> set_row_style(
  type    = "rtype",    # "header", "data", "summary", "spacer"
  bold    = "rbold",
  italic  = "ritalic",
  color   = "rcolor",   # CSS colors
  bg      = "rbg",
  badge   = "rbadge",   # text label anchored to first column
  icon    = "ricon",    # emoji/unicode anchored to first column
  indent  = "rindent"   # 0, 1, 2, ...
)






Warning



badge and icon render into the first visible text column’s cell. If you reorder columns, they re-anchor to the new first column. See Binding rules for details.




Code
styled_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Primary", "  CV Death", "  MI", "Secondary", "  Stroke"),
  hr = c(NA, 0.82, 0.79, NA, 0.88),
  lower = c(NA, 0.72, 0.68, NA, 0.74),
  upper = c(NA, 0.94, 0.92, NA, 1.05),
  rtype = c("header", "data", "data", "header", "data"),
  rbold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE),
  rindent = c(0, 1, 1, 0, 1)
)

forest_plot(styled_data,
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper", label = "study",
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
) |>
  set_row_style(type = "rtype", bold = "rbold", indent = "rindent") |>
  set_theme("nejm")








set_marker_style()
Interval marker styling (points and lines for effect estimates):
plot |> set_marker_style(
  color   = "marker_color",  # CSS colors
  shape   = "marker_shape",  # "square", "circle", "diamond", "triangle"
  opacity = "marker_alpha",  # 0–1
  size    = "marker_size"    # size multiplier
)


Code
marker_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Trial A", "Trial B", "Trial C", "Trial D"),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.95, 0.68, 1.15),
  lower = c(0.55, 0.78, 0.52, 0.90),
  upper = c(0.94, 1.15, 0.89, 1.47),
  direction = c("benefit", "neutral", "benefit", "harm")
) |>
  mutate(
    marker_color = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
      lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
      TRUE ~ "#64748b"
    ),
    marker_shape = ifelse(direction == "benefit", "circle", "square")
  )

forest_plot(marker_data,
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper", label = "study",
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
) |>
  set_marker_style(color = "marker_color", shape = "marker_shape") |>
  set_theme("nejm")








set_column_style()
Per-cell styling for a specific column:
plot |> set_column_style(column = "hr",
  bold  = "is_sig",
  color = "hr_color",
  bg    = "hr_bg"
)






TipStyling priority



When both row and column styles target the same cell, column styles win. Row styles apply to cells without column overrides — so you can set row-wide defaults with column-specific highlights.




set_theme() and theme modifiers
# Apply by name
plot |> set_theme("nejm")

# Apply by object
plot |> set_theme(web_theme_nejm())

# Modify a Tier 1 input
plot |> set_theme(web_theme_nejm() |> set_inputs(brand = "#1a365d"))

# Modify spacing on the active theme (operates on a WebTheme directly)
custom <- web_theme_nejm() |>
  set_spacing(row_height = 24) |>
  set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE)
plot |> set_theme(custom)
Theme modifiers operate on a WebTheme object (not a plot directly). Build the theme you want, then attach it via set_theme().







Modifier
Edits




set_inputs()
Tier 1 customer inputs (brand, accent, series, fonts, status)


set_density() set_header_style() set_first_column_style()
Per-table variant setters (density, header style, first-column style)


set_spacing()
Per-token spacing override on top of the density preset


set_theme_field(theme, path, value)
Generic deep setter; path walks the theme tree



See Themes guide for the full ontology and Styling reference for parameter tables.

title: The Fluent API
text:
Column Operations
Restructure the columns of a running plot without re-rendering.

add_column()
Insert a new column. Use after to position it; NULL appends.
plot |> add_column(col_numeric("year", "Year"), after = "n")
plot |> add_column(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper"))
Static:


Code
df <- data.frame(
  study = c("A", "B", "C"),
  hr = c(0.8, 1.1, 1.4),
  n = c(100, 200, 150)
)
tabviz(df, label = "study",
  columns = list(col_numeric("hr", "HR"))
) |>
  add_column(col_numeric("n", "N"), after = "hr")






Proxy:
observeEvent(input$show_year, {
  tabviz_proxy("plot") |>
    add_column(col_numeric("year", "Year"), after = "n")
})


remove_column()
plot |> remove_column("notes")
On a proxy this hides the column without dropping data; it can be re-added later.


move_column()
# Numeric index (1-based on R side; verb converts to 0-based for the store)
plot |> move_column("notes", to = 1)

# Or position relative to another column
plot |> move_column("notes", before = "hr")


resize_column()
plot |> resize_column("hr", width = 140)   # pixels


update_column()
Change column properties — header, alignment, wrap, width, or any option in the column’s options list:
plot |> update_column("hr", header = "Hazard Ratio", align = "right")
plot |> update_column("hr", options = list(decimals = 3))
Unknown keys are merged into options, matching R-side semantics.

title: The Fluent API
text:
Row & Data Operations

sort_rows()
plot |> sort_rows("hr", direction = "asc")
plot |> sort_rows("hr", direction = "desc")
plot |> sort_rows("hr", direction = "none")   # restore original order
On a static WebSpec, direction = "none" is a no-op. On a proxy it clears the sort.


filter_rows() and clear_filters()
Operators: "eq", "neq", "gt", "lt", "contains".
plot |> filter_rows("n", operator = "gt", value = 100)
plot |> filter_rows("drug_class", operator = "eq", value = "SGLT2i")

plot |> clear_filters()
On a WebSpec, filter_rows() mutates @data in place. On a proxy it applies a display-only filter — the original data is preserved.


select_rows() and move_row()
plot |> select_rows(c("study_A", "study_B"))       # by row id
plot |> move_row("study_C", to = 1)                # to top
select_rows() on a proxy forwards selection state to any Shiny input bindings tied to the widget.


update_data()
Replace the underlying data. On a proxy, this requires a full WebSpec (not just a data frame) because column types must be re-serialized:
new_spec <- tabviz(new_df, label = "study", columns = list(...),
                  .spec_only = TRUE)
tabviz_proxy("plot") |> update_data(new_spec)

title: The Fluent API
text:
Groups & Zoom

toggle_group()
Collapse or expand a group header:
plot |> toggle_group("North America")               # flip current state
plot |> toggle_group("North America", collapsed = TRUE)
plot |> toggle_group("Europe",        collapsed = FALSE)


set_zoom()
plot |> set_zoom(zoom = 0.8)
plot |> set_zoom(zoom = 1.0, auto_fit = TRUE)
plot |> set_zoom(max_width = 900, max_height = 600)
plot |> set_zoom(show_controls = FALSE)
Any subset of arguments may be passed; unchanged knobs are left alone.

title: The Fluent API
text:
Cell Edits (proxy-only)
These verbs mutate rendered state and require a live widget. On a static WebSpec they warn and return unchanged.
# Change a single cell value
tabviz_proxy("plot") |> set_cell("row_id", "hr", 1.23)

# Rename a row's label
tabviz_proxy("plot") |> set_row_label("row_id", "Renamed")

# Drop all in-browser edits
tabviz_proxy("plot") |> clear_edits()

title: The Fluent API
text:
Conditional Styling with dplyr
Compute style columns with dplyr before plotting:


Code
analysis_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("EMPA-REG", "CANVAS", "DECLARE", "DAPA-HF"),
  hr = c(0.62, 0.86, 0.93, 0.74),
  lower = c(0.49, 0.75, 0.84, 0.65),
  upper = c(0.77, 0.97, 1.03, 0.85),
  pval = c(0.0001, 0.02, 0.17, 0.0001)
)

styled_analysis <- analysis_data |>
  mutate(
    is_sig = pval < 0.05,
    sig_color = ifelse(is_sig, "#16a34a", "#94a3b8")
  )

forest_plot(styled_analysis,
  point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper", label = "study",
  columns = list(col_pvalue("pval")),
  scale = "log", null_value = 1
) |>
  set_marker_style(color = "sig_color") |>
  set_row_style(bold = "is_sig") |>
  set_theme("nejm")












TipStyle column naming



Use consistent suffixes: _bold, _color, _bg, _badge. Makes it clear which columns are data vs styling.

title: The Fluent API
The Fluent API
text:
Verb Reference
Styling







Verb
Purpose




set_row_style()
Row-level (bold, italic, color, type, indent, badge, icon)


set_marker_style()
Interval marker (color, shape, opacity, size)


set_column_style()
Per-cell styling for one column


set_theme()
Apply theme by name or object


set_brand(), set_accent(), set_density(), set_header_style()
Theme-level modifiers (operate on a WebTheme, then set_theme() to attach)



Columns



Verb
Purpose




add_column()
Insert at a position


remove_column()
Drop / hide


move_column()
Reorder by index or relative position


resize_column()
Set width in pixels


update_column()
Modify header, alignment, options



Rows & data



Verb
Purpose




sort_rows()
Sort by a column


filter_rows()
Apply a predicate


clear_filters()
Remove all filters


select_rows()
Set the selected-row set


move_row()
Reorder by index


update_data()
Replace underlying data (WebSpec on proxy)



Groups, cells, global



Verb
Purpose




toggle_group()
Collapse/expand a group header


set_cell()
Change a cell value (proxy-only)


set_row_label()
Rename a row’s label (proxy-only)


clear_edits()
Drop in-browser edits (proxy-only)


set_zoom()
Zoom / auto-fit / dimensions



See Styling reference and Data-ops reference for complete parameter documentation.

title: Row Groups
text: Organize your forest plot into collapsible sections using the group parameter. Ideal for meta-analyses with natural categories like study design, region, or subpopulation.

title: Row Groups
section: What you can pass to group
text:
What you can pass to group







Form
Effect




group = "region"
Single flat grouping — one collapsible header per unique value


group = c("region", "country")
Hierarchical nesting — outer level collapses inner levels


group = list(web_group("North", collapsed = FALSE), web_group("South", collapsed = TRUE))
Explicit definitions; lets you set initial collapse state and custom labels









Noteweb_group(id, ...) contract



The id of each web_group() must match a value present in the corresponding data column — that’s how tabviz knows which rows belong to which group. With web_group("North", ...) and web_group("South", ...), the data must have a column whose values include "North" and "South" (tabviz auto-detects which column). Use label = if you want the displayed header to differ from the data value.

title: Row Groups
text:
Basic Grouping
Pass a column name to group to create collapsible sections:


Code
grouped_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Study A1", "Study A2", "Study B1", "Study B2", "Study B3"),
  region = c("North", "North", "South", "South", "South"),
  or = c(0.8, 0.9, 1.1, 1.3, 0.95),
  lower = c(0.6, 0.7, 0.85, 1.0, 0.7),
  upper = c(1.1, 1.15, 1.4, 1.7, 1.3)
)

tabviz(grouped_data,
  label = "study",
  group = "region",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1)
  )
)






Click the group headers to collapse or expand sections. Groups are rendered with:

Distinct header rows with chevron indicators
Automatic indentation of child rows
Subtle background shading for visual hierarchy

title: Row Groups
text:
Hierarchical Grouping
For multi-level organization, pass multiple column names as a vector. tabviz creates nested, collapsible hierarchies:


Code
hierarchical_data <- data.frame(
  study = c(
    "Northeast Trial", "Southeast Trial", "Midwest Trial",
    "Ontario Study", "Quebec Study",
    "England RCT", "Scotland RCT",
    "Bavaria Trial", "Berlin Trial"
  ),
  region = c(
    rep("Americas", 5),
    rep("Europe", 4)
  ),
  country = c(
    rep("USA", 3), rep("Canada", 2),
    rep("UK", 2), rep("Germany", 2)
  ),
  or = c(0.82, 0.91, 0.78, 0.85, 0.88, 0.92, 0.87, 0.79, 0.84),
  lower = c(0.68, 0.75, 0.62, 0.70, 0.72, 0.76, 0.71, 0.64, 0.68),
  upper = c(0.99, 1.10, 0.98, 1.03, 1.07, 1.11, 1.07, 0.98, 1.04)
)

tabviz(hierarchical_data,
  label = "study",
  group = c("region", "country"),
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm()
)






Each level of the hierarchy:

Can be collapsed independently
Has progressively deeper indentation
Uses subtle visual differentiation (font weight, background)

title: Row Groups
Row Groups
text:
Starting Collapsed
To have groups start in a collapsed state, you’ll need to use explicit group definitions with web_group():


Code
tabviz(grouped_data,
  label = "study",
  group = list(
    web_group("North", collapsed = FALSE),
    web_group("South", collapsed = TRUE)
  ),
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1)
  )
)


This is useful for large datasets where you want users to expand only the sections they’re interested in.

title: Row Groups
text:
Grouping vs Row Styling
tabviz provides two complementary ways to organize data:








Feature
Purpose
Best For




group
Automatic collapsible sections from column values
Categorical data with natural groupings


row_type
Manual section headers, summaries, spacers
Structured layouts with subtotals



Use group when your data has a grouping column. Use row_type when you need precise control over the layout structure, such as meta-analysis summary rows. See Row Styling for the manual approach.
You can combine both approaches—use group for automatic organization and row_type for summary diamonds within each group.

title: Row Groups
text:
See Also

Row Styling — Manual row types, headers, summaries, and visual styling
Themes — Customize group header appearance
tabviz() — Complete argument reference

title: Export
text: Export tabviz widgets to static files for publications, presentations, and sharing. SVG is built in via the V8 engine; PNG and PDF go through the optional rsvg package.

title: Export
text:
Quick Example
Build a forest plot, then write it to SVG:


Code
data(glp1_trials)

p <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 180),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_nejm(),
    title = "GLP-1 Agonist Cardiovascular Outcomes"
  )

save_plot(p, "export-examples/forest_example.svg", width = 700)





Exported SVG

title: Export
text:
save_plot() Arguments
save_plot() operates in three sizing modes — see the reference for the full table. The short version: which args you pass determines the mode, not the values.








Argument
Default
Purpose




x
required
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or SplitForest


file
required
Output path; format inferred from extension (.svg, .png, .pdf)


width
NULL
Display width in pixels. NULL preserves natural; with height or ratio triggers relayout.


height
NULL
Display height in pixels. Same semantics as width.


ratio
NULL
Target aspect ratio (width / height). Mode-3 trigger.


anchor
NULL
When only ratio is given, picks "width" / "height" / "auto" for how dims fall out of it.


flex
TRUE
Cap on flex-column absorption (TRUE = 2× / 0.5×; FALSE = no flex; numeric = custom).


auto_wrap
FALSE
Bump wrap on long-text columns to fill tall-aspect height with content.


scale
2
Raster fidelity multiplier for PNG (warns on .svg / .pdf).



Natural mode (no width / height / ratio) renders at the spec’s intrinsic dimensions — exactly what tabviz_natural_dimensions() reports:


Code
# Natural — let the spec decide its own shape
save_plot(p, "natural.svg")

title: Export
text:
Format Comparison








Format
Use case
Dependency




SVG
Vector format, infinite scalability, journals, editing
None (built in)


PNG
Raster format, presentations, screenshots
rsvg package


PDF
Vector format, print publications
rsvg package









NotePNG and PDF require rsvg



save_plot() writes SVG using V8 alone. PNG and PDF formats convert that SVG via rsvg::rsvg_png() / rsvg::rsvg_pdf(). Install with install.packages("rsvg").

title: Export
text:
SVG Export


Code
bar_data <- data.frame(
  department = c("Cardiology", "Oncology", "Neurology", "Pediatrics"),
  enrollment = c(245, 312, 189, 156)
)

p_bar <- tabviz(bar_data, label = "department",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("enrollment", color = "#3b82f6"),
            header = "Enrollment", width = 180, axis_range = c(0, 350)),
    col_numeric("enrollment", "N")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Trial Enrollment by Department"
)

save_plot(p_bar, "export-examples/bar_example.svg", width = 500)





Bar Chart Export

title: Export
text:
Themed Exports
The active theme is preserved on export.


Code
p_jama <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_nejm(),
    title = "Figure 1. Primary Endpoint"
  )

save_plot(p_jama, "export-examples/jama_theme.svg", width = 500)





JAMA Theme




Code
p_dark <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_aurora(),
    title = "Dark Theme Export"
  )

save_plot(p_dark, "export-examples/dark_theme.svg", width = 500)





Dark Theme

title: Export
text:
PNG and PDF Export
# PNG at 2x resolution
save_plot(p, "figure.png", width = 800, scale = 2)

# PDF for print
save_plot(p, "figure.pdf", width = 800)

Resolution Guide for PNG



scale
Effective DPI
Use case




1
72 DPI
Quick preview


2
144 DPI
Web / screen


3
216 DPI
Presentations


4
288 DPI
High-res print

title: Export
section: What matches the screen — and what deliberately doesn’t
text:
What matches the screen — and what deliberately doesn’t
Exports are WYSIWYG by contract: the SVG/PDF/PNG pipeline draws from the same theme tokens, the same layout engine, and (for borders, headers, axis typography) literally the same derivation functions as the live widget. A small set of exceptions is deliberate:

Browser-only effects — glass panes, glows, and aurora backdrop blobs need real compositing; exports omit them rather than fake them.
Interactive page state — an export always renders the whole table, even when the live widget is showing one page of a paginated spec (a figure is the dataset; a page is a view). Multi-page PDF pagination is the exception below.
Raw-spec column widths — driving generateSVG directly from hand-written JSON (no R, no browser) estimates text widths instead of measuring them; columns can land a few pixels off. Every supported flow avoids this: R’s save_plot() injects pixel-exact systemfonts measurements, and widget-triggered exports reuse the live widths.

Anything else that differs between screen and export is a bug — the regression suite diffs the two pixel-by-pixel across the preset matrix.

title: Export
section: Pagination — long tables across multiple pages
text:
Pagination — long tables across multiple pages
When a table is taller than a printed page (long meta-analyses, multi-cohort registries), attach paginate = paginate_spec() (or one of the presets) to break it into pages. The HTML viewer renders one page at a time with prev/next controls; save_plot(*.pdf) emits a multi-page PDF.


Code
data(glp1_trials)
# Synthesize a longer dataset for demonstration
big <- do.call(rbind, replicate(4, glp1_trials |>
  dplyr::filter(row_type == "data"), simplify = FALSE))
big$study <- sprintf("%s %02d", big$study, seq_len(nrow(big)))

p_long <- big |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    group = "group",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 180),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_nejm(),
    title = "Long meta-analysis (paginated)",
    paginate = paginate_letter()
  )

save_plot(p_long, "export-examples/paginated.pdf", width = 700)


The result is a multi-page PDF — one PDF page per logical page, merged via qpdf::pdf_combine():


Code
qpdf::pdf_length("export-examples/paginated.pdf")


[1] 4



Configuring pagination
paginate_spec() exposes the full configuration; presets (paginate_letter(), paginate_a4(), paginate_slide()) pick sensible row counts for common formats.








Argument
Default
Purpose




rows
30
Maximum data rows per page


break_on
"split"
Where forced breaks occur: "split", "group", or "none"


keep_groups
TRUE
Never break inside a group — push to next page instead


orphan_min
3
Minimum rows on a trailing page; pulls rows back if too thin


repeat_header / repeat_legend / repeat_title
TRUE
Repeat page furniture on every PDF page


footnotes_on
"last"
"last" (final page only) or "every"


page_label
"x_of_y"
TRUE/FALSE/"x"/"x_of_y" or a function(page, total)


oversized_group_policy
"overflow"
What to do when a single group is larger than rows: "overflow", "warn", "error"





Shortcuts
The paginate argument on tabviz() and save_plot() accepts several shortcut forms:
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = TRUE)              # default paginate_spec()
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = 50)                # rows-per-page shorthand
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = paginate_letter()) # preset
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = NULL)              # explicit "no pagination"
A fluent modifier paginate() is also available for use after construction:
tabviz(data, ...) |> paginate(rows = 20, orphan_min = 5)
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = TRUE) |> paginate(NULL)  # turn off


Override at save time
save_plot() accepts its own paginate argument that overrides whatever is on the spec — useful for one-off renders without rebuilding:
# Force a denser page count for one render
save_plot(p_long, "out.pdf", paginate = paginate_spec(rows = 50))

# Flatten a paginated spec for a single-image format
save_plot(p_long, "out.png", paginate = NULL)






NoteSingle-image formats flatten by default



.png and .svg are single-image formats and cannot represent multiple pages. When you save a paginated tabviz to one of these, the spec is flattened to a single image and a cli warning fires. Pass paginate = NULL at the call site to silence the warning, or save to .pdf for true paginated output.








NoteMulti-page PDF needs qpdf



Each page is rendered separately to SVG → PDF (via rsvg), then the per-page PDFs are merged with qpdf::pdf_combine(). If qpdf is not installed, save_plot() falls back to writing a numbered series (out_p01.pdf, out_p02.pdf, …) and emits a warning. Install with install.packages("qpdf").




Pagination + split_by
When split_by is active, paginate cascades to every subview — each subview owns its own page set with its own row-count budget. Saving emits one combined multi-page PDF per split leaf:
sf <- tabviz(data, ..., split_by = "region", paginate = paginate_spec(rows = 20))
save_plot(attr(sf, "splitforest"), "out/region.pdf", width = 700)
# Writes: out/North.pdf, out/South.pdf, ... — each with its own pages

title: Export
text:
Exporting Split Tables
Pass a SplitForest (the output of split_table()) to save_split_table() to write one file per subgroup. The directory structure mirrors the split hierarchy.
split_p <- tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group"))

save_split_table(split_p, "output/plots", format = "svg")
# Writes: output/plots/Male/Male_Young.svg, output/plots/Female/Female_Old.svg, ...
save_split_table() accepts the same width, height, scale arguments as save_plot(). format is one of "svg", "pdf", "png".






Tipsave_plot() accepts SplitForest too



If you call save_plot(split_p, "output/plots") it dispatches to save_split_table() automatically. Pass a path with an extension to control the format.

title: Export
text:
See Also

Themes — Style for publication
Reference: save_plot() — Full API

title: Row Styling
text: Control the visual appearance of individual rows using row_* parameters. This gives you precise control over text styling, indentation, colors, and badges—essential for creating publication-ready meta-analysis figures.

title: Row Styling
text:
Text Styling
Control text appearance with row_bold, row_italic, and row_color:


Code
# Use glp1_trials which has pre-computed row_bold
data(glp1_trials)

text_demo <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(group == "Main Trials", row_type == "data") |>
  head(4) |>
  mutate(
    ritalic = !row_bold,
    rcolor = ifelse(row_bold, "#0369a1", "#64748b")
  )

tabviz(text_demo,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log")
  ),
  row_bold = "row_bold",
  row_italic = "ritalic",
  row_color = "rcolor"
)

title: Row Styling
text:
Indentation
Create visual hierarchy with row_indent. Values represent nesting levels (0, 1, 2, …):


Code
indent_demo <- data.frame(
  label = c(
    "All Patients",
    "  Age < 65",
    "    Male", "    Female",
    "  Age >= 65",
    "    Male", "    Female"
  ),
  hr = c(0.78, 0.72, 0.70, 0.75, 0.85, 0.82, 0.88),
  lower = c(0.68, 0.58, 0.54, 0.60, 0.72, 0.66, 0.72),
  upper = c(0.89, 0.89, 0.91, 0.94, 1.00, 1.02, 1.08),
  rindent = c(0, 1, 2, 2, 1, 2, 2),
  rbold = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE)
)

tabviz(indent_demo,
  label = "label",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log")
  ),
  row_indent = "rindent",
  row_bold = "rbold",
  theme = web_theme_nejm()
)

title: Row Styling
text:
Badges
Add small labels to highlight specific rows with row_badge:


Code
# nba_efficiency has an 'award' column perfect for badges
data(nba_efficiency)

nba_efficiency |>
  filter(conference == "East") |>
  head(6) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "player",
    columns = list(
      col_text("team", "Team", width = 60),
      col_numeric("ppg", "PPG"),
      viz_forest(point = "per", lower = "per_lower", upper = "per_upper",
                 null_value = 15, axis_label = "PER")
    ),
    row_badge = "award",
    row_bold = "all_star",
    theme = web_theme_nejm()
  )






Badges are useful for marking awards, flagging key results, or indicating data quality.






WarningBadge and icon anchor to the first column



row_badge and row_icon render into the leftmost visible text column’s cell — they do not belong to the row itself. If you call move_column() or insert a new column with add_column(..., after = "__start__"), the badge will re-attach to the new first column without warning. To keep a stable anchor, keep your label/study column in position 1. See Binding Rules below.








TipSee Also



For more examples with badge data, see Package Data.

title: Row Styling
text:
Background Colors
Highlight rows with row_bg for background colors:


Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "label",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log")
  ),
  row_bg = "background_color"  # Column with hex colors like "#f0f9ff"
)


Use sparingly—subtle tints work best.

title: Row Styling
text:
Semantic Styling
For theme-aware styling that adapts automatically when you change themes, use one of the five semantic tokens. Each names a visual treatment that the theme paints in its own colors — a row “tagged” with row_accent will pick the active theme’s accent color, so the same code restyles itself when you swap from JAMA to Lancet.








Parameter
Treatment
Use for




row_emphasis
Bold + primary-color text and marker
Key results, primary endpoints


row_muted
Lighter color, reduced prominence
Secondary results, exploratory data


row_accent
Bold + accent-color text and marker (most common)
Highlighted findings, callouts


row_bold
Just a weight bump, no color change
Distinguish without coloring


row_fill
Bold + a strong row fill (auto-contrasting fg)
Summary / pooled rows





Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "label",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log")
  ),
  row_emphasis = "is_primary",   # Column with TRUE/FALSE
  row_muted    = "is_secondary", # Column with TRUE/FALSE
  row_fill     = "is_pooled"     # Column with TRUE/FALSE — strong row fill
)


These adapt automatically to any theme, so you don’t need to update color values when switching from web_theme_nejm() to web_theme_nejm(). See Themes — Semantic tokens for the underlying RowSemantic bundles and per-token customization.

Cascading into viz cells
row_accent, row_emphasis, and row_muted aren’t just text styling — they also reach into any viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, or viz_violin column on the same row. The intent (“this row is special”) is declared once and styles both the row text and its visualization.
The behavior depends on whether the viz column has one effect or several:







Viz column
Behavior when row is accented/emphasized/muted




Single-effect (viz_forest(point=..., lower=..., upper=...), viz_bar(effect_bar(...)), etc.)
The marker / bar / box / violin fill is replaced with the theme’s accent / foreground / muted color.


Multi-effect (viz_forest(effects = list(...)), viz_bar(effect_bar(a), effect_bar(b)), etc.)
The per-effect color is preserved (so users don’t lose their visual encoding). An outline in the theme color is added around each glyph in the row.



Single-effect example — a forest where rows are colored by significance:


Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  row_accent   = ~ pval < 0.01,         # accent fill on strong results
  row_emphasis = ~ pval >= 0.01 & pval < 0.05,
  row_muted    = ~ status == "exploratory",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)


Multi-effect example — same row_accent keeps the per-effect colors but adds an outline:


Code
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  row_accent = "is_key",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(effects = list(
      effect_forest("itt_hr", "itt_lo", "itt_hi", color = "#2563eb"),
      effect_forest("pp_hr",  "pp_lo",  "pp_hi",  color = "#16a34a"),
      effect_forest("saf_hr", "saf_lo", "saf_hi", color = "#f59e0b")
    ), scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
)


The same cascade works on viz_bar, viz_boxplot, and viz_violin. See the styling cascade reference for the full 4-layer precedence model and gallery_17–gallery_21 for rendered examples covering each viz type.






TipWhen to use marker_color vs semantic classes




Semantic classes (row_accent, row_emphasis, row_muted) — for “this row is special” intent. Themable: changing the theme automatically restyles markers. Reach for these first.
marker_color = "col" \| ~ formula — for exact-color control (categorical palettes, colorblind-safe encodings, primary colors). Wins over the semantic cascade. Returns NA to fall through to the semantic layer.

title: Row Styling
text:
Expression-Based Styling
Instead of creating style columns in your data, you can use formula expressions that are evaluated on the fly. This is especially useful for conditional styling based on data values:


Code
expr_demo <- data.frame(
  study = c("Alpha 2020", "Beta 2021", "Gamma 2022", "Delta 2023"),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91, 0.68),
  lower = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75, 0.52),
  upper = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10, 0.89),
  pval = c(0.01, 0.1, 0.3, 0.005)
)

tabviz(expr_demo,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log")
  ),
  # Formula expressions - no need for pre-computed columns
  row_bold = ~ pval < 0.05,
  row_color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.05, "#166534", "#64748b"),
  title = "Expression-Based Styling",
  subtitle = "Bold + green color for significant results (p < 0.05)"
)







How It Works

Use the tilde (~) to start a formula expression
The expression is evaluated against your data frame
All column names in your data are available in the expression
Results should be logical (TRUE/FALSE) for boolean styles, or strings for colors



Multiple Conditions
Combine conditions using & (and) or | (or):


Code
# Bold if significant AND large effect
row_bold = ~ pval < 0.05 & hr < 0.8

# Emphasize if significant OR key study
row_emphasis = ~ pval < 0.05 | is_primary

# Color based on effect direction and significance
row_color = ~ case_when(
  hr < 1 & pval < 0.05 ~ "#166534",  # Significant benefit - green
  hr > 1 & pval < 0.05 ~ "#dc2626",  # Significant harm - red
  TRUE ~ "#64748b"                    # Non-significant - gray
)








TipWhen to Use Expressions vs Columns



Use formulas when:

Styling is based on simple conditions (pval < 0.05)
You want concise, readable code
Style logic is tightly coupled to the visualization

Use columns when:

Style values are pre-computed or loaded from data
Same styling is reused across multiple plots
Complex logic is better computed separately

title: Row Styling
text:
Row Types
The row_type parameter controls fundamental row behavior. This is a manual approach for creating structured layouts with headers, summaries, and spacers—useful when you need precise control over the document structure.



Type
Behavior
Marker




"data"
Standard data row
Point with CI line


"header"
Section header, spans full width
None


"summary"
Pooled/subtotal row
Diamond shape


"spacer"
Empty row for separation
None





Code
# glp1_trials has pre-computed row_type column
glp1_trials |>
  filter(group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 null_value = 1, scale = "log")
    ),
    row_type = "row_type"
  )












ImportantNA Values for Non-Data Rows



Rows with row_type = "header" or "spacer" should have NA for point, lower, and upper. Summary rows need valid values to display the diamond.








TipRow Types vs Row Groups



For automatic grouping based on a column in your data, consider using the group parameter instead—it creates collapsible sections without manually specifying row types. See Row Groups.








NoteBuilt-in Dataset



The glp1_trials dataset includes pre-computed row_type and row_bold columns. See Package Data for details.

title: Row Styling
text:
Complete Example: Meta-Analysis Figure
Here’s a publication-ready meta-analysis combining all row styling features:


Code
meta_analysis <- data.frame(
  label = c(
    "Cardiovascular Outcomes",
    "  EMPA-REG 2015", "  CANVAS 2017", "  DECLARE 2019", "  Subtotal", "",
    "Renal Outcomes",
    "  CREDENCE 2019", "  DAPA-CKD 2020", "  Subtotal", "",
    "Overall"
  ),
  hr = c(NA, 0.86, 0.86, 0.93, 0.88, NA, NA, 0.70, 0.61, 0.66, NA, 0.78),
  lower = c(NA, 0.74, 0.75, 0.84, 0.82, NA, NA, 0.59, 0.51, 0.57, NA, 0.73),
  upper = c(NA, 0.99, 0.97, 1.03, 0.95, NA, NA, 0.82, 0.72, 0.76, NA, 0.84),
  n = c(NA, 7020, 10142, 17160, 34322, NA, NA, 4401, 4304, 8705, NA, 43027),
  rtype = c(
    "header", "data", "data", "data", "summary", "spacer",
    "header", "data", "data", "summary", "spacer", "summary"
  ),
  rbold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE),
  rindent = c(0, 1, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0),
  rcolor = c("#0369a1", NA, NA, NA, "#0369a1", NA, "#0369a1", NA, NA, "#0369a1", NA, "#166534"),
  rbadge = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, "n=3", NA, NA, NA, NA, "n=2", NA, "n=5")
)

tabviz(meta_analysis,
  label = "label",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("n", "N"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log", axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_type = "rtype",
  row_bold = "rbold",
  row_indent = "rindent",
  row_color = "rcolor",
  row_badge = "rbadge",
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "SGLT2 Inhibitors Meta-Analysis",
  subtitle = "Cardiovascular and renal outcomes"
)

title: Row Styling
text:
Binding Rules
Not every row-level parameter is anchored the same way. Some attach to the row (stable under column reordering); others render into a specific cell (can re-attach when columns move). Knowing which is which prevents surprises when you use move_column(), add_column(..., after = "__start__"), or remove_column().








Parameter
Bound to
Stable under column reorder?




row_bold, row_italic, row_color, row_bg
Row CSS class
✅ Yes


row_emphasis, row_muted, row_accent, row_bold, row_fill
Row CSS class (theme-aware)
✅ Yes


row_indent
Row CSS class (applies to label column)
✅ Yes


row_type ("data", "header", "summary", "spacer")
Row CSS class
✅ Yes


row_badge, row_icon
First visible text column’s cell
⚠️ No — re-attaches if first column changes


marker_color, marker_shape, marker_size, marker_opacity
SVG marker on the forest column
✅ Yes


set_row_label() edits (proxy)
Label column
⚠️ Lost if label column is removed




Why badge and icon are special
Badges and icons visually extend the row label — they always need a specific cell to anchor inside. rather than hard-coding them to a named column, tabviz resolves their target at serialization time by picking the first visible text column. This keeps the API simple (no extra column = argument on set_row_style()) at the cost of quiet coupling to column order.
In practice:

Keep your label column in position 1. This is the common case and nothing will surprise you.
If you call move_column("tier", to = 1) or add_column(col_icon("flag"), after = "__start__"), expect badges/icons to re-render on the new leftmost column.
If you rebuild a plot via update_data() with a WebSpec whose columns are in a different order, the same re-anchoring applies.



Stable anchoring
If you need a badge that stays with a specific column regardless of layout, set badge = "col" directly on the target column constructor instead of using row_badge. Column-level badges are bound to their column and unaffected by row-level re-anchoring.


Code
# Row-level: anchors to the leftmost visible column (may move)
tabviz(data, label = "study", row_badge = "sig", columns = list(...))

# Column-level: anchors to the "study" column (stable)
tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(
  col_text("study", badge = "sig"),
  ...
))

title: Row Styling
text:
Parameter Reference
All row_* parameters accept either a column name (string) or a formula expression (~ ...).








Parameter
Column Contains
Description




row_bold
TRUE/FALSE
Bold text


row_italic
TRUE/FALSE
Italic text


row_color
CSS color (e.g., "#1a365d")
Text color


row_bg
CSS color
Background color


row_indent
Integer (0, 1, 2, …)
Indentation level


row_badge
Text
Badge label


row_icon
Emoji/unicode
Icon before label


row_emphasis
TRUE/FALSE
Bold + theme primary text/marker


row_muted
TRUE/FALSE
Lighter color, reduced prominence


row_accent
TRUE/FALSE
Bold + theme accent text/marker


row_bold (semantic)
TRUE/FALSE
Weight bump only, no color change


row_fill
TRUE/FALSE
Bold + strong row fill (auto-contrasting fg)


row_type
"data", "header", "summary", "spacer"
Row behavior and marker type









NoteFormula Examples



# Using column name
row_bold = "is_significant"

# Using formula expression
row_bold = ~ pval < 0.05

title: Row Styling
text:
See Also

Row Groups — Automatic collapsible sections from column values
Visualization Styling — How row_accent / row_emphasis / row_muted cascade into viz cells
Fluent API — Pipe-based equivalents (set_row_style()) for programmatic workflows
Styling Overview — Where this layer fits in the cascade
tabviz() — All arguments

title: Columns
text: Columns are the unit of display in tabviz. You compose a table by passing a list of column specifications to tabviz(columns = ...). Each column helper (col_*(), viz_*()) picks a display type and exposes formatting knobs; the underlying web_col() constructor is there if you need something unusual.

title: Columns
text:
Universal arguments
Every col_*() helper accepts the same arguments for layout and styling. These behave identically regardless of column type.



Argument
Default
Purpose




Primary data (see per-helper table)
required
Which column(s) in data provide values


header
NULL (auto)
Column header text; defaults to the field name or a type-specific default


width
NULL (auto)
Column width in pixels


align
"left" (numeric types default to "right")
Body cell alignment: "left", "center", "right"


header_align
follows align
Header alignment; override to decouple


show_header
auto
TRUE/FALSE to force; NULL shows when header is non-empty


wrap
FALSE
Multi-line cell text. FALSE/0 = single line (ellipsis on overflow). TRUE/1 = up to 2 lines (1 extra). Integer n = up to n + 1 lines. Author \n is honoured first, then long segments word-wrap within the column width.


sortable
TRUE
Allow sort on header click


na_text
NULL
Text to display for NA/missing values (default is blank)


tooltip
NULL
Column whose values become per-cell hover text


formatter
NULL
R function function(x) ... applied to values; output rendered as text. See Custom formatters below.


bold, italic, color, bg, badge, icon, emphasis, muted, accent
NULL
Per-cell styling driven by another column — see Cell Styling









TipEverything routes through web_col()



The col_*() helpers are thin, opinionated wrappers over web_col(). When a helper doesn’t cover your case (custom type, non-standard option), call web_col() directly.

title: Columns
text:
Catalog at a glance








Helper
Use for
Type-specific arguments




col_text(field)
Plain text, categoricals
max_chars


col_label(field)
Row-identifier column
max_chars


col_numeric(field)
General numbers
decimals, digits, thousands_sep, abbreviate


col_n(field)
Sample sizes
decimals, digits, thousands_sep, abbreviate


col_percent(field)
Proportions / percentages
decimals, digits, multiply, symbol


col_currency(field)
Money
symbol, position, decimals, digits, thousands_sep, abbreviate


col_pvalue(field)
P-values
stars, thresholds, format, digits, exp_threshold, abbrev_threshold


col_interval(point, lower, upper)
Point estimate + CI
decimals, separator, imprecise_threshold


col_events(events, n)
Events / N ratio
separator, show_pct, thousands_sep, abbreviate


col_range(low, high)
Low – High range
separator, decimals, abbreviate


col_date(field)
Dates
format


col_bar(field)
Inline magnitude bar (no axis)
max_value, show_label, color, scale


col_sparkline(field)
Trend charts
type, height, color


col_heatmap(field)
Color-scale values
palette, min_value, max_value, decimals, show_value, scale


col_progress(field)
Percentage progress bar
max_value, show_label, color, scale


col_icon(field)
Icons / emoji
mapping, size, color


col_badge(field)
Status chips
variants, colors, size


col_stars(field)
Rating (1–N stars)
max_stars, color, empty_color, half_stars, min_value, max_value, size


col_pictogram(field)
Glyph counts or rating piles
glyph, glyph_field, max_glyphs, half_glyphs, min_value, max_value, color, empty_color, size, layout, value_label, label_format, label_decimals


col_ring(field)
Donut gauge with optional thresholds
min_value, max_value, color, thresholds, track_color, size, show_label, label_format, label_decimals


col_img(field)
Inline images
height, max_width, fallback, shape


col_reference(field)
Truncated text + optional link
href_field, max_chars, show_icon


col_group(header, ...)
Group child columns under one header
—



For the four axis-backed visualization columns (viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin), see Visualizations.

title: Columns
text:
Composing columns
A tabviz() call takes a list of column specs in display order. The special label argument produces an implicit leftmost identifier column; you can also declare it explicitly with col_label() if you want finer control.


Code
data(glp1_trials)

meta <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(4)

tabviz(meta,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    col_events("events", "n"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
  )
)

title: Columns
text:
Headers and widths
Headers default to the field name if omitted; pass a second positional argument (or named header) to override, and an empty string or show_header = FALSE to hide.
Widths are auto-sized by default. Pass a pixel value to fix a column.


Code
col_text("drug")                          # header: "drug", auto width
col_text("drug", "Drug")                  # header: "Drug"
col_text("drug", header = "", width = 80) # no header, 80px wide
col_text("drug", show_header = FALSE)     # header cell hidden

title: Columns
text:
Alignment
col_* helpers that render numeric content right-align by default (col_numeric, col_n, col_percent, col_currency, col_pvalue, col_range). Everything else left-aligns. Override with align; header alignment follows the body unless you set header_align explicitly.


Code
col_numeric("value", align = "left")                      # force body left
col_numeric("value", header_align = "center")             # center header only

title: Columns
text:
Numeric formatting
The numeric formatting vocabulary is shared across col_numeric, col_n, col_percent, col_currency, col_pvalue, col_heatmap, col_events, and col_interval. Use whichever helper fits your data — you get the same formatting primitives.







Argument
Meaning




decimals
Fixed number of decimal places. Mutually exclusive with digits.


digits
Significant figures. Mutually exclusive with decimals.


thousands_sep
Separator string (",", " ", ".", …) or FALSE to disable


abbreviate
TRUE to shorten large numbers: 1,234 → "1.2K", 2.5e6 → "2.5M"





Code
options_data <- data.frame(
  item  = c("Small", "Medium", "Large"),
  n     = c(125, 4520, 1250000),
  rate  = c(0.156, 0.892, 0.034),
  value = c(1.234, 12.34, 123.4)
)

tabviz(options_data, label = "item",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n",                              "Count"),
    col_n("n",        abbreviate = TRUE,    "Abbreviated", id = "n_abbr"),
    col_numeric("value", decimals = 2,      "2 decimals"),
    col_numeric("value", digits   = 3,      "3 sig figs",   id = "value_sigfig"),
    col_percent("rate",                     "Percent")
  )
)












NoteDecimals vs. digits



decimals = 2 always prints 2 places (0.10, 3.14, 125.00). digits = 3 prints 3 meaningful figures (0.156, 3.14, 125). Use digits when values span orders of magnitude.

title: Columns
text:
Handling missing data
Set na_text on any column to replace NA cells with custom text.


Code
col_numeric("response", na_text = "—")
col_pvalue("pvalue", na_text = "n/a")
col_events("events", "n", na_text = "—")


The default is a blank cell.

title: Columns
text:
Per-cell tooltips
Pass tooltip = "<col>" to any column helper to drive the cell’s hover-text from another column in the data. This overrides the default behavior of using the cell’s own value as the tooltip.


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("status", tooltip = "status_explanation"),  # custom hover text
    col_n("n", tooltip = "n_source")                      # e.g. "Per-protocol N"
  )
)


tooltip accepts a column name; the value at that cell becomes the rendered title attribute.

title: Columns
text:
Custom formatters
When the built-in formatting vocabulary doesn’t fit (decimals/digits/abbreviate/thousands_sep), pass a formatter function. The function is applied to the column’s values at render time and the output is treated as text — the column is rendered verbatim.


Code
data <- data.frame(
  study = c("A", "B", "C"),
  estimate = c(1.234, 0.85, 2.4),
  se       = c(0.12, 0.08, 0.31)
)

tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    web_col("estimate",
      formatter = function(x) sprintf("%.2f ± %.2f", x, data$se))
  )
)


The formatter receives the raw column vector; return a character vector of the same length. Use this for unit suffixes, conditional formatting, or composite values that don’t fit any single col_*() helper.






NoteWhen to reach for formatter



For routine formatting (decimals, sig figs, percentages, currencies, abbreviations), prefer the built-in arguments — they’re discoverable, declarative, and fast. Reach for formatter only when the desired output can’t be expressed via the built-in vocabulary.

title: Columns
Columns
text:
Column groups
col_group(header, ...) wraps a set of columns under a shared header row — useful for multiple outcomes, time points, or analyses.


Code
outcomes <- data.frame(
  study          = c("PARADIGM-HF", "DAPA-HF", "EMPEROR-Reduced"),
  primary_hr     = c(0.80, 0.74, 0.75),
  primary_lower  = c(0.73, 0.65, 0.65),
  primary_upper  = c(0.87, 0.85, 0.86),
  primary_p      = c(0.001, 0.001, 0.001),
  secondary_hr   = c(0.84, 0.70, 0.69),
  secondary_p    = c(0.01, 0.001, 0.001)
)

tabviz(outcomes, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "primary_hr", lower = "primary_lower",
               upper = "primary_upper", null_value = 1, scale = "log"),
    col_group("Primary",
      col_interval("primary_hr", "primary_lower", "primary_upper",
                   header = "HR (95% CI)"),
      col_pvalue("primary_p", "P")
    ),
    col_group("Secondary",
      col_numeric("secondary_hr", "HR", decimals = 2),
      col_pvalue("secondary_p", "P")
    )
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm()
)

title: Columns
text:
Visualization columns
