Four viz_*() columns render axis-backed plots inline: viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin. They share axis-control arguments (axis_range, axis_ticks, axis_gridlines, axis_label, scale) and are documented separately:

Forest plots — point + CI with annotations
Bar charts — horizontal bars with an axis
Boxplots — quartiles, median, outliers
Violin plots — kernel-density distributions

Unlike col_bar, the viz columns share a consistent axis across rows.
All four viz columns honor the marker-styling cascade: row semantic classes (row_accent/row_emphasis/row_muted) and per-row marker_color from tabviz() reach into the viz cell automatically. Single-effect viz columns get a fill replacement; multi-effect columns get an outline that preserves per-effect color identity. See Row Styling → Cascading into viz cells for the full picture.

title: Columns
text:
Per-helper detail

Text and identifiers

col_text() and col_label()
Plain text with optional truncation. col_label() is the convenience helper for the row-identifier column; it also prettifies the field name into a Title Case header by default.


Code
col_text("drug")
col_text("notes", "Notes", max_chars = 50)      # truncate with ellipsis
col_text("description", wrap = TRUE, width = 220)   # wrap (up to 2 lines)
col_text("notes", wrap = 3, width = 200)            # up to 4 lines

col_label("study_id")                           # header auto: "Study Id"
col_label("study_id", header = "Study")          # explicit header





Numbers

col_numeric()
General-purpose numeric column with decimals / digits / thousands_sep / abbreviate.


Code
col_numeric("estimate")                          # 2 decimals
col_numeric("count", decimals = 0,
            thousands_sep = ",")                 # "12,345"
col_numeric("value", digits = 3)                 # 3 sig figs
col_numeric("population", abbreviate = TRUE)     # "1.2M"




col_n()
Convenience wrapper for integer counts: defaults to decimals = 0, thousands_sep = ",", header "N".


Code
col_n("n")
col_n("enrolled", header = "Enrolled", abbreviate = TRUE)




col_percent()
Formats proportions or percentages.








Arg
Default
Effect




multiply
TRUE
TRUE when input is [0, 1] (multiply by 100); set FALSE if your data is already on the 0–100 scale


symbol
TRUE
Append % to the formatted number





Code
col_percent("rate")                           # 0.05 → "5.0%"
col_percent("accuracy", multiply = FALSE)     # data is already 0–100
col_percent("share", symbol = FALSE)          # drop the "%"




col_currency()
Money with currency symbol prefix/suffix.








Arg
Default
Effect




symbol
"$"
Currency glyph (e.g. "€", "¥", "R$")


position
"prefix"
"suffix" for post-fixed currencies


thousands_sep
","
String or FALSE; European users often pass "."


abbreviate
FALSE
TRUE for "$1.2M"-style short form





Code
col_currency("price")
col_currency("amount", symbol = "€", thousands_sep = ".", position = "suffix")
col_currency("revenue", abbreviate = TRUE)    # "$1.2M"




col_pvalue()
P-values with smart formatting and optional significance stars.








Arg
Default
Effect




stars
FALSE
Append */**/*** based on thresholds


thresholds
c(0.05, 0.01, 0.001)
Star cutoffs


format
"auto"
"auto" / "scientific" / "decimal"


digits
2
Significant figures


exp_threshold
0.001
Below this, switch to scientific notation


abbrev_threshold
NULL
Below this, render as e.g. "<0.0001"





Code
col_pvalue("pval")
col_pvalue("pval", stars = TRUE)
col_pvalue("pval", abbrev_threshold = 0.0001)





Paired values

col_interval()
Renders a point estimate with its confidence interval: 0.85 (0.70, 1.03).








Arg
Default
Effect




point, lower, upper
required
Field names


decimals
2
Decimal places


separator
" "
Separator between point and CI block


imprecise_threshold
NULL
If upper/lower > threshold, render "—" instead





Code
col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
col_interval("or", "or_lo", "or_hi", decimals = 3, separator = ", ")
col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", imprecise_threshold = 10)




col_events()
Events / N ratio, optionally with percentage.


Code
col_events("events", "n")                           # "45/120"
col_events("events", "n", show_pct = TRUE)          # "45/120 (37.5%)"
col_events("events", "n", separator = " of ",
           abbreviate = TRUE)                       # "1.1K of 12K"




col_range()
Low – high range, optionally with an inline bar.


Code
col_range("age_min", "age_max", "Age")
col_range("ci_lower", "ci_upper", decimals = 1)
col_range("low_price", "high_price", digits = 3)





Dates

col_date()
Dates formatted with strftime-style tokens. R-side formatting, so the browser sees pre-formatted strings.


Code
col_date("enrollment_date", format = "%Y-%m-%d")
col_date("published",       format = "%b %Y")          # "Jan 2024"
col_date("visit",           format = "%A, %d %B %Y")    # full





Inline visuals

col_bar()
Small horizontal bar inside the cell — use for visual weight without a shared axis. For axis-backed bars across rows, reach for viz_bar().


Code
col_bar("weight")
col_bar("score", max_value = 100, color = "#2563eb")
col_bar("value", scale = "log")




col_sparkline()
Mini line/area/bar chart per row. Requires a list-column of numeric vectors.


Code
col_sparkline("trend")                          # default: line
col_sparkline("trend", type = "area")
col_sparkline("trend", type = "bar", height = 30, color = "#16a34a")








NotePreparing sparkline data



Create a list-column with mutate(trend = list(c(1, 2, 3))) or tibble(trend = I(list(...))). Vectors do not need to be the same length across rows.




col_heatmap()
Color-scale cell background with optional numeric overlay.


Code
col_heatmap("score")                                   # default palette
col_heatmap("score", palette = c("#fef3c7", "#92400e"),
            min_value = 0, max_value = 100)
col_heatmap("magnitude", scale = "log", show_value = FALSE)




col_progress()
Horizontal progress bar with numeric label.


Code
col_progress("completion", max_value = 100)
col_progress("share", max_value = 1, show_label = FALSE)
col_progress("fill", color = "#8b5cf6", height = 6)





Status and categories

col_badge()
Colored chips keyed to row values.







Arg
Effect




variants
Named list mapping values → semantic color ("success", "warning", "error", "info", "muted")


colors
Named list mapping values → literal CSS colors (overrides variants)


size
"base" or "sm"





Code
col_badge("status")                              # plain chips
col_badge("status", variants = list(
  Published = "success", Preprint = "warning", Retracted = "error"
))
col_badge("priority", colors = list(
  high = "#dc2626", medium = "#f59e0b", low = "#22c55e"
))




col_icon()
Icons, emoji, or unicode glyphs.


Code
col_icon("status_icon")                          # emoji already in data
col_icon("result", mapping = list(
  pass = "✔", fail = "✖", pending = "…"
))
col_icon("flag", size = "lg", color = "#16a34a")




col_stars()
Visual rating (filled/empty stars).


Code
col_stars("quality")                              # 1–5 default
col_stars("quality", max_stars = 10, half_stars = TRUE)
col_stars("score_0_100", min_value = 0, max_value = 100)   # remap to star count
col_stars("rating", size = "lg")                            # larger glyphs




col_pictogram()
Repeated glyphs as either a count (render round(value) glyphs) or a rating (fill value of max_glyphs). Useful for “isotype”-style displays — three persons for n = 3, four-of-five skulls for a risk score.







Arg
Effect




glyph
Glyph name ("person", "skull", "leaf", "flame", "coin", …) or a named character vector for per-row glyph selection (paired with glyph_field)


glyph_field
Column name whose value picks the key in a named glyph vector


max_glyphs
NULL → count mode; integer → rating mode


half_glyphs
Allow half-fill on the boundary glyph


min_value / max_value
Remap input range into [0, max_glyphs] (rating mode)


color / empty_color
Filled / unfilled glyph colors (defaults follow the active theme)


size
"sm" / "base" / "lg"


layout
"row" (default) or "stack" — vertical pile, best in tall rows


value_label
FALSE / TRUE (= "trailing") / "leading" / "trailing"


label_format, label_decimals
"integer" or "decimal"; decimal places when decimal





Code
# Count mode — three persons for n = 3
col_pictogram("score", glyph = "person", value_label = TRUE)

# Rating mode — three of five skulls
col_pictogram("perf", glyph = "skull", max_glyphs = 5,
              color = "#f97316", empty_color = "#3f3f46")

# Per-row glyph selection — leaf on "low" rows, flame on "high"
col_pictogram("count", glyph = c("low" = "leaf", "high" = "flame"),
              glyph_field = "category")

# Vertical stack with trailing label (use in tall rows next to a viz column)
col_pictogram("eaten", glyph = "coin", layout = "stack",
              value_label = "trailing")




col_ring()
Donut gauge — fills (value - min_value) / (max_value - min_value) of a circle, with optional threshold-driven color shifts. Pairs well with proportional or score-style data.







Arg
Effect




min_value / max_value
Input range (default 0 / 1 — fraction)


color
Filled arc color. Single color, or character vector of length length(thresholds) + 1


thresholds
Numeric breakpoints (raw input units) where arc color shifts


track_color
Unfilled track color (default theme muted)


size
"sm" (~18 px) / "base" (~24 px) / "lg" (~32 px)


show_label
Show numeric value to the right of the ring


label_format, label_decimals
"percent" (default) / "decimal" / "integer"





Code
# Single-color ring, value as fraction [0, 1]
col_ring("temptation", color = "#d4a955")

# Two-color threshold (gold below 50%, red above)
col_ring("temptation",
         color = c("#d4a955", "#a855f7"), thresholds = 0.5)

# Three-color status: positive / warning / negative (auto-defaulted colors)
col_ring("risk", thresholds = c(0.33, 0.66))

# Integer score on [0, 100]
col_ring("score", min_value = 0, max_value = 100,
         label_format = "integer")





Media and links

col_img()
Inline image cells. Images come from URL/path in the data column.


Code
col_img("logo_url")
col_img("avatar", shape = "circle", width = 40)
col_img("thumbnail", fallback = "No image")




col_reference()
Truncated text (e.g. citation) with optional clickable link and external-link icon.


Code
col_reference("citation")
col_reference("title", href_field = "doi_url",
              max_chars = 40)
col_reference("source", href_field = "url", show_icon = FALSE)





Grouping

col_group()
Wraps child columns under a shared header row. See Composing columns above.

title: Columns
text:
See also

Cell Styling — conditional formatting, formula pronouns
Row Styling — row-level bold/italic/color/bg/badge/icon
Visualizations — viz_*() axis-backed columns
web_col() reference — the low-level constructor

title: Bar Charts
text: viz_bar() creates horizontal bar charts that share a common axis across rows so the eye can compare magnitudes directly. Use it for counts, scores, percentages, or any single-value-per-row metric.

title: Bar Charts
text:
Quick example


Code
bar_data <- data.frame(
  metric = c("Response Rate", "Completion Rate", "Retention Rate", "Satisfaction"),
  value  = c(78, 92, 85, 88),
  category = c("Primary", "Primary", "Secondary", "Secondary")
)

tabviz(bar_data, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    col_text("category", "Category"),
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("value"),
      header = "Rate (%)",
      width  = 200,
      axis_range = c(0, 100)
    ),
    col_numeric("value", "%", width = 60)
  ),
  title = "Performance Metrics"
)

title: Bar Charts
text:
Multiple effects (grouped bars)
Pass several effect_bar() items to compare values per row with grouped bars:


Code
comparison_data <- data.frame(
  region   = c("North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific", "Latin America"),
  q1_sales = c(245, 189, 312, 98),
  q2_sales = c(278, 215, 345, 125),
  q3_sales = c(312, 242, 398, 156)
)

tabviz(comparison_data, label = "region",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("q1_sales", label = "Q1", color = "#3b82f6"),
      effect_bar("q2_sales", label = "Q2", color = "#22c55e"),
      effect_bar("q3_sales", label = "Q3", color = "#f59e0b"),
      header = "Quarterly Sales ($K)",
      width  = 250
    )
  ),
  title = "Regional Sales Comparison"
)






When you don’t pass per-effect colors, the theme’s series_anchors fill them automatically — switching themes restyles every bar. See Visualization Styling — Layer 1 for the full palette flow.

title: Bar Charts
text:
viz_bar() arguments



Argument
Default
Purpose




...
required
One or more effect_bar() items, optionally followed by named cell-styling args (see below)


header
NULL
Column header. NULL resolves to the primary effect’s label / field name


show_header
TRUE
Render the header cell above the chart


header_align
"center"
"left" / "center" / "right"


width
NULL (auto)
Column width in pixels


scale
"linear"
"linear" or "log" (log requires all-positive values)


axis_range
auto
Numeric c(min, max); auto-computed when NULL


axis_ticks
auto
Numeric vector of explicit tick positions


axis_gridlines
FALSE
Show vertical gridlines at tick positions


axis_label
"Value"
Label rendered below the axis


show_axis
TRUE
Hide the axis with FALSE for compact dashboard tiles


null_value
NULL
Optional reference value — paints a dashed line at this position (a synthetic refline())


annotations
NULL
List of refline() items for additional reference lines (forest_annotation() is forest-specific and ignored here)









NoteCell-styling passthrough



Named arguments that follow the effect_bar() items are forwarded to web_col(): bold, italic, color, bg, badge, icon, emphasis, muted, accent. Cell-level args that don’t make sense for a graphical column (na_text, tooltip, formatter) are silently ignored — rows with no valid data are skipped, not text-filled.

title: Bar Charts
text:
effect_bar() arguments








Argument
Default
Purpose




value
required
Column name containing the bar value


label
NULL
Display label (used in legends and as the resolved column header for single-effect viz)


color
NULL
Bar fill color (overrides the theme palette for this effect)


opacity
NULL
Bar opacity in [0, 1]

title: Bar Charts
text:
Axis control
Every axis option lives on viz_bar() directly. Set them per column.

Fixed range


Code
tabviz(bar_data, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("value"),
            header = "Rate (%)",
            axis_range = c(0, 100))
  ),
  title = "Axis pinned to 0–100"
)








Custom ticks and gridlines


Code
tabviz(bar_data, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("value"),
            header = "Rate (%)",
            axis_range = c(0, 100),
            axis_ticks = c(0, 25, 50, 75, 100),
            axis_gridlines = TRUE)
  ),
  title = "Custom ticks + gridlines"
)








Log scale
scale = "log" requires every bar value to be positive. Useful for population counts, dollar magnitudes, or any quantity that spans orders of magnitude.


Code
log_data <- data.frame(
  city = c("Tokyo", "Delhi", "Shanghai", "São Paulo", "Manila"),
  pop  = c(37e6, 32e6, 28e6, 22e6, 14e6)
)

tabviz(log_data, label = "city",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("pop"), header = "Population",
            scale = "log",
            axis_label = "Metro population (log scale)")
  )
)








Hide the axis for dense dashboards


Code
mini <- bar_data |> head(3)

tabviz(mini, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("value"),
            header = "%",
            show_axis = FALSE,
            width = 120,
            axis_range = c(0, 100))
  )
)

title: Bar Charts
text:
Reference lines
Use null_value for a single dashed line at a baseline / target / threshold. For multiple lines, pass a list of refline() items via annotations.


Code
target_data <- data.frame(
  team = c("Alpha", "Bravo", "Charlie", "Delta"),
  score = c(72, 88, 65, 91)
)

tabviz(target_data, label = "team",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("score"),
            header = "Score",
            axis_range = c(0, 100),
            null_value = 80)
  ),
  title = "null_value = 80 — target line"
)








Code
tabviz(target_data, label = "team",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("score"),
            header = "Score",
            axis_range = c(0, 100),
            annotations = list(
              refline(60, label = "Floor",  style = "dashed", color = "#dc2626"),
              refline(80, label = "Target", style = "solid",  color = "#16a34a"),
              refline(95, label = "Stretch", style = "dotted", color = "#0369a1")
            ))
  ),
  title = "Custom annotation set"
)

title: Bar Charts
text:
Styling cascade
Bar colors resolve through the same four-layer cascade as every other viz_*() column — the theme palette is the default, per-effect color = "..." pins a series identity, semantic row tokens (row_accent, row_emphasis, row_muted, row_bold, row_fill) reach into bars on tagged rows, and per-row marker_color wins for exact pixel control. See Visualization Styling for the full cascade with rendered examples.


Code
tabviz(target_data, label = "team",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(effect_bar("score"),
            header = "Score",
            axis_range = c(0, 100),
            null_value = 80)
  ),
  row_accent = ~ score >= 80,
  title = "row_accent reaches into bars",
  subtitle = "Teams ≥ 80 take the theme accent fill"
)

title: Bar Charts
text:
When to reach for what

viz_bar() when you need a focal axis-backed comparison across rows (counts, scores, percentages).
col_bar() (Columns) when you only need an inline weight indicator inside a cell — no shared axis, no scale, fits next to a label.
viz_boxplot() / viz_violin() when you have a distribution per row, not a single value.
viz_forest() when you have point + lower + upper (confidence intervals).

title: Bar Charts
text:
See also

Visualization Styling — The 4-layer color cascade across all viz types
Visualizations Overview — Tooltips, export, shared features
Columns — col_bar() — Inline cell-level bars
Forest Plots — Confidence intervals
Boxplots / Violin Plots — Distribution viz

title: Violin Plots
text: viz_violin() shows the full distribution shape using kernel density estimation. Use it when distribution shape matters — bimodality, skewness, asymmetry — rather than just summary statistics.

title: Violin Plots
text:
Basic Usage


Code
set.seed(456)
violin_data <- data.frame(
  group = c("Young Adults", "Middle Age", "Seniors"),
  scores = I(list(
    c(rnorm(80, 72, 8), rnorm(20, 90, 5)),     # bimodal
    rnorm(100, 68, 12),                          # normal
    c(rnorm(70, 60, 10), runif(30, 40, 80))     # skewed
  )),
  n = c(100, 100, 100)
)

tabviz(violin_data, label = "group",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_violin(
      effect_violin(data = "scores", color = "#f59e0b"),
      header = "Score Distribution",
      width = 250,
      show_median = TRUE,
      show_quartiles = TRUE
    )
  ),
  title = "Score Distributions by Age Group"
)

title: Violin Plots
text:
Comparing Distributions
Use multiple effect_violin() items to overlay distributions:


Code
set.seed(789)
compare_violin <- data.frame(
  condition = c("Healthy", "Mild", "Moderate", "Severe"),
  biomarker_a = I(list(
    rnorm(60, 25, 5),
    rnorm(60, 35, 8),
    rnorm(60, 50, 12),
    rnorm(60, 70, 15)
  )),
  biomarker_b = I(list(
    rnorm(60, 30, 6),
    rnorm(60, 40, 7),
    rnorm(60, 55, 10),
    rnorm(60, 65, 18)
  ))
)

tabviz(compare_violin, label = "condition",
  columns = list(
    viz_violin(
      effect_violin(data = "biomarker_a", label = "Biomarker A", color = "#3b82f6"),
      effect_violin(data = "biomarker_b", label = "Biomarker B", color = "#ef4444"),
      header = "Biomarker Distributions",
      width = 280,
      show_median = TRUE
    )
  ),
  title = "Biomarker Profiles by Disease Severity"
)

title: Violin Plots
text:
viz_violin() Arguments



Argument
Default
Purpose




header
NULL
Column header text


width
NULL (auto)
Column width in pixels


scale
"linear"
"linear" or "log"


axis_range
auto
Fixed axis range c(min, max)


axis_ticks
auto
Custom tick positions


axis_gridlines
FALSE
Show vertical gridlines


bandwidth
NULL
KDE bandwidth (NULL uses Silverman’s rule of thumb)


show_median
TRUE
Show the median line


show_quartiles
FALSE
Show Q1/Q3 dashed lines


axis_label
"Value"
Label text below the axis


show_axis
TRUE
Show the axis


null_value
NULL
Optional reference value — paints a dashed line at this position


annotations
NULL
List of refline() items

title: Violin Plots
text:
effect_violin() Arguments



Argument
Purpose




data
Column containing array of values (required)


label
Display label for legend


color
Violin fill color


opacity
Fill opacity (default 0.5)

title: Violin Plots
text:
See Also

Visualization Styling — How row_accent / marker_color cascade into violin fills
Visualizations Overview — Tooltips, export, common features
Bar Charts — Single values with viz_bar()
Boxplots — Distribution summaries with viz_boxplot()

title: Boxplots
text: viz_boxplot() summarizes distributions with median, quartiles, and outliers. It accepts either raw arrays of values or pre-computed summary statistics.

title: Boxplots
text:
Mode 1 — From Array Data
When each row contains an array of values, boxplot statistics are computed automatically:


Code
set.seed(42)
boxplot_data <- data.frame(
  treatment = c("Control", "Low Dose", "Medium Dose", "High Dose"),
  response = I(list(
    rnorm(50, mean = 100, sd = 15),
    rnorm(50, mean = 110, sd = 18),
    rnorm(50, mean = 125, sd = 20),
    rnorm(50, mean = 140, sd = 25)
  )),
  n = c(50, 50, 50, 50)
)

tabviz(boxplot_data, label = "treatment",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_boxplot(
      effect_boxplot(data = "response", color = "#8b5cf6"),
      header = "Response Distribution",
      width = 250,
      show_outliers = TRUE
    )
  ),
  title = "Dose-Response Distribution"
)

title: Boxplots
text:
Mode 2 — From Pre-computed Statistics
If you already have quartile statistics, map them directly:


Code
summary_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("Study A", "Study B", "Study C", "Study D"),
  min = c(45, 52, 38, 60),
  q1 = c(62, 68, 55, 72),
  median = c(78, 82, 71, 85),
  q3 = c(89, 94, 86, 95),
  max = c(105, 112, 98, 108)
)

tabviz(summary_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_boxplot(
      effect_boxplot(
        min = "min", q1 = "q1", median = "median", q3 = "q3", max = "max",
        color = "#22c55e"
      ),
      header = "Score Distribution",
      width = 220
    ),
    col_numeric("median", "Median")
  ),
  title = "Study Score Distributions"
)

title: Boxplots
text:
Multiple Effects
Compare distributions across conditions with multiple effect_boxplot() items:


Code
set.seed(123)
multi_box_data <- data.frame(
  timepoint = c("Baseline", "Week 4", "Week 8", "Week 12"),
  placebo = I(list(
    rnorm(40, 50, 12), rnorm(40, 52, 13), rnorm(40, 51, 14), rnorm(40, 53, 12)
  )),
  treatment = I(list(
    rnorm(40, 50, 12), rnorm(40, 62, 14), rnorm(40, 70, 15), rnorm(40, 75, 13)
  ))
)

tabviz(multi_box_data, label = "timepoint",
  columns = list(
    viz_boxplot(
      effect_boxplot(data = "placebo", label = "Placebo", color = "#94a3b8"),
      effect_boxplot(data = "treatment", label = "Treatment", color = "#3b82f6"),
      header = "Score by Treatment Arm",
      width = 280,
      show_outliers = TRUE
    )
  ),
  title = "Treatment Effect Over Time"
)

title: Boxplots
text:
viz_boxplot() Arguments



Argument
Default
Purpose




header
NULL
Column header text


width
NULL (auto)
Column width in pixels


scale
"linear"
"linear" or "log"


axis_range
auto
Fixed axis range c(min, max)


axis_ticks
auto
Custom tick positions


axis_gridlines
FALSE
Show vertical gridlines


show_outliers
TRUE
Display outlier points


axis_label
"Value"
Label text below the axis


show_axis
TRUE
Show the axis


null_value
NULL
Optional reference value — paints a dashed line at this position


annotations
NULL
List of refline() items

title: Boxplots
text:
effect_boxplot() Arguments







Argument
Purpose




data
Column containing array of values (mode 1)


min, q1, median, q3, max
Column names for pre-computed stats (mode 2)


outliers
Column containing outlier values (optional, mode 2)


label
Display label for legend


color
Box fill color


opacity
Box fill opacity (default 0.7)

title: Boxplots
text:
See Also

Visualization Styling — How row_accent / marker_color cascade into box fills
Visualizations Overview — Tooltips, export, common features
Bar Charts — Single values with viz_bar()
Violin Plots — Full distributions with viz_violin()

title: Quick Start
text: Build your first interactive forest plot in about 30 lines, then layer in styling, themes, and conditional formatting.

title: Quick Start
text:
Installation
# Install from GitHub using pak
install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("kaskarn/tabviz")






NoteKey concepts




Label column: the row identifier (study name, category, item).
Column types: col_*() for data columns, viz_*() for visualizations.
Row types: "data", "header", "summary", "spacer".
Column mapping: pass column names as strings; values are computed per row.

title: Quick Start
text:
Your first table


Code
data(glp1_trials)

meta_data <- glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5)

tabviz(meta_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  )
)












TipBuilt-in datasets



This example uses glp1_trials, one of five datasets shipped with the package. See Package Data for all available datasets.

title: Quick Start
text:
The core pattern
tabviz(data,
  label = "label_column",    # row labels
  columns = list(...)        # what columns to show
)
Each col_*() function defines a column. The most common helpers:








Function
Purpose
Example




col_text()
Text
col_text("category")


col_numeric()
Numbers
col_numeric("value", decimals = 1)


col_n()
Sample sizes
col_n("n")


viz_forest()
Forest plot
viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")


col_interval()
Point (CI)
col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR")


col_pvalue()
P-values
col_pvalue("pval")



See Columns for the full catalog.

title: Quick Start
text:
Adding more columns


Code
tabviz(meta_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("drug", "Drug"),
    col_n("n"),
    col_numeric("events", header = "Events"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  )
)

title: Quick Start
text:
Applying themes
tabviz ships with nine curated presets, each owning a distinct expressive axis — from the restrained clinical default to fully illustrative looks:

Code
tabviz(meta_data[1:3, ],
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(), title = "nejm"
)
tabviz(meta_data[1:3, ],
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_ledger(), title = "ledger"
)













The nine presets: web_theme_nejm() (default), web_theme_ledger(), web_theme_brutalist(), web_theme_aurora(), web_theme_terminal(), web_theme_newsprint(), web_theme_blueprint(), web_theme_synthwave(), web_theme_dwarven().
See Themes for customization, the set_*() modifier family, and how to register your own preset.

title: Quick Start
text:
Row styling
Build visual hierarchy with row types and styling:


Code
styled_data <- data.frame(
  label = c("Primary Endpoint", "  CV Death", "  MI", "Summary"),
  hr = c(NA, 0.82, 0.79, 0.78),
  lower = c(NA, 0.72, 0.68, 0.65),
  upper = c(NA, 0.94, 0.92, 0.93),
  rtype = c("header", "data", "data", "summary"),
  rbold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

tabviz(styled_data,
  label = "label",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_type = "rtype",
  row_bold = "rbold"
)












NoteRow types






Type
Behavior




"data"
Standard row with interval marker


"header"
Section header, no marker


"summary"
Summary row with diamond marker


"spacer"
Empty row for separation



Header and spacer rows should have NA for point, lower, and upper.

title: Quick Start
text:
Conditional styling
Style rows based on data values using formula expressions:


Code
tabviz(meta_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_bold = ~ upper < 1,
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b")
)






Or use pre-computed columns:


Code
direction_data <- meta_data |>
  mutate(
    marker_color = case_when(
      upper < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
      lower > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
      TRUE ~ "#64748b"
    )
  )

tabviz(direction_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  marker_color = "marker_color"
)

title: Quick Start
text:
Next steps



Topic
What you’ll learn




Columns
The full col_*() / viz_*() catalog


Row Styling
Headers, summaries, indentation, badges, semantic tokens


Cell Styling
Conditional formatting for individual cells


Row Groups
Collapsible groups, hierarchical nesting


Forest Plots
Advanced forest column options


Themes
The nine preset themes and customization


Export → Pagination
Break long tables across multiple PDF pages

title: Titles and Labels
text: Add context to any tabviz table with titles, subtitles, captions, and footnotes.

title: Titles and Labels
text:
Basic Usage


Code
data(glp1_trials)

glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    title = "GLP-1 Agonist Cardiovascular Outcomes",
    subtitle = "Major randomized controlled trials",
    caption = "HR < 1 favors treatment",
    footnote = "Data from published trial results",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    )
  )

title: Titles and Labels
text:
Label Positions



Argument
Position
Typical Use




title
Top, prominent
Main figure title


subtitle
Below title, smaller
Additional context, date range


caption
Bottom left
Interpretation help, scale explanation


footnote
Bottom right
Data source, methodology notes

title: Titles and Labels
text:
Without Visualizations
Labels work on any tabviz table, not just those with forest plots:


Code
data(nba_efficiency)

nba_efficiency |>
  filter(conference == "West") |>
  head(5) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "player",
    title = "Top Western Conference Players",
    subtitle = "2023-24 Season Statistics",
    caption = "PER: Player Efficiency Rating (15 = average)",
    columns = list(
      col_text("team", "Team"),
      col_numeric("ppg", "PPG", decimals = 1),
      col_numeric("per", "PER", decimals = 1),
      col_badge("award", "Award", variants = list(
        MVP = "success",
        `All-NBA 1st` = "info",
        `All-NBA 2nd` = "warning"
      ))
    ),
    theme = web_theme_nejm()
  )

title: Titles and Labels
text:
Styling Labels
Label appearance is controlled by the theme. Different themes style labels differently:


Code
demo_data <- data.frame(
  item = c("Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma"),
  value = c(1.23, 4.56, 7.89)
)

tabviz(demo_data,
  label = "item",
  title = "Presentation Theme",
  subtitle = "Larger fonts for slides",
  caption = "Source: Example data",
  columns = list(col_numeric("value", "Value", decimals = 2)),
  theme = web_theme_nejm() |> set_density("spacious")
)

title: Titles and Labels
section: Axis labels live on the visualization column
text:
Axis labels live on the visualization column
Axis labels (the text below a forest / bar / box / violin chart’s x-axis) are a property of the viz column, not of the table. Set them inside the viz_*() constructor with axis_label = "...":
viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
           scale = "log", null_value = 1,
           axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)")
See Forest Plots — Axis Control for the full set of axis options (axis_range, axis_ticks, axis_gridlines, axis_label).

title: Titles and Labels
text:
See Also

Themes — Control label typography and colors
Visualizations — Forest plots and other graphical columns
Export — Save figures with labels intact

title: Split Plots
text: Split plots produce a separate plot for each value of a categorical variable, with an interactive sidebar for navigation. Use them for subgroup analyses where each subgroup deserves its own focused view.

title: Split Plots
text:
Two Ways to Split







API
When to use




tabviz(..., split_by = "region", shared_axis = TRUE)
Quick: split inline as you build the widget


tabviz(...) \|> split_table(by = ..., shared_axis = ...)
Fluent: split after constructing a WebSpec



Both produce a SplitForest object.

title: Split Plots
text:
split_table() Arguments








Argument
Default
Purpose




x
required
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or data frame


by
required
Column name (single string) or character vector for hierarchical splits


shared_axis
FALSE
Use the same axis range across all subgroups


shared_column_widths
FALSE
Stamp identical per-column widths across every sub-plot (v0.16+). Useful when stacking screenshots in slides / PowerPoint — sub-plot columns won’t shift between tiles. Widths are computed once from the combined data.

title: Split Plots
text:
Basic Split


Code
set.seed(42)
trial_data <- data.frame(
  study = paste0("Study ", 1:30),
  region = sample(c("North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific"), 30, replace = TRUE),
  or = exp(rnorm(30, log(0.8), 0.3))
)
trial_data$lower <- trial_data$or * exp(-1.96 * 0.25)
trial_data$upper <- trial_data$or * exp(1.96 * 0.25)

tabviz(trial_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Odds Ratio (95% CI)")
  ),
  split_by = "region",
  title = "Treatment Effect by Region"
)






The floating sidebar shows each region. Click to navigate between subgroups.

title: Split Plots
text:
Hierarchical Splits
Pass a character vector to by for nested navigation:


Code
set.seed(123)
n <- 50
subgroup_data <- data.frame(
  study = paste0("Study ", sprintf("%02d", 1:n)),
  sex = sample(c("Male", "Female"), n, replace = TRUE),
  age_group = sample(c("18-40", "41-65", "65+"), n, replace = TRUE),
  or = exp(rnorm(n, log(0.75), 0.25))
)
subgroup_data$lower <- subgroup_data$or * exp(-1.96 * 0.2)
subgroup_data$upper <- subgroup_data$or * exp(1.96 * 0.2)

tabviz(subgroup_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1, axis_label = "Odds Ratio")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm()
) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group"))






The sidebar shows “Sex” as the first level header, with “Age Group” nested under each sex.

title: Split Plots
text:
Adding Extra Columns
Columns in the underlying tabviz() carry over into every sub-plot:


Code
tabviz(subgroup_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("sex", header = "Sex"),
    col_text("age_group", header = "Age"),
    col_interval("or", "lower", "upper", header = "OR (95% CI)"),
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm()
) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group"))

title: Split Plots
text:
Shared Axis Range
By default each sub-plot auto-scales its axis. Pass shared_axis = TRUE for consistent visual scale across subgroups:


Code
tabviz(trial_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm()
) |>
  split_table(by = "region", shared_axis = TRUE)

title: Split Plots
text:
Shared Column Widths (v0.16+)
When you plan to stack sub-plot screenshots in slides, PowerPoint, or side-by-side layouts, set shared_column_widths = TRUE. Each sub-plot will render with identical per-column widths, computed once from the combined data, so the columns line up tile-to-tile:


Code
tabviz(trial_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm()
) |>
  split_table(
    by = "region",
    shared_axis = TRUE,
    shared_column_widths = TRUE
  )






The default is FALSE to preserve the pre-v0.16 behavior where each sub-plot sizes its columns to its own content.
Since v0.19 the sidebar also has an Align column widths checkbox in its footer — users can toggle this behavior at runtime without going back to R.

title: Split Plots
text:
Sidebar Features
The sidebar includes:

Section headers — split-variable names displayed as uppercase labels
Collapsible — click the chevron to collapse the sidebar
Search — filter subgroups by typing
Keyboard navigation — arrow keys move between subgroups
Bold active — currently selected subgroup is highlighted

title: Split Plots
Split Plots
text:
Exporting Split Plots
Pass the SplitForest to save_split_table() (or save_plot(), which dispatches automatically). The directory structure mirrors the split hierarchy:
p <- tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  )
) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group"))

save_split_table(p, "output", format = "svg")
# Writes (one file per (sex, age_group) leaf, mirrored as a directory tree):
#   output/Female/Female_18-40.svg
#   output/Female/Female_41-65.svg
#   output/Female/Female_65+.svg
#   output/Male/Male_18-40.svg
#   output/Male/Male_41-65.svg
#   output/Male/Male_65+.svg
See Export for save_plot() / save_split_table() details.

title: Interactivity
text: Every tabviz widget is fully interactive by default. This page is the authoritative reference for web_interaction() — the spec object that toggles which UI chrome appears and which user capabilities are enabled.

title: Interactivity
text:
Default Interactions
Every tabviz widget includes these interactions out of the box:







Interaction
Action




Sort
Click column headers to sort


Select
Click rows to select/deselect


Hover
Hover to highlight rows


Collapse
Click group headers to collapse/expand


Resize
Drag column borders to resize


Filter
Per-column filter popovers in headers


Reorder
Drag rows or columns to reorder


Edit
Double-click cells to edit — also works on existing title / subtitle / caption / footnote


Paint
Paint-brush button opens a popover for stamping emphasis / muted / accent onto rows or cells


Export
Download menu in header (hover to reveal)


Themes
Theme switcher in header





Code
data(glp1_trials)

glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data", group == "Main Trials") |>
  head(5) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160),
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    ),
    theme = web_theme_nejm(),
    footnote = "Try: click headers to sort, hover over rows, drag column borders"
  )

title: Interactivity
text:
The show_* vs enable_* Rule
web_interaction() uses two prefixes deliberately:

show_* controls static UI visibility — whether a piece of chrome appears at all (show_legend, show_group_counts). Nothing the user does at runtime brings it back.
enable_* controls user capabilities — whether a behavior is available (enable_sort, enable_filters, enable_edit, …). The associated UI is rendered when the capability is on, hidden when off.

When in doubt: render-or-not is show_; can-the-user-do-it is enable_.

title: Interactivity
text:
web_interaction() Arguments








Argument
Default
Purpose




show_legend
TRUE
Show legend for multi-effect plots


show_group_counts
FALSE
Show (n) after each row-group label


tooltip_fields
NULL
Character vector of column names to show in marker hover tooltip


enable_sort
TRUE
Click headers to sort


enable_collapse
TRUE
Click group headers to collapse/expand


enable_select
TRUE
Click rows to select


enable_hover
TRUE
Highlight rows on hover


enable_resize
TRUE
Drag column borders to resize


enable_export
TRUE
Show download button in header


enable_filters
TRUE
Per-column filter popovers in column headers


enable_reorder_rows
TRUE
Drag to reorder rows (within a group)


enable_reorder_columns
TRUE
Drag to reorder columns (within a column group)


enable_edit
TRUE
Double-click inline editing for text/numeric/label cells


enable_theme_edit
TRUE
Settings cog + panel. FALSE = author freeze: viewers of a published dashboard can’t restyle the figure live (the theme switcher stays governed by enable_themes)


enable_themes
"default"
Theme menu: "default", NULL, or list of WebTheme

title: Interactivity
Interactivity
Interactivity
text:
Presets
tabviz ships three preset configurations.







Preset
Description




web_interaction()
Full interactivity (the default)


web_interaction_minimal()
Hover only — no sorting, selection, resize, export, filtering, reorder, edit


web_interaction_publication()
Fully static — every interaction off, no legend


web_interaction_full()
Explicit “everything on” — same as web_interaction() defaults




Minimal Interaction


Code
glp1_trials |>
  filter(row_type == "data") |>
  head(4) |>
  tabviz(
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1, width = 160)
    ),
    interaction = web_interaction_minimal(),
    theme = web_theme_nejm()
  )








Publication Mode
web_interaction_publication() removes all interactivity — suitable for print, static documents, or screenshots.


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(...),
  interaction = web_interaction_publication()
)

title: Interactivity
text:
Theme Menu
The enable_themes argument controls the theme switcher. Three values are accepted:







Value
Effect




"default"
Show all themes returned by package_themes()


NULL
Hide the theme menu entirely


list(...)
Show only the listed WebTheme objects (named entries override displayed names)





Code
# Hide the theme menu
tabviz(data, interaction = web_interaction(enable_themes = NULL), ...)

# Curate a specific shortlist
tabviz(data, interaction = web_interaction(
  enable_themes = list(
    web_theme_nejm(),
    web_theme_nejm(),
    web_theme_nejm()
  )
), ...)


selectable_themes() is a fluent sibling that does the same thing post-construction:


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study") |>
  set_theme("nejm") |>
  selectable_themes(list(
    Classical = web_theme_nejm(),
    Modern    = web_theme_nejm()
  ))


The active theme is always auto-included in the menu so the user can revert.

title: Interactivity
text:
Pan & Zoom on Visualization Columns
Every viz column (viz_forest, viz_bar, viz_boxplot, viz_violin) supports direct-manipulation pan and zoom on its x-axis:







Gesture
Action




Mouse wheel over the viz area
Zoom in/out, anchored on the cursor


Click and drag
Pan the axis


Double-click
Reset to the default domain



State is per column — two forest columns side-by-side can be inspected independently. It is session-only (refreshing the page resets everything); there is no R-side argument to persist a zoom level.
Exports are WYSIWYG: zooming or panning in the browser changes what save_plot() writes to SVG/PNG and what the in-widget download button produces. Confidence intervals that fall outside the zoomed window are rendered with boundary arrows; marks on bar/boxplot/violin columns are clipped to the visible region.
On log-scale forests (viz_forest(scale = "log")), gestures that would push the lower bound to zero or below are ignored.

title: Interactivity
text:
Aspect-ratio slider
The toolbar’s zoom button (the magnifier icon) opens a dropdown with three sizing controls: zoom level, fit-to-width, and an aspect-ratio slider that drives the same lever ladder that save_plot(ratio = ...) uses for static export.







Control
Action




Zoom slider (50–200 %)
Scales the entire rendered widget. Same as Cmd +/–.


Fit-to-width button
Resets to a scale that matches the container width.


Aspect-ratio slider
Reshapes the layout — flex columns absorb width changes (capped at 2× of natural), row heights absorb height changes. Sticky detent at the spec’s natural aspect; drag-and-release returns to natural cleanly.


Readable rows toggle
Below the aspect slider. When on, the slider uses anchor = "auto": wide ratios grow output width and preserve row legibility, scaling content to fit the canvas. When off, behaves like anchor = "width": narrows row heights to fit a strict aspect within the canvas. The toggle auto-flips on first slider drag so interactive use is “ambitious” by default.


Max-width / max-height selects
Container constraints, independent of the aspect slider.



Behind the scenes, the slider mutates the spec’s target_aspect and target_aspect_anchor fields. The “View source” panel emits the corresponding set_aspect_ratio(N, anchor = "auto") call so any slider position round-trips into reproducible R code. The settings panel’s This figure band shows the per-figure row-height pins the slider creates (see Themes — live spacing values).
For the static-export side of the same machinery, see save_plot(..., ratio = , anchor = ) and set_aspect_ratio().

title: Interactivity
text:
Row Selection
Selected rows are tracked internally and persist during sorting and filtering.


Code
sample_data <- data.frame(
  item = c("Alpha", "Beta", "Gamma", "Delta"),
  value = c(10, 25, 15, 30),
  change = c(0.05, -0.12, 0.08, 0.22)
)

tabviz(sample_data, label = "item",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("value", "Value"),
    col_percent("change", "Change")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  footnote = "Click rows to select (indicated by left border)"
)

title: Interactivity
text:
Paint Tool (v0.15+)
The paint-brush button on the widget toolbar opens a small popover where authors can stamp semantic classes onto rows or cells interactively. This is the in-widget equivalent of the R-side row_emphasis = <col> / row_muted = <col> / row_accent = <col> arguments, but applied by clicking rather than coded up front.
Flow:

Open the paint popover (brush icon in the top-right toolbar).
Pick a token: Emphasis, Muted, or Accent.
Flip the Scope switch between Row (whole row) and Cell (just one cell).
Click any row or cell to toggle the active flag on/off. The cursor turns into a crosshair while paint is active so you can spot where the next click lands.
Click the prominent Exit paint mode button at the top of the popover (or press Esc) when done. The brush icon stays lit in the toolbar’s accent color as long as paint is armed.

Persistence: painted flags merge into exportSpec, so save_plot() picks them up, and the flags round-trip through the “View source” button in the settings panel. You can also clear all paint with the “Clear all paint” button if you want to start over.
Painted flags coexist with R-side row_emphasis / etc. columns: the R column sets the baseline, paint overrides per row.

title: Interactivity
text:
Configure Columns (v0.18+)
Every col_* and viz_* column has a Configure… entry in its header context menu (right-click the column header, or use the column-menu chevron). The popover exposes the column’s relevant authoring arguments so you can tweak them without rebuilding the widget in R:

col_numeric — decimals, format.
col_interval — decimals, separator, imprecise threshold.
col_text — max chars, wrap.
viz_forest — scale, null value, axis label, and a full Axis section (range, tick count/values, gridlines, null tick, marker margin, symmetric).
viz_bar / viz_boxplot / viz_violin — full CRUD on effects (add / remove / reorder / rename / recolor / opacity / data field), plus the same Axis section. Boxplot can toggle between array-shaped and pre-computed stats data.

Double-clicking a viz column’s axis opens the configure popover pre-scrolled to the Axis section.
Changes merge into exportSpec and round-trip through the “View source” button — save_plot() picks them up. The interactive Axis theme tab was retired in v0.18 since axis values are now per-column.

title: Interactivity
text:
Title / Subtitle / Caption / Footnote Editing (v0.15+)
When enable_edit is on:

Double-click any existing title, subtitle, caption, or footnote to edit it inline (Enter to commit, Esc to cancel).
Add a missing label from R (title =, subtitle =, caption =, footnote = on tabviz()). The settings panel deliberately carries no text fields — content is edited on the canvas, appearance in the panel.

Edits merge into exportSpec.labels, so save_plot() reflects them.

title: Interactivity
Interactivity
section: How many rows? The scale envelope
text:
How many rows? The scale envelope
tabviz is built for publication figures, not data grids. The honest envelope, measured (the perf baselines ship in the repo):

Up to ~200 rows — everything is smooth: render, sort, paint, inline edit, arrange mode, export.
200+ rows — tables auto-paginate by default (200 rows/page, breaking on group boundaries; opt out with paginate = FALSE). Pagination is the designed scale story: one page mounts at a time, so every interaction stays in the smooth zone while exports still render the whole table.
~1,000–5,000 rows unpaginated (opted out) — the algorithms hold (width measurement: ~2.5 ms at 1k, ~12 ms at 5k) but the DOM mount and style recalculation dominate: initial render and theme switches turn sluggish, and arrange mode degrades first — its per-row-kind ghost highlights and live seam readouts touch every mounted row.
10,000+ rows unpaginated — unsupported as an interactive surface. Render it paginated, split it (split_table()), or aggregate first. (Static SVG/PDF export of large tables is fine — export cost scales with the algorithms, not the DOM.)

title: Interactivity
text:
See Also

Reference: Interaction Settings — Full API reference
Export — Exporting interactive tables
Shiny Integration — Interactivity in Shiny apps

title: Themes
text: A theme decides every visual choice in your table that isn’t data: surface colors, text tones, divider weights, spacing, fonts, marker fills. tabviz themes are designed so you can pick a preset, change one or two things, and never think about it again — or build a fully branded theme from a dozen inputs when you need to.

title: Themes
section: Three steps from “pick a preset” to “fully bespoke”
text:
Three steps from “pick a preset” to “fully bespoke”
Each step builds on the previous. Most users live at step 1.





flowchart LR
    A["1. Pick a preset"] --> B["2. Tweak inputs"]
    B --> C["3. Override component fields"]

    style A fill:#dbeafe
    style B fill:#bfdbfe
    style C fill:#93c5fd







Step 1 — theme = web_theme_nejm(). Done. Nine curated presets cover the common publication, dashboard, and illustrative surfaces.
Step 2 — Swap a primary color, change density, switch to a serif font. The preset stays the foundation; you nudge a few inputs.
Step 3 — Reach into a specific component (e.g. the L1 row-group bar background) for surgical control. Rare; the preset cascade handles 95% of cases.

title: Themes
text:
Step 1: Pick a preset


Code
tabviz(
  meta_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1)
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm()
)


Nine presets ship in the package across four registers — clinical (1), editorial (2), design (2), and expressive (4). Each owns a distinct expressive axis rather than trying to be everything.








Preset
Register
Identity




web_theme_nejm()
clinical
The package default — restraint-led: teal + warm-coral accent, Inter, comfortable density


web_theme_newsprint()
editorial
Texture: warm grain, serif body


web_theme_dwarven()
editorial
Fantasy: Cinzel display serif


web_theme_ledger()
design
Color: teal + oxblood, ruled rules


web_theme_brutalist()
design
Geometry: sharp corners, thick rules, no effects


web_theme_aurora()
expressive
Effects: glass + glow on a dark canvas


web_theme_terminal()
expressive
Mono phosphor CRT


web_theme_blueprint()
expressive
Draft / grid: cyanotype


web_theme_synthwave()
expressive
Neon



Switch presets at any point with set_theme():


Code
my_table |> set_theme("nejm")


The full registry is in package_themes() (a 2-level named list keyed clinical / editorial / design / expressive).

Curating the in-widget switcher
The in-widget settings panel includes a theme dropdown that lets readers swap between presets without reloading. By default it shows every package theme; pass enable_themes = list(...) to web_interaction() or selectable_themes() to curate the menu:


Code
# Flat list — no tabs, just a single column of choices.
selectable_themes(my_table, list(
  NEJM   = web_theme_nejm(),
  Ledger = web_theme_ledger()
))

# 2-level named list — each top-level key becomes a tab in the dropdown.
selectable_themes(my_table, list(
  Editorial = list(
    Newsprint = web_theme_newsprint(),
    Dwarven   = web_theme_dwarven()
  ),
  Expressive = list(Aurora = web_theme_aurora())
))


The active theme is auto-included so readers can always revert. For categorized lists, if the active theme isn’t in any user-supplied category, a Current tab is prepended.

title: Themes
text:
Step 2: Tweak inputs
Every theme — including every preset — is determined by the V4 Tier-1 inputs: a handful of OKLCH color anchors plus orthogonal axes for polarity, accessibility, typography, density, geometry, marks, and effects.








Group
Input
What it controls




Anchors
paper
Light-end neutral anchor. The neutral ramp interpolates paper → ink; surfaces, dividers, and body text all derive from it.



ink
Dark-end neutral anchor (text, strong rules).



brand
The identity hue. Seeds the brand ramp: headers, gradients, series slot 1, title color.



accent
Optional engagement hue — hover, selected, callouts, highlight bar. Mirrors brand when unset.


Axes
polarity
"light" / "dark" — reflects every anchor’s lightness around the midpoint.



monochrome
The neutral ramp rides the brand hue (phosphor / sepia / cyanotype themes in one toggle).



mode
Accessibility: "standard" / "high-contrast" / "reduced-transparency". Orthogonal to polarity.


Status
status_positive … status_info
Semantic palette. Theme-scoped identity (synthwave’s neon vs a journal’s clinical green); reverts to a curated HC-safe palette under high-contrast mode.


Type
fonts_body / fonts_display / fonts_mono
Font stacks; fonts_display mirrors body when unset. type_base_size, type_scale_ratio, and type_weights shape the 9-role size/weight matrix.


Size
density (+ density_factor)
Spacing preset plus a continuous multiplier — identity-bearing for some presets (brutalist runs 0.88).



geometry
Radius scale (sm/md/lg/pill) and border-width scale (hair/thin/regular/thick).


Shell
shell_mode / shell_texture
The two-surface model: a shell band around the paper (flush / raised / float / transparent) and its texture (ruled / grid / dotted / grain). Textures fall through to the paper when the shell has no band.


Marks
marks
Viz-mark identity: point_shape (circle / square / diamond / triangle) and interval_weight (hair / regular / thick).


Effects
effects
Glow, gradient shell, elevation, glass (frosted / aurora), caption style (chip / stripe / both), header and title styles.


Data
categorical / sequential / diverging
Data palette schemes for multi-effect series.



Build a custom theme by passing any of these to web_theme() — anchors accept hex strings or oklch() triples:


Code
my_theme <- web_theme(
  name   = "my-brand",
  brand  = "#1F3A5F",                       # navy identity
  accent = "#B08938",                       # warm gold engagement
  fonts_body = "Charter, Georgia, serif",
  density = "comfortable",
  effects = list(title_style = "bar")
) |>
  set_pin("--tv-ink2", "#862721")           # oxblood rubrication (pinnable token)


Defaults are predictable: accent mirrors brand; fonts_display mirrors fonts_body; paper/ink default to a clinical near-white / near-black pair carrying the brand hue at low chroma.

What each anchor actually drives

paper → ink define the neutral ramp — eleven OKLCH steps that every surface tone, divider, and text color binds to. Tint them (paperC / inkC in derive_preset_anchors(), or [tint_from_brand()]) for warm-paper editorial looks.
brand seeds the brand ramp: header bands, the brand gradient (caption stripe, title underline), series slot 1, and the pooled-effect diamond.
accent owns the engagement layer — hover, selected, semantic callouts, the highlight bar. Keep it visually distinct from brand.

The --tv-ink2 rubrication token survives as a pinnable token — set it with set_pin("--tv-ink2", "#hex") for an editorial second color (e.g. oxblood significance stars or caption chips) without disturbing the cascade.
To pin any derived field directly, use set_theme_field() — explicit pins survive re-resolution.


L1 group header and banding
When theme@row@banding cycles at the L1 level (the default for every preset — banding = "group" with one nesting level), the banding cycle already paints alternating fills across the L1 group rows. The renderer detects this and drops the explicit theme@row_group@L1@bg paint on the affected rows so the banded color reads as continuous and the group-bar doesn’t double-paint on top.
If you want a custom L1 bar paired with a banded background, switch banding modes (e.g. set_theme_field(c("row", "banding"), "row") or "none") so the L1 paint isn’t subsumed.
At deeper banding levels (e.g. banding = "group-2" with nested groups), the L1 bar’s explicit fill is preserved unchanged — only the banded level loses its explicit fill.


Semantic tokens — naming visual treatments
Tabviz ships with five semantic tokens that name visual treatments you can apply to a row or cell. Each token is a RowSemantic bundle on theme.row: bg, fg, border, marker_fill, font_weight, font_style. The renderer picks one token per row/cell and paints its bundle.







Token
Default treatment




emphasis
Bold + primary text and marker


muted
Lighter color, reduced prominence


accent
Bold + accent-color text and marker (most common)


bold
Just a weight bump — no color change


fill
Bold + a strong row fill (defaults to a derived theme.semantic.fill); fg auto-contrasts



Two paths to apply a token to a row:

Data column — pass a logical column to tabviz():


Code
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lo", "hi")),
  row_accent = "is_primary",      # rows where is_primary == TRUE → accent token
  row_fill   = "is_overall"       # rows where is_overall == TRUE → fill token
)


Painter (interactive) — click the paintbrush icon, pick a chip (emphasis / muted / accent / bold / fill), then click rows or cells to stamp the token on. Hover before clicking shows a semi-transparent preview of the would-be visual. The painter defaults to accent + row-scope on first open. Toggle Row/Cell scope from the popover.


Customizing token visuals
Two ways to override:

Tier-2 named color for the fill background — pin a specific color for the fill token instead of letting it derive from accent:


Code
set_theme_field(t, c("semantic", "fill"), "#1F3A5F")  # custom fill navy


Per-bundle override for any field on any token:


Code
set_theme_field(t, c("row", "fill",   "fg"),     "#FFFFFF")  # force white text on fill rows
set_theme_field(t, c("row", "accent", "border"), "#C8553D")  # red border on accent rows






Variants — three knobs every table can flip
Variants are per-table choices that don’t change the theme’s identity. They’re orthogonal to inputs and freely composable:


Code
web_theme(
  brand              = "#1F3A5F",
  density            = "comfortable",   # compact | comfortable | spacious
  header_style       = "tint",          # light | tint | bold (legacy variant)
  first_column_style = "bold",          # default | bold (Excel-style; opt-in, no preset uses it)
  effects = list(header_style = "fill") # Tier-1 form — supersedes the variant
)



density drives every spacing token (row height, padding, gaps). The three presets cover almost every screen and print scenario.
header_style picks between a light header, a brand-tinted band, and a solid-ink fill band with inverse text. Prefer the Tier-1 input (effects = list(header_style = "fill")), which supersedes the legacy variant; both are always available on every theme.
first_column_style gives the row-label column an Excel-style emphasized treatment (background tint + heavier weight). Useful when your first column is genuinely a label, not data.

You can also set variants directly on the tabviz() call as overrides (see tabviz reference).

title: Themes
section: The three editing verbs — and what each edit travels with
text:
The three editing verbs — and what each edit travels with
Every theme-editing surface in tabviz — the R modifiers, the widget’s settings panel — performs exactly one of three verbs. Knowing which verb you’re using tells you how far the edit reaches and how it behaves when the theme changes around it.









Verb
What it does
Reach
Functions




Re-tune a role
Change what a named role means (text-muted → a darker grade; the footnote type role → a smaller step)
Global — everything bound to that role follows
set_role(), set_type_role(), set_corners(), set_rules()


Re-route a component channel
Point one component’s channel at a different role (title color → accent-text; axis-label family → numeric)
Local to that component — but still cascade-coherent: the routed role keeps re-resolving under polarity flips, anchor edits, and high-contrast mode
set_component() (the interactive surface is being redesigned)


Pin
Hard-code one token to a raw value (--tv-text-footnote-size: "0.7rem")
Local and inert — a pinned value ignores polarity, anchors, and accessibility modes. Last resort.
set_pin()





Code
web_theme_nejm() |>
  set_role("text-muted", "neutral", 9) |>            # re-tune (global)
  set_component("title", col = "accent-text") |>     # re-route (local, coherent)
  set_pin("--tv-text-footnote-size", "0.7rem")       # pin (local, inert)


Discover what each verb can address: [list_roles()] (every tunable role across color, type, and geometry domains), [list_components()] (every routable component, its region, states, and channels), and [inspect_token()] (where any single token’s value comes from).

What travels with what
Edits land in one of three tiers, and the tier decides where the edit follows you:

Theme — roles, re-routes, pins, fonts, density. Travels with the portable theme artifact (theme_to_wire() / write_theme(), the interactive export). Send the JSON to a colleague and every edit arrives.
Figure — column widths and order, row-kind height pins, banding override, watermark. Rides the spec (spec.figureLayout), survives data updates, round-trips through Shiny via set_figure_layout().
View — zoom, fit, contrast mode. Per-reader, per-document (browser local storage); never serialized.

The settings panel is organized around exactly this split: the theme half edits what travels with the theme, the This figure half edits figure state, and the toolbar’s zoom controls are view state.

title: Themes
section: How it cascades under the hood
text:
How it cascades under the hood
A theme has three tiers. You only ever interact with Tier 1; the other tiers are computed.





flowchart TB
    subgraph T1["Tier 1 — Inputs (you edit these)"]
      anchors["anchors: paper · ink · brand · accent"] & axes["polarity · monochrome · mode"] & rest["fonts · density · geometry · marks · effects"]
    end

    subgraph T0["Ramps (generated)"]
      ramps["neutral / brand / accent — 11 OKLCH steps each + alpha companions"]
    end

    subgraph T2["Tier 2 — Roles (derived)"]
      roles["surface · text · border · accent-solid · highlight · status · series slots"]
    end

    subgraph T3["Tier 3 — Component tokens (the wire)"]
      tokens["~150 --tv-* CSS variables: row, header, cell, plot, shell, glass, caption …"]
    end

    T1 --> T0 --> T2 --> T3

    style T1 fill:#dbeafe
    style T0 fill:#fef3c7
    style T2 fill:#dcfce7
    style T3 fill:#f3e8ff






One declarative pipeline. Anchors expand into ramps; roles bind to ramp grades; every component token declares which resolver realizes it. Polarity reflection happens at the anchor level (one place); high-contrast mode remaps roles and drops effects at resolve time. Changing brand re-derives everything brand-touched and nothing else — explicit pins survive re-resolution.
OKLCH derivation. All blends, lightenings, and darkenings happen in OKLCH (perceptually uniform). The result: tints stay readable across the brightness range, and high-chroma brands don’t mud out when blended with surfaces.
Slot bundles. Each series anchor expands into a 7-field bundle: fill, stroke, fill_muted, stroke_muted, fill_emphasis, stroke_emphasis, text_fg. The renderer picks the right field based on row state (default / muted / emphasized) without you having to define every state by hand. Override individual fields if you need to.
If you want to inspect a resolved theme:


Code
t <- web_theme_nejm()
# Resolved --tv-* tokens (the canonical inspection path):
theme_css_vars(t)[["--tv-surface-bg"]]  # paper / surface background
theme_css_vars(t)[["--tv-text"]]        # primary ink
theme_css_vars(t)[["--tv-accent"]]      # accent
# Or read S7 slots directly:
t@accent@default     # accent role
t@series[[1]]@fill   # first series anchor
t@row@hover@bg       # accent.muted blend

title: Themes
Themes
text:
Column house styles — column_defaults
A theme can carry per-column-TYPE defaults, so picking a theme also picks how its column types dress. The nejm preset turns on significance stars and a pill for p-value columns; terminal sets numerals in mono and boxes its badges square; synthwave oversizes glyph columns. Same data, same tabviz() call — the house style rides the theme.


Code
my_theme <- web_theme(
  brand = "#0B6E4F",
  column_defaults = list(
    pvalue = list(stars = TRUE, significantStyle = "pill"),
    badge  = list(shape = "square", outline = TRUE),
    numeric = list(fontClass = "mono")
  )
)


The same field exists for JS authors (buildTheme({ column_defaults: {...} }) — option keys are the wire camelCase). Three rules keep it safe, enforced by the engine at every ingest:

The author always wins. A theme default fills an option only while it sits at its schema default; an explicit col_pvalue(stars = FALSE) is never clobbered. Switching themes re-bases cleanly — the old theme’s house style never sticks.
Kind gate. Only options whose schema kind is styling or presentation are accepted; core options (the data semantics — fields, thresholds, precision) are dropped. Ask column_schema("pvalue") which options a theme may touch — the themeable column.
Untrusted-wire safety. column_defaults travels in the portable theme artifact, so every string value passes the same grammar gate as token pins before it can reach an exported SVG attribute.

Discover the vocabulary with list_column_types() (the themeable count per type) and column_schema(type) (per-option detail).

title: Themes
text:
Going deeper: spacing and typography

Density preset values



Token
compact
comfortable
spacious




row_height
20
24
30


header_height
26
32
40


padding
8
12
16


axis_gap
8
12
16


cell_padding_x
8
10
14


column_group_padding
6
8
12


title_subtitle_gap
10
13
18


bottom_margin
12
16
22


indent_per_level
14
16
20



set_spacing() overrides any of these per-token; the rest fall back to the active density preset. Read Density for how spacing tokens flow into the renderer.


Aspect-aware spacing
When the in-widget aspect-ratio slider is engaged (or set_aspect_ratio() is pinned on the spec), the lever ladder scales row_height to absorb the height delta. The Spacing settings tab reflects this live:

A small banner at the top of the tab explains: “Row height is currently driven by the aspect-ratio slider. Editing it will clear the aspect target.”
The Row height field reads its effective value (post-lever-ladder) rather than the raw spec value, and is labelled Row height (aspect) to flag its provenance.
Editing the field clears target_aspect (so your typed value pins, the slider returns to natural). View-source emits the new set_spacing(row_height = ...) call cleanly, with no overlap with set_aspect_ratio().

Other spacing tokens (header_height, axis_gap, header_gap, footer_gap, etc.) keep their authored values in the live widget, so the slider’s effect is concentrated on row_height. Static export via save_plot() honours the full lever ladder including chrome scaling — when achieved aspect deviates from target by > 5 %, a cli::cli_warn() surfaces the residual.


Typography roles
A theme exposes nine named text roles, each emitting --tv-text-<role>-{family,size,weight} tokens from the modular scale (type_base_size × type_scale_ratio^n):



Role
Used for




title
Plot title (display family, large, semibold)


subtitle
Subtitle (body family, medium)


caption
Caption above the plot


footnote
Footnote below the plot


body
Default body text (cells, headers when not bold)


numeric
Numeric cells (tabular figures)


cell
Data cell text (defaults to body)


label
Axis labels


tick
Axis tick labels (smaller)



Edit individual fields with set_theme_field():


Code
web_theme_nejm() |>
  set_theme_field(c("text", "title", "weight"), 700) |>
  set_theme_field(c("text", "title", "italic"), TRUE)

title: Themes
section: The nine presets — when to use which
text:
The nine presets — when to use which
The roster is nine committed identities (the 27→9 cull, 2026-06): the clinical default nejm plus ledger, the design movements brutalist and newsprint, the moods aurora, terminal, blueprint, and synthwave, and the storyteller dwarven. Each owns a distinct expressive axis; several also carry a column house style.
Every preset is rendered side-by-side on one fixture in the Preset Gallery — start there. Identity is enforced: a CI distinctness gate guarantees no two presets share an anchor tuple, and the roster spreads typography, density, radius, mark shapes, and effects rather than just hue.
The everyday recipe is preset + one brand swap:


Code
theme = set_brand(web_theme_ledger(), "#1F3A5F")


See the Gallery → LOTR Easter Egg page for a worked LOTR example.

title: Themes
text:
Editing themes interactively
Two surfaces, deliberately split — and the settings panel itself is task-framed: lean by default, with depth one click away.

The in-widget settings panel is being REDESIGNED around five surfaces (Variations · Labels · Identity · Plots · Styling — the layered-engagement model in docs/dev/settings-redesign.md). Until it lands, treat the R verbs above as the stable editing surface; the current panel’s sections are historical and not worth learning.
The studio is dormant — it will be rebuilt after the core settings UX lands.

Discover the bindable roles from R with list_roles() (the set_role() companion) and the pinnable tokens with list_component_tokens() (the set_pin() companion).

title: Themes
text:
The theme JSON envelope
Both editing surfaces export and import one portable artifact:
{
  "$schema": "tabviz-theme/v4",
  "name": "nejm",
  "inputs": { "anchors": { "...": "..." }, "polarity": "light", "...": "..." },
  "roleOverrides": { "text-muted": "brand.8" },
  "pins": { "--tv-text-footnote-size": "0.7rem" }
}

inputs are the Tier-1 authoring values — everything the settings panel writes.
roleOverrides (set_role()) and pins (set_pin()) are the optional total-control members; a settings-only export simply carries them empty. Role bindings serialize as stable name aliases ("brand.8" = ramp brand, grade 8) — DTCG-shaped and rename-migratable; the legacy { "ramp": "brand", "grade": 8 } coordinate form is still read back.
Every egress and ingress — the interactive surfaces and R’s read_theme() — speaks this envelope; theme_from_wire() resolves one into a WebTheme. Files written by the resolved-blob write_theme() form are also read back transparently.

Pins and role overrides survive Tier-1 edits: change the brand anchor on an imported pinned theme and the pins re-overlay after the cascade re-resolves.

title: Themes
section: Saving a custom theme as a function
text:
Saving a custom theme as a function
For team-wide use, wrap your custom theme in a named function:


Code
web_theme_acme <- function() {
  web_theme(
    name   = "acme",
    brand  = "#FF5500",
    accent = "#0066CC",
    fonts_body    = "'Source Sans 3', system-ui, sans-serif",
    fonts_display = "'Fraunces', Georgia, serif",
    density = "comfortable",
    effects = list(header_style = "tint")
  ) |>
    tint_from_brand("subtle") |>
    set_pin("--tv-ink2", "#CC3300")
}

# Now share it across the team
tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(...), theme = web_theme_acme())


Pass to set_theme() either by string (when registered as a preset) or by handing the constructed WebTheme object directly:


Code
my_table |> set_theme(web_theme_acme())

title: Themes
text:
Reference summary

web_theme() — custom-theme constructor: anchors (hex or oklch())

polarity / monochrome / mode + fonts + density + geometry + marks + effects + status.

web_theme_*() — 9 preset constructors across clinical / editorial / design / expressive (Preset Gallery).
package_themes() — categorized named list of every preset.
set_theme(spec, theme) — switch a WebSpec (or a live proxy) to a different theme.
Focused setters — set_brand() set_accent() set_paper() set_ink() set_polarity() set_monochrome() set_mode() set_density() set_geometry() set_curves() set_effects() set_status() set_status_palette() tint_from_brand().
set_inputs(theme, ...) — multi-input escape hatch.
set_spacing(theme, ...) — per-token spacing overrides.
set_theme_field(theme, path, value) — generic deep setter; pins survive re-resolution.
selectable_themes(spec, themes) — curate the in-widget theme switcher.

For complete argument lists and class definitions see the Themes reference.

title: Shiny Integration
text: tabviz works seamlessly with Shiny through the htmlwidgets framework. This page covers basic integration, the proxy API for live updates, and split-table outputs.

title: Shiny Integration
text:
Output and Render








UI
Server
Used for




tabvizOutput()
renderTabviz()
A single tabviz widget


splitTabvizOutput()
renderSplitTabviz()
A SplitForest (tabviz + sidebar of subgroups)



library(shiny)
library(tabviz)

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Forest Plot Explorer"),
  tabvizOutput("plot", height = "500px")
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    tabviz(meta_data,
      label = "study",
      columns = list(
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1)
      )
    )
  })
}

shinyApp(ui, server)

Sizing
Both output functions accept standard CSS sizing:



Argument
Default
Examples




width
"100%"
"800px", "50vw"


height
"400px" ("600px" for split)
"600px", "80vh"

title: Shiny Integration
Shiny Integration
text:
The Proxy API
Proxy functions update the plot without a full re-render — scroll position, selection state, and layout stay stable.







Function
Purpose




tabviz_proxy(id)
Reference a tabvizOutput for live updates


split_tabviz_proxy(id)
Reference a splitTabvizOutput for live updates


split_tabviz_select(proxy, key)
Switch a split table to a specific subgroup









ImportantAlways create proxies inside reactive contexts



Never create a proxy at the top level of your app. Both tabviz_proxy() and split_tabviz_proxy() require an active Shiny reactive domain.



Fluent verbs dispatch on proxies
Every fluent modifier — every set_*, add_*, remove_*, sort_*, filter_*, plus set_cell() and clear_edits() — dispatches on tabviz_proxy and sends a custom message to the running widget instead of mutating a static spec. The same call site works on a WebSpec, an htmlwidget, and a proxy:







Category
Verbs




Columns
add_column(), remove_column(), move_column(), resize_column(), update_column()


Rows
sort_rows(), filter_rows(), clear_filters(), select_rows(), move_row()


Data
update_data() (proxy requires a fresh WebSpec)


Groups
toggle_group()


Cells
set_cell(), set_row_label(), clear_edits() (proxy-only)


Global
set_theme(), set_zoom()





Sorting and filtering
observeEvent(input$sort_asc,  sort_rows(tabviz_proxy("plot"), "hr", "asc"))
observeEvent(input$sort_desc, sort_rows(tabviz_proxy("plot"), "hr", "desc"))

observeEvent(input$filter_n, {
  filter_rows(tabviz_proxy("plot"), "n", "gt", input$min_sample_size)
})

observeEvent(input$clear_filters, {
  clear_filters(tabviz_proxy("plot"))
})
Filter operators: "eq", "neq", "gt", "lt", "contains".


Replacing the dataset
update_data() on a proxy takes a WebSpec, not a raw data frame. Build one with tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE):
observeEvent(input$switch_dataset, {
  new_spec <- tabviz(
    get_dataset(input$dataset_name),
    label = "study",
    columns = list(
      viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                 scale = "log", null_value = 1)
    ),
    .spec_only = TRUE
  )
  update_data(tabviz_proxy("plot"), new_spec)
})


Chaining
Every proxy verb returns the proxy invisibly, so calls chain:
observeEvent(input$reset_view, {
  tabviz_proxy("plot") |>
    clear_filters() |>
    sort_rows("study", "none") |>
    toggle_group("all", collapsed = FALSE)
})

title: Shiny Integration
Shiny Integration
Shiny Integration
text:
Two-way Integration: Reading Widget State
The widget emits every interactive dimension back to Shiny as named inputs. This lets a dashboard mirror what the user is doing inside the widget — sort clicks, filter changes, column show/hide, paint tokens, group collapses, even zoom and pan state.

The contract
Every dimension is emitted as input$<id>_<dimension> with a uniform envelope:
list(value = ..., source = "user" | "proxy", ts = <ms>)
source distinguishes user-driven mutations from proxy-pushed ones, so an observer can ignore its own writes:
observeEvent(input$plot_sort, {
  req(input$plot_sort$source == "user")  # don't echo proxy writes
  current_sort <- input$plot_sort$value  # list(column =, direction =)
  # ...react to user-driven sort change
})


tabviz_state() — read everything at once
