  refline(0.8, label = "Threshold", style = "dashed", color = "#e11d48")
)

title: Cheatsheet
text:
Split plots
# Single variable split
tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest(...)),
       split_by = "region")

# Hierarchical split with shared axis
tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest(...)),
       split_by = c("region", "country"),
       shared_axis = TRUE)

# Identical column widths across sub-plots (v0.16+)
tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest(...)),
       split_by = "region",
       shared_column_widths = TRUE)

title: Cheatsheet
text:
Exporting
p <- tabviz(data, ...)
save_plot(p, "plot.svg")              # Vector
save_plot(p, "plot.png", scale = 2)   # Raster (2x resolution)
save_plot(p, "plot.pdf")              # Print

title: Cheatsheet
text:
Pagination
Break long tables across multiple pages. HTML viewer renders one page at a time with prev/next; save_plot(*.pdf) emits a true multi-page PDF.
# Defaults (30 rows / page, group integrity, orphan_min = 3)
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = TRUE)

# Rows-per-page shorthand
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = 50)

# Format presets
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = paginate_letter())
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = paginate_a4(orient = "landscape"))
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = paginate_slide())

# Full control
tabviz(data, ..., paginate = paginate_spec(rows = 25, orphan_min = 5,
                                           break_on = "split"))

# Override per render (keep the spec, change for one save)
save_plot(p, "out.pdf", paginate = paginate_spec(rows = 50))

# Flatten a paginated spec for a PNG without a warning
save_plot(p, "out.png", paginate = NULL)

# Fluent modifier — set / clear pagination on an existing spec
spec |> paginate(rows = 30)
spec |> paginate(NULL)              # turn off
Multi-page PDF merges per-page PDFs via qpdf::pdf_combine() (in Suggests). Missing qpdf falls back to a numbered series with a warning.

title: Cheatsheet
text:
Quick patterns
# Log scale for ratios
viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
           scale = "log", null_value = 1)

# Linear scale for differences
viz_forest(point = "md", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
           scale = "linear", null_value = 0)

# Significant rows in bold
row_bold = ~ pvalue < 0.05

# Color markers by direction
marker_color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#94a3b8")

# Scale marker size by sample size
tabviz(data, ..., marker_size = "n")

# Column groups
col_group("Treatment", col_n("n_tx"), col_events("ev_tx", "n_tx"))

# Hierarchical grouping
group = c("region", "country")

# Interval column (always explicit)
col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)", separator = " ")

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Note



Living document. The cascade reference in Styling Cascade tells you what the tokens are. This document tells you why. Read this before proposing a fourth identity tier, a “trend” slot, a charm recipe, or otherwise expanding the surface area.
Decisions get appended to the Decision log at the bottom — never rewritten — so future maintainers can diagnose drift against original intent.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Why this document exists
A cascade specification is a what. It enumerates inputs, derived tokens, component bindings, and the formulas that connect them. It can’t tell you why the inputs are those inputs and not different ones, or why the cascade ends at three tiers and not four, or why a class of feature requests has been considered and rejected over and over again.
This document is the why companion. It’s also a record: every substantive shape decision is dated and reasoned through here, so when someone proposes re-litigating one of them, the prior conversation is on the page.
Three audiences:

Future contributors evaluating a feature request that touches the cascade. Read the anti-patterns section first.
Theme authors building a new preset. The theme authoring recipe is the practical starting point.
Curious users who want to know what they’re using. The two-knob principle and the three-axis contract are the entry points.

If you want the formal contract, go to Styling Cascade.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Publication-quality as a constraint
tabviz’s product wedge is publication-quality output. Not “looks professional,” not “modern aesthetic” — publication-quality: tables that could appear unmodified in a peer-reviewed journal article, a regulatory submission, an annual report.
This is a constraint, not a label. Publication-quality is downstream of a specific aesthetic discipline:

Restraint over expressivity. A fourth color knob, an emoji recipe, a decorative glyph slot — each one is an opportunity for a user to make their table louder than it should be.
Fewer dimensions, used well. Two coordinated colors that read as intentional beat five independent colors that read as crowded.
Chrome stays neutral. Banding, hairlines, gridlines, axis text — these are scaffolding. Pull them toward the brand at low strength for cohesion; never at high strength for prominence.
Defaults that work everywhere. A user who pins primary and writes one line of R should get a table that wouldn’t look out of place in The Lancet. Anything that requires editorial intervention to look acceptable has failed.

Most of the choices documented below are downstream of this single frame. If you find yourself defending an addition with “but the user could choose to use it well,” check that frame again. Publication-quality is what most users get by default, not what the most disciplined users can achieve with effort.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
The two-knob principle
The theme contract is two knobs: identity and engagement.
Identity is the brand axis — the tone the table opens with, the tone it returns to under any structural emphasis. It mirrors down the chain: secondary defaults to primary unless the author explicitly pins a second color. In a mono theme (most journals), there is one identity color; in a two-color editorial theme (LoTR-style), there are two.
Engagement is the accent — the color that fires when something extra is happening. Hover. Selected. A semantic row callout. The status.info fallback. The selection edge bar. Accent is orthogonal to identity; it never enters the mirror chain. It’s also reserved: it does not land on default content (badges, glyph fills, ornament). Those belong on identity, where they coordinate with the rest of the table.
Why two knobs and not more:

A single identity knob makes mono themes trivial: pin primary, done.
A second identity knob (secondary, mirroring primary) makes two-color editorial themes coherent without adding any complexity to mono themes.
A separate engagement knob (accent) keeps the “look here” signal semantically distinct from the brand surface, so a hovered row can’t read the same color as a regular badge two rows down.
Adding any knob beyond these reopens the question of which knob a new feature should land on. The contract collapses.

Why accent is reserved for engagement:

When accent doubled as both “the engagement color” and “the default fill for content-bearing glyph columns” (badges, pictograms, rings, stars), the same hue did two unrelated jobs and the emphasis signal lost its meaning. A hovered row read the same color as a regular badge. The 2026-04-28 redesign fixed this: glyph col defaults moved to the secondary identity axis (which mirrors primary in mono themes — visually identical to before for journal users — and lights up secondary in two-color themes).
The mental model “identity for what is, accent for what’s happening” is the entire reason themes feel coherent.

If a new role doesn’t cleanly map to one of these two axes, the answer is almost always to refine the role’s purpose, not to add a third axis.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
The three-axis contract
Identity is two tiers, but the actual roles are three. Each axis owns exactly one job:








Axis
Owns
Default behavior




primary (+ primary_deep)
Prominence: title text, bold leaf-header band, container border, series[0] anchor
Required input. _deep auto-darkens by 15% (OKLCH) if NA.


secondary (+ secondary_deep)
Structure: bold col-group band, row-group L1/L2/L3 bg, glyph col defaults (badge/pictogram/ring/stars), AND chrome texture (surface.muted, divider.subtle/strong, alt-row banding, gridline, axis/tick)
NA mirrors primary. secondary_deep mirrors primary_deep when secondary is NA; auto-darkens otherwise.


accent (+ accent_deep)
Engagement: hover, selected, row.accent.fg, accent.tint_subtle/medium, semantic.fill, selection edge color, status.info fallback
Required input. Orthogonal — never enters the identity mirror chain.



The single rule for every theme: pin what’s distinct, mirror the rest. Mono themes pin primary only. Two-color editorial themes pin primary + secondary. Accent is always pinned (engagement is never silent).
The structural axis is doing the most work. In mono themes, you don’t notice — secondary mirrors primary, so chrome and groupings read as primary-tinted variants of the canvas. In two-color themes, secondary lights up and you get coordinated movement across:

The bold column-group band reads in secondary
Row-group bars read in a 16% (or 24% under tint/bold header) mix of secondary into surface
Cell hairlines and dividers carry a faint secondary tint
Alt-row banding picks up the same secondary chrome
Glyph cells (badges, rings, stars, pictograms) default to secondary

All from one pin. No per-theme overrides. That’s the dividend the contract pays.

title: Theming Manifesto
section: Why tertiary was added (2026-04-28) and removed (2026-04-29)
text:
Why tertiary was added (2026-04-28) and removed (2026-04-29)
Honest record. Tertiary was a 24-hour decision and the postmortem teaches the most important lesson in this document.

The 2026-04-28 decision
The previous architecture was brand / brand_deep + accent. Editorial themes (LoTR-style demos) wanted a second color for chrome texture — specifically, the umber/moss/wheat tones that distinguish the three LoTR themes from each other. Without a knob for it, theme authors were either hardcoding hex values into every chrome token (high maintenance, drifts on input change) or accepting a primary-tinted chrome that looked wrong against the bold identity bands.
The proposal: rename brand → primary, add secondary for column-group bg + row-group bg + glyph defaults, add tertiary for chrome texture (surface.muted, dividers, banding, gridlines, axis/tick). Three identity tiers, each mirroring up the chain. Accent stays orthogonal.
This shipped to main on 2026-04-28. Memory entry saved. Themes migrated. Validation green.


The 2026-04-29 reversal
Within 24 hours, two problems surfaced when stress-testing editorial themes:

Three colors usually communicate worse than two. When designers could pin three identity hues, they did — even when the third hue’s only job was “tint the chrome differently from the band.” The result was tables with too much going on. Editorial restraint requires that the architecture not invite the third pin in the first place.
Chrome wants to read structurally, not ornamentally. Routing chrome through its own knob made it feel like a third identity color (because it was). Folding chrome onto the secondary axis — same axis that owns col-group bg, row-group bg, glyph defaults — collapsed it into “the structural color” instead of “the chrome color.” The mental model simplified: secondary is everything that supports the primary band.

The reversal: tertiary and tertiary_deep removed entirely. Chrome texture migrated onto secondary_deep at the same mix strengths. Mono themes are visually unchanged (secondary mirrors primary). Two-color themes get one structural axis instead of two.
The lesson:

Adding expressivity is reversible only if you add it carefully. Tertiary was added with full validation, full migration, full memory trail — which made the reversal cheap. A messier addition would have cost weeks.
A 24-hour postmortem on a fresh decision is healthier than living with the wrong shape for a quarter. The reversal entry is in this log not as embarrassment but as evidence that the loop is short.
The right number of identity tiers is two. Probably forever.

The LoTR themes lost nothing essential. Dwarven kept bronze (primary) + gold (secondary, now tinting chrome too) + ember (accent). The third umber pin folded into the gold cascade — and the chrome in Dwarven now reads as warm-burnished-metallurgy, which is more on-theme than the original cool umber against warm bronze ever was.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Mirror chain semantics
The identity chain is a single rule:

If secondary is NA at construction, it mirrors primary during resolution. If secondary_deep is NA, it mirrors primary_deep (when secondary itself is NA) or auto-darkens from secondary (when secondary is pinned).

A few things to know about how this behaves:
Mirroring happens at resolve time, not at construction. ThemeInputs(brand = "#0891b2") stores secondary = NA literally; the NA only resolves to "#0891b2" after resolve_theme() has run. This means re-resolving an already-resolved theme is idempotent: the resolver only writes into NA-valued fields, so a previously-mirrored secondary stays at primary’s value, and a pinned secondary stays at the user’s pin.
Pinning a tier breaks the mirror for that tier and downstream tiers. If you pin secondary, secondary_deep no longer follows primary_deep — it auto-derives from your pinned secondary instead. This is the intentional break: the moment you say “I want a second color,” the deep companion tracks the second color, not the first.
Accent never mirrors. It’s a required input precisely because engagement is never silent. Even in a mono theme, accent is a deliberate choice (often a deliberate counterpoint — Cochrane pairs cool teal primary with warm coral accent specifically because the contrast lights up the engagement signal).
The mirror is not just visual. It propagates through every derived token. In a mono theme, divider.strong reads secondary_deep which mirrors primary_deep which auto-darkens primary — a single edit to primary cascades through dividers, surface.muted, banding, gridlines, all of it. This is the dividend that justifies the implicit-NA design over having theme authors restate primary in every cascade slot.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Cascade tiers
Three tiers, each with one job:

T1 — Inputs. What the customer chooses. primary, secondary, accent, plus _deep companions, plus slot_style (enum), header_style (enum), neutral ramp, status colors, series anchors, fonts. ~15 fields. This is the only tier most users edit directly.
T2 — Semantic roles. Derived tokens with named semantics: surface.base/muted/raised, content.primary/secondary/muted/inverse, divider.subtle/strong, accent.default/muted/tint_subtle/tint_medium, semantic.fill, status.*, plus per-slot SlotBundle for series marks. The resolver fills these from T1 via OKLCH formulas; users override individual fields to pin a tone.
T3 — Component bindings. Per-cluster overrides: header.{light, tint, bold}.{bg, fg, rule}, column_group.*, row_group.L1/L2/L3.bg, cell.border, plot.axis_line/tick_mark/gridline/reference. These default to T2 values with cluster-local computations (e.g., bold rule is a 40% mix of inverse against the band’s own bg). Users can pin individual leaves; the rest cascade from T2.

Three tiers of overrides means three chances for a user to break a required relationship. Construction-time validation (validate_resolved_theme) catches the obvious failures (bold-band contrast, body-text contrast) at theme construction — not at render — so the error message points at the source of the bad pin, not at a downstream symptom. WCAG AA Large (3.0) for chrome bold text; AA Normal (4.5) for body text. Tests can opt out with .validate = FALSE.
The cascade is fully introspectable from R:


Code
inspect_resolved(web_theme_nejm(), "header.bold.bg")
#> $path: "header.bold.bg"
#> $value: "#006B9C"
#> $cascade: c("inputs$primary_deep", "inputs$primary")
#> $derivation: "inputs$primary_deep (auto: oklch_darken(primary, 0.15))"


The same provenance feeds the widget’s Theme tab tooltips — single source of truth for “where did this hex come from?”.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Anti-patterns
These are not “things we haven’t gotten around to.” These are things tabviz will refuse to do. If you encounter one in a feature request, push back.

Don’t add a fourth identity tier (or revive tertiary)
Two-knob is canonical. Tertiary was added on 2026-04-28 and removed on 2026-04-29 — see the postmortem. Any future proposal to add a third identity color must either (a) demonstrate a use case that the secondary mirror chain genuinely cannot serve, or (b) explain why the prior reversal’s reasoning no longer applies. Aesthetic preference for three colors is not sufficient — the reversal happened because designers reached for three when two would have read better.


Don’t introduce slot-typed Tier 1 anchors
A previous architectural proposal added slot.trend, slot.distribution, slot.range, etc. as Tier 1 anchors keyed to recipe types. This was considered and retracted. The two-knob model handles every case the slot-typed proposal addressed (via accent-tint and identity-deep derivations). Reintroducing slot-typed anchors adds dimensionality that the cascade has to thread through every component binding for near-zero practical gain.


Don’t mix accent into default content
Accent owns engagement. Hover, selected, callouts, status.info, semantic row treatments. It does not own:

Default badge fills
Glyph cell defaults (rings, stars, pictograms)
Ornament that fires by default

Those belong on the secondary identity axis. The 2026-04-28 redesign documented this explicitly: glyph col defaults moved from accent to secondary precisely so the engagement signal stays meaningful. If you find yourself wanting accent on a content surface that’s always rendered, the correct fix is almost certainly identity, not accent.


Don’t tint chrome toward accent
Chrome (banding, dividers, gridlines, axis text) is structural. It tints toward secondary_deep — which in mono themes mirrors primary_deep, so chrome stays cohesive with the brand. Tinting chrome toward accent makes hovered/selected rows merge into the chrome band. This was wrong before the cascade rework and would still be wrong after.


Don’t add charm recipes
Pictogram arrays, emoji marks, decorative glyph recipes, isotype-style visualizations. These are charming in stress-test demos but distract from publication-quality positioning. The recipe library is intentionally small: it grows when a serious publication-quality use case demonstrably isn’t covered (e.g., gauge-against-target, dots-of-N, stacked-bar proportion compositions are on the roadmap), not when a demo would be fun.
The boundary check: would this recipe appear in The Lancet? If no, hold the line.


Don’t bypass the cascade
Every now and then a feature request asks for a theme override that “skips” the cascade — a one-off color that doesn’t flow through any of the derivations, doesn’t participate in mirroring, can’t be inspected. This signal almost always means the cascade is missing a case. The right response is to extend the cascade (add the slot to T2 or T3, document its derivation, run validation), not to introduce a side door.
If genuinely no T1/T2/T3 location fits, that’s a decision-quality moment. Stop and re-examine the role’s semantics before committing to a new slot.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Authoring a new theme
The recipe is short. Most themes need only Tier 1 inputs.
Mono theme (the journal default — Cochrane, Lancet, JAMA fall here):


Code
my_theme <- web_theme(
  name = "my_journal",
  inputs = list(
    brand = "#0F4C81",     # identity hero
    accent  = "#C8553D",     # warm engagement counterpoint
    fonts_body = "Georgia, 'Times New Roman', serif"
  )
)


That’s it. Secondary mirrors primary; chrome reads as primary-tinted; glyph defaults read primary; engagement (hover/selected/info) reads accent. The contract gives you a coherent table with two pinned values.
Two-color editorial theme (LoTR-style):


Code
my_editorial <- web_theme(
  name = "my_editorial",
  inputs = list(
    brand   = "#7A4E22",   # bronze
    secondary = "#D4A955",   # gold — drives col-group bands, row-group bars,
                             #        glyph defaults, AND chrome texture
    accent    = "#E0673A",   # ember — hover, selected, status.info
    fonts_body = "'EB Garamond', Georgia, serif"
  ),
  variants = list(header_style = "bold")
)


One additional pin (secondary) and one variant flip (header_style) — the rest cascades.
Verifying your theme:


Code
# Check what a specific leaf resolved to + why.
inspect_resolved(my_theme, "header.bold.bg")
inspect_resolved(my_theme, "divider.strong")
inspect_resolved(my_theme, "row_group.L1.bg")

# Construction-time validation runs automatically — broken contrast
# aborts at web_theme() with a structured error pointing at the leaf.
# To bypass for experimental / synthetic colors:
my_test <- web_theme(..., .validate = FALSE)


What to override at T2/T3 (and when):

Override at T1 first. 90% of themes never need to touch T2 or T3.
Override at T2 when a family of derived tokens needs adjustment — e.g., divider.strong to pin the rule color across header/group/axis.
Override at T3 only for cluster-specific tweaks — e.g., a particular preset wants the bold-mode header band darker than its primary_deep would give.

The wider you cast the override, the higher up the cascade you should pin it. T3 overrides are bandages; T1 overrides are intent.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Open questions
The architecture is mature enough that big redesigns are unlikely. Outstanding questions are tactical:

Recipe fallbacks at narrow widths. Whether violins below a width threshold should automatically degrade to density strip / boxplot. Could be guidance or runtime fallback. No current opinion.
Slot.style additional variants. Currently three values (fill_with_darker_stroke, flat_fill, outlined). A “stroke_only” variant has been suggested for very dense plots. Not yet implemented; awaiting a real-world need.
Header tint strength. The tint variant is a 12% mix of primary_deep into surface.base. This was chosen by eye; whether 10% or 15% reads better is taste-dependent and may benefit from per-theme override.

When new questions arise, append to the Decision log. When questions get resolved, move them out of this section and into the log entry that resolved them.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
Decision log
Append-only. Newest first. Each entry: date, decision, alternatives considered, reasoning. Future maintainers should append, not rewrite.

2026-04-29 — Tertiary removed; chrome migrates to secondary
Decision. Drop tertiary and tertiary_deep from ThemeInputs. Chrome texture (surface.muted, divider.subtle/strong, alt-row banding, gridline, axis/tick) now reads from secondary_deep at the same mix strengths. Identity is two tiers (primary + secondary mirror chain) plus orthogonal accent.
Alternatives considered. - Keep three tiers, lower the bar for what tertiary owns. Rejected: doesn’t solve the “designers reach for three colors” problem. - Add a separate chrome_tint knob (not in identity chain). Rejected: same expressivity creep with extra surface area. - Keep three tiers but make tertiary “advanced-only” (hidden from default panels). Rejected: API surface stays bloated; documentation cost is high; the prompt to use three colors is structural, not UI.
Reasoning. See the postmortem. Two-knob is canonical. Bundled with this rework: slot_style lifted to T1; header_style expanded with tint variant; row-group L1 bg gains header_style-driven subtle/strong strength; construction-time contrast validation; inspect_resolved() introspection API; 7 presets migrated (LoTR trio rebalanced as 2-color).
Migration cost. ~3 hours of focused refactoring. Caught a real bug along the way: dark theme’s auto-derived primary_deep (#5C85C8) had 2.57:1 contrast against its inverse text — pinned darker to #2E5290 (5.33:1).


2026-04-28 — Tertiary added (later reversed)
Decision. Add tertiary and tertiary_deep to ThemeInputs. Three identity tiers in a mirror chain (primary → secondary → tertiary). Tertiary owns chrome texture.
Reasoning at the time. Editorial themes wanted a third color for chrome texture distinct from the structural secondary; pinning it kept chrome cohesive without per-theme hardcoded hex values.
Reversed within 24 hours. See the 2026-04-29 entry above.


2026-04-28 — Accent reserved for layered emphasis
Decision. Glyph col defaults (badge, pictogram, ring, stars) move from accent to secondary identity. Accent is reserved strictly for layered emphasis: roles that fire when something extra is happening (hover, selected, semantic row callouts, selection edge, status.info fallback).
Reasoning. When accent doubled as both “the engagement color” and “the default fill for content-bearing glyph columns,” the same hue did two unrelated jobs and the emphasis signal lost meaning — a hovered row read the same color as a regular badge two rows down.
This decision survives the 2026-04-29 tertiary reversal unchanged. It’s the most important constraint on what accent does.


Earlier — Slot-typed Tier 1 anchors retracted
Decision. Do not introduce slot.trend, slot.distribution, slot.range, etc. as Tier 1 anchors keyed to recipe types.
Reasoning. The two-knob theme architecture (identity + accent with cascade-pinning + element overrides) handles the cases the slot-typed proposal addressed. Reintroducing slot-typed anchors adds dimensionality that the cascade has to thread through every component binding for near-zero practical gain.
This is documented in engineering-priorities.md §“Anti-priorities” as a binding constraint on future architecture work.

title: Theming Manifesto
text:
The seven shipped presets
Preset constructors that ship with the package. The four journal presets are restraint-led; the three LOTR presets exercise the full surface (display fonts, secondary tier, distinctive accents) and are useful as worked examples of the manifesto’s principles in non-journal contexts.








Preset
Source
Aesthetic discipline




web_theme_nejm()
R/themes.R
Package default; teal + warm coral, comfortable density


web_theme_nejm()

Black-and-white, ultra-compact, strong dividers

R/themes.R

web_theme_dwarven()

Stone neutrals, forge-glow accent, broad serif display

package_themes() returns these as a 2-level named list (journals / lotr); set_theme(spec, "<name>") switches by string name.

text:
See also

title: Concepts
text: The mental models that make tabviz easier to use. Read these once and the rest of the API will feel obvious.

title: Concepts
text:
Pages



Page
What you’ll learn




Data Flow
How a data.frame becomes JSON, then a Svelte widget — what column references resolve to, when formulas evaluate, the actual wire shape (rows / styles / paginate), and how grouping and pagination flow through.


Styling Cascade
The four-layer precedence model: theme → row → cell → marker. The five semantic tokens (emphasis / muted / accent / bold / fill), how they map to theme paths, and how row_* semantic flags reach into viz-cell colors.


Schema System
The producer / consumer contract, layer doctrine (renderers ↔︎ themes ↔︎ behaviors), index-stable row identity, and the canonical cond(...) + condition(...) worked example. Read this when you want to know why a feature is split where it is.


Theming Manifesto
Why themes are shaped the way they are. Two-knob principle (identity + engagement), three-axis contract, anti-patterns, the seven shipped presets, and an append-only decision log. Read this before proposing a fourth identity tier.

title: Concepts
text:
At a glance





flowchart LR
    A["data.frame"] --> B["tabviz()"]
    B --> C["WebSpec (S7)"]
    C --> D["JSON"]
    D --> E["Svelte widget"]

    style B fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style C fill:#0891b2,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ff3e00,color:#fff






tabviz() is the only entry point you need. Everything else — col_*(), viz_*(), web_theme_*(), set_*() — builds the spec that tabviz() consumes.

title: Concepts
section: Two ways to configure a plot
text:
Two ways to configure a plot
You can pass everything to tabviz() directly:
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                            scale = "log", null_value = 1)),
  row_bold = ~ pval < 0.05,
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b"),
  theme = web_theme_nejm()
)
Or build it up with fluent modifiers:
tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                            scale = "log", null_value = 1))
) |>
  set_row_style(bold = ~ pval < 0.05) |>
  set_marker_style(color = ~ ifelse(upper < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b")) |>
  set_theme("nejm")
Both produce the same widget. Pick whichever reads better at the call site.

title: Export
text:
Export tabviz widgets to static file formats (SVG, PDF, PNG).
The two exported entry points are:
Both delegate to a shared SVG generator (the same one the browser uses), then convert to PDF / PNG via rsvg. See save_plot() for the full reference, dependencies, and resolution guidance.
Code
p <- tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  )
)

save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg")
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.png", width = 1200, scale = 3)
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.pdf", width = 800)

title: Export
text:
Pagination
Long tables can span multiple PDF pages. Attach a paginate_spec() to the tabviz() call (or pass paginate = ... to save_plot() to override per render):


Code
p <- tabviz(data, label = "study",
            columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")),
            paginate = paginate_letter())   # 30 rows / page

save_plot(p, "figures/forest.pdf")          # multi-page PDF via qpdf::pdf_combine
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.png")          # warns + flattens to one image
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.png",
          paginate = NULL)                  # silence the flatten warning


For SplitForest outputs, pagination cascades into every subview — save_plot() writes one combined multi-page PDF per split leaf. See Export → Pagination in the guide for the full surface, defaults, and qpdf-missing fallback details.

title: Export
text:
Related

save_plot() — the single-plot exporter and save_split_table() for split views
split_table() — produce the SplitForest accepted by save_split_table()
Themes — theme presets aimed at print (web_theme_nejm(), web_theme_nejm(), web_theme_nejm())
web_interaction_publication() — quiet, print-clean interaction preset

title: Columns
text:
Reference for every column-defining helper: data columns (col_*()), per-row visualization columns (viz_*()), the low-level web_col() constructor, column groups, and the per-effect helpers used by multi-effect viz columns.
All col_*() helpers require field (the data column name) as the first positional argument. They also accept the cell-styling arguments documented in Cell-level styling below: bold, italic, color, bg, badge, icon, emphasis, muted, accent, fill, plus align, header_align, wrap, sortable, na_text, tooltip, formatter, and flex.

Argument
Type
Default

required
Badge text column.

NULL

  published = "success", draft = "warning", rejected = "error"
))


col_stars()
col_stars(field, header = NULL, width = NULL,
          max_stars = 5, color = "#f59e0b",
          empty_color = "#d1d5db",
          half_stars = FALSE, domain = NULL, ...)
domain = c(min, max) remaps arbitrary input ranges into [0, max_stars].


col_pictogram()
col_pictogram(field, header = NULL, width = NULL,
              glyph = "person", glyph_field = NULL,
              max_glyphs = NULL, half_glyphs = FALSE,
              min_value = NULL, max_value = NULL,
              color = NULL, empty_color = NULL,
              size = c("base", "sm", "lg"),
              layout = c("row", "stack"),
              value_label = FALSE,
              label_format = NULL, label_decimals = 1,
              na_text = NULL, ...)
Repeated glyphs as either a count (render round(value) glyphs) or a rating (fill value of max_glyphs). glyph_field = "<col>" paired with a named-vector glyph selects per-row glyphs (glyph = c("low" = "leaf", "high" = "flame")). min_value / max_value remap an arbitrary input range into [0, max_glyphs] (rating mode). value_label accepts FALSE / TRUE (= "trailing") / "leading" / "trailing".


col_ring()
col_ring(field, header = NULL, width = NULL,
         min_value = 0, max_value = 1,
         color = NULL, thresholds = NULL,
         track_color = NULL,
         size = c("base", "sm", "lg"),
         show_label = TRUE,
         label_format = c("percent", "decimal", "integer"),
         label_decimals = 0,
         na_text = NULL, ...)
Donut gauge that fills (value - min_value) / (max_value - min_value) of a circle. thresholds is a numeric vector of breakpoints (raw input units) where the arc color shifts; pass a paired color vector of length length(thresholds) + 1, or omit color for the auto-defaulted positive/warning/negative palette (with up to two thresholds).


col_img()
col_img(field, header = NULL, width = NULL,
        height = NULL, max_width = NULL,
        fallback = "[img]",
        shape = c("square", "circle", "rounded"), ...)

NULL



shared_axis
logical(1)
NULL
In split forests, opt this column in/out of the split-level shared axis.





effect_forest()
effect_forest(point, lower, upper,
              label = NULL, color = NULL,
              shape = NULL, opacity = NULL)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




point
character(1)
required
Point column.


lower
character(1)
required
Lower bound column.


upper
character(1)
required
Upper bound column.


label
character(1)
point
Legend label.


color
character(1)
NULL (theme cycle)



shape
choice
NULL (theme cycle)
One of "square", "circle", "diamond", "triangle".


opacity
numeric(1)
NULL
0–1.









NoteNo per-effect size



In multi-effect plots, marker size is row-driven (via set_marker_style(size = ...) or marker_size on tabviz()) so weights remain comparable across effects.




viz_bar()
viz_bar(...,                  # one or more effect_bar() objects
        header = NULL, show_header = TRUE, header_align = "center",
        width = NULL,
        scale = c("linear", "log"),
        axis_range = NULL, axis_ticks = NULL,
        axis_gridlines = FALSE,
        axis_label = "Value", show_axis = TRUE)

effect_bar()
effect_bar(value, label = NULL, color = NULL, opacity = NULL)



viz_boxplot()
viz_boxplot(...,              # one or more effect_boxplot() objects
            header = NULL, show_header = TRUE, header_align = "center",
            width = NULL,
            scale = c("linear", "log"),
            axis_range = NULL, axis_ticks = NULL,
            axis_gridlines = FALSE,
            show_outliers = TRUE,
            whisker_type = c("iqr", "minmax"),
            axis_label = "Value", show_axis = TRUE)

effect_boxplot()
Two modes — provide either data (raw array column) or all five summary stats.
# From raw array data (auto-compute quartiles)
effect_boxplot(data,
               outliers = NULL, label = NULL,
               color = NULL, opacity = 0.7)

# From pre-computed summary stats
effect_boxplot(min, q1, median, q3, max,
               outliers = NULL, label = NULL,
               color = NULL, opacity = 0.7)



viz_violin()
viz_violin(...,                # one or more effect_violin() objects
           header = NULL, show_header = TRUE, header_align = "center",
           width = NULL,
           scale = c("linear", "log"),
           axis_range = NULL, axis_ticks = NULL,
           axis_gridlines = FALSE,
           bandwidth = NULL,
           show_median = TRUE, show_quartiles = FALSE,
           axis_label = "Value", show_axis = TRUE)

effect_violin()
effect_violin(data, label = NULL, color = NULL, opacity = 0.5)

title: Columns
text:
Column groups

col_group()
Wrap several columns under a shared header.
col_group(header, ...)



Argument
Type
Description




header
character(1)
Group header label.


...
ColumnSpec
One or more col_*() / viz_*() columns.



col_group("Treatment Effect",
  col_numeric("hr", "HR"),
  col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "95% CI")
)

title: Columns
text:
Low-level constructor

web_col()
The constructor every col_*() helper delegates to. Use this directly only when you need a column type the helpers don’t cover.
web_col(field,
        header = NULL,
        type = c("text", "numeric", "interval", "bar", "pvalue",
                 "sparkline", "icon", "badge", "stars", "pictogram",
                 "ring", "img", "reference", "range", "forest",
                 "heatmap", "progress", "viz_bar", "viz_boxplot",
                 "viz_violin", "custom"),
        width = NULL,
        align = NULL, header_align = NULL,
        show_header = NULL,
        wrap = FALSE, sortable = TRUE,
        options = list(),
        na_text = NULL,
        # Cell styling
        bold = NULL, italic = NULL,
        color = NULL, bg = NULL,
        badge = NULL, icon = NULL,
        emphasis = NULL, muted = NULL, accent = NULL,
        fill = NULL)
Returns a ColumnSpec.

title: Columns
text:
Cell-level styling
Every col_*() helper accepts the same set of cell-styling arguments via .... Each takes either a column name (resolved at render time) or a formula evaluated against the row (use .x to refer to the column’s own value).








Argument
Resolved value
Effect




bold
logical
Bold text (literal weight).


italic
logical
Italic text.


color
CSS color
Text color (literal).


bg
CSS color
Cell background (literal).


badge
character
Inline badge in the cell.


icon
emoji/unicode
Icon next to value.


emphasis (semantic)
logical
Bold + theme primary color.


muted (semantic)
logical
Lighter color, reduced prominence.


accent (semantic)
logical
Bold + theme accent color.


fill (semantic)
logical
Bold + strong row-fill background; fg auto-contrasts.



# Bold significant p-values, color them
col_pvalue("pval",
           bold  = ~ .x < 0.05,
           color = ~ ifelse(.x < 0.05, "#16a34a", "#71717a"))

# Reference another column
col_numeric("hr", "HR",
            color = ~ ifelse(pval < 0.05, "#16a34a", "#71717a"))

title: Columns
text:
Alignment
Two slots: align for body cells, header_align for the header. Defaults are type-aware:







Helper
Body align default




col_text, col_numeric, col_pvalue, col_bar, col_interval, col_sparkline, col_percent, col_reference, col_heatmap, col_progress, col_date
"left"


col_icon, col_badge, col_stars, col_img
"center"


col_range, col_currency
"right"


viz_forest
header "center"


viz_bar
header "left"


viz_boxplot, viz_violin
header "center"



header_align defaults to “follow the body” — set it explicitly to decouple.

title: Columns
text:
Examples


Code
data <- data.frame(
  study  = c("Trial A", "Trial B", "Trial C"),
  hr     = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.68),
  lower  = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.52),
  upper  = c(0.95, 1.03, 0.89),
  n      = c(245, 180, 320),
  weight = c(35, 28, 37),
  pval   = c(0.018, 0.092, 0.003)
)

tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_bar("weight"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pval", stars = TRUE)
  )
)






# Column groups
columns = list(
  col_n("n"),
  col_group("Treatment Effect",
    col_numeric("hr", "HR"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "95% CI")
  ),
  col_pvalue("pval")
)

title: Columns
text:
Related

tabviz() — passes a columns list of these helpers
Styling modifiers — set_column_style() for after-the-fact per-cell styling
Annotations — refline() for viz_forest(annotations = ...)

title: Styling modifiers
text:
Fluent verbs that mutate a WebSpec, htmlwidget, or live Shiny tabviz_proxy to add row, column, marker, theme, and zoom styling. Every modifier returns the same kind of object it received, so they compose cleanly with |>.
For theme-level modifiers (palettes, typography, axis, layout) see Themes.

title: Styling modifiers
text:
set_row_style()
Apply row-level styling driven by columns in the data.
set_row_style(
  x,
  bold = NULL,
  italic = NULL,
  color = NULL,
  bg = NULL,
  badge = NULL,
  icon = NULL,
  indent = NULL,
  type = NULL,
  weight = NULL,
  emphasis = NULL,
  muted = NULL,
  accent = NULL,
  fill = NULL
)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget
required
Spec or widget.


bold
column name
NULL
Logical column → semantic bold token (weight bump only, no color change).


italic
column name
NULL
Logical column for italic rows (literal).


color
column name
NULL
CSS color column for row text (literal).


bg
column name
NULL
CSS color column for row background (literal).


badge
column name
NULL
Text column rendered as a badge on the label cell.


icon
column name
NULL
Emoji/unicode column rendered on the label cell.


indent
column name
NULL
Numeric column for label indentation.


type
column name
NULL
One of "data", "header", "summary", "spacer".


weight
column name
NULL
Numeric column driving marker size. Same shape as marker_size.


emphasis
column name
NULL
Logical column → semantic emphasis token (bold + theme primary).


muted
column name
NULL
Logical column → semantic muted token (lighter, reduced prominence).


accent
column name
NULL
Logical column → semantic accent token (bold + theme accent).


fill
column name
NULL
Logical column → semantic fill token (bold + strong row-fill background; fg auto-contrasts).



Returns: modified input (WebSpec or htmlwidget).


Code
styled <- data.frame(
  label = c("Primary Endpoint", "  CV Death", "  MI", "Summary"),
  hr = c(NA, 0.82, 0.79, 0.78),
  lo = c(NA, 0.72, 0.68, 0.65),
  hi = c(NA, 0.94, 0.92, 0.93),
  rtype = c("header", "data", "data", "summary"),
  rbold = c(TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE)
)

tabviz(styled, label = "label",
  columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                            scale = "log", null_value = 1))
) |>
  set_row_style(type = "rtype", bold = "rbold")

title: Styling modifiers
text:
set_column_style()
Per-cell styling targeting a single column.
set_column_style(
  x, column,
  bold = NULL, italic = NULL,
  color = NULL, bg = NULL,
  badge = NULL, icon = NULL
)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget
required



column
character(1)
required
Field name of the target column.


bold / italic
column name
NULL
Logical-column driver.


color / bg
column name
NULL
CSS color column.


badge / icon
column name
NULL
Per-cell badge / icon.



When both row and column styles target the same cell, the column style wins.
tabviz(data, label = "study",
  columns = list(col_pvalue("pval"))
) |>
  set_column_style("pval", bold = "is_sig", color = "sig_color")

title: Styling modifiers
text:
set_marker_style()
Style the primary effect’s interval markers.
set_marker_style(x,
                 color = NULL, shape = NULL,
                 opacity = NULL, size = NULL)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




color
column name
NULL
CSS color column for marker fill.


shape
column name
NULL
Column of "square" / "circle" / "diamond" / "triangle".


opacity
column name
NULL
Numeric column 0–1.


size
column name
NULL
Numeric weight-like column (e.g. study weights 0–100). The renderer normalizes via 0.5 + sqrt(w/100) * 1.5. Pass raw weights, not a pre-normalized multiplier.



For multi-effect plots, additional effects use the per-effect color/shape/opacity from effect_forest().

title: Styling modifiers
text:
set_zoom()
Zoom level and container constraints. Works on htmlwidgets and live tabviz_proxy objects (it is not a WebSpec modifier — apply it after rendering).
set_zoom(x,
         zoom = 1.0,
         auto_fit = TRUE,
         max_width = NULL,
         max_height = NULL,
         show_controls = TRUE)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
htmlwidget / tabviz_proxy
required



zoom
numeric(1)
1.0
0.5–2.0.


auto_fit
logical(1)
TRUE
Shrink content to fit container if too large; never enlarges.


max_width
numeric(1)
NULL
Max container width in px (≥ 100).


max_height
numeric(1)
NULL
Max container height in px (≥ 100).


show_controls
logical(1)
TRUE
Show zoom controls on hover.









ImportantReplaces tabviz() zoom arguments



tabviz(zoom = ...), tabviz(auto_fit = ...), tabviz(max_width = ...), tabviz(max_height = ...), and tabviz(show_zoom_controls = ...) were deprecated in 0.9.0. Pipe to set_zoom() after constructing the widget instead.


tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lo", "hi"))) |>
  set_zoom(zoom = 0.8, max_width = 900)

title: Styling modifiers
text:
set_theme()
Swap the active theme on a WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy. On a proxy, sends a message so the widget swaps themes without a full re-render.
set_theme(x, theme)








Argument
Type
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget / tabviz_proxy



theme
character or WebTheme
Either a built-in name or a web_theme_*() object.



Built-in names: "cochrane", "lancet", "jama", "dark", "dwarven", "elvish", "hobbit". "default" is accepted as a non-breaking alias for "cochrane". Pass a WebTheme object directly for anything else.
tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi"))) |>
  set_theme("nejm")

title: Styling modifiers
text:
selectable_themes()
Curate which themes appear in the in-widget theme switcher. This is the fluent sibling of the enable_themes argument of web_interaction().
selectable_themes(x, themes)








Argument
Type
Description




x
WebSpec or htmlwidget
Live proxy not supported.


themes
NULL / "default" / list of WebTheme
NULL hides the switcher; "default" shows all package_themes(); a list shows exactly those (named entries override displayed names). The active theme is always auto-included so the user can revert.



tabviz(data, label = "study") |>
  set_theme("nejm") |>
  selectable_themes(list(
    Classical = web_theme_nejm(),
    Modern    = web_theme_nejm()
  ))

title: Styling modifiers
text:
Quick reference








Modifier
Operates on
Purpose




set_row_style()
WebSpec, htmlwidget
Row-level styling and row types.


set_column_style()
WebSpec, htmlwidget
Per-cell styling for a single column.


set_marker_style()
WebSpec, htmlwidget
Marker color / shape / opacity / size driver.


set_zoom()
htmlwidget, tabviz_proxy
Zoom and container size constraints.


set_theme()
WebSpec, htmlwidget, tabviz_proxy
Swap the active theme.


selectable_themes()
WebSpec, htmlwidget
Curate the in-widget theme switcher.

title: Styling modifiers
text:
Related

Themes — set_brand(), set_accent(), set_density(), set_header_style() for the v2 theme builder
Data ops — add_column(), sort_rows(), filter_rows(), …
Interaction — web_interaction() and the show_* / enable_* rule
tabviz() — equivalent constructor arguments

title: Data Operations
text:
The data-operation verbs provide a fluent, pipe-friendly interface for adding, removing, rearranging, filtering, sorting, and editing the columns, rows, and cells of a table. Every verb dispatches on input type:
This single surface means the same code can compose a static plot or drive a live Shiny widget.

title: Data Operations
text:
Column Operations

add_column()
Insert a new column into the table.
add_column(x, col, after = NULL)







Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


col
A ColumnSpec from col_text(), col_numeric(), col_bar(), etc.


after
Field/id of an existing column to insert after. NULL (default) appends; "__start__" prepends.



Returns: Modified input (same type).


Code
spec <- web_spec(data, columns = list(col_text("study"), col_numeric("hr")))

# Append a column
spec |> add_column(col_numeric("se", "SE"))

# Insert after a specific field
spec |> add_column(col_badge("sig"), after = "hr")

# Prepend
spec |> add_column(col_icon("flag"), after = "__start__")




remove_column()
Remove a column by field or id.
remove_column(x, field)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field or id of the column to remove



On a proxy this sends hideColumn, which hides the column client-side without destroying its spec — add_column() with the same field will restore it.


Code
spec |> remove_column("notes")




move_column()
Change a column’s position.
move_column(x, field, to)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field or id of the column to move


to
1-based integer index, or the field name of another column



On the R side, indices are 1-based. The proxy payload converts to 0-based before sending to JS — you do not need to adjust.


Code
# Move to position 3
spec |> move_column("hr", to = 3L)

# Move before the "pval" column
spec |> move_column("hr", to = "pval")








Note



Within-group moves via static dispatch are currently unsupported — move_column() on a column that lives inside a col_group() will error. Use proxy dispatch or restructure groups.




resize_column()
Set a column’s pixel width.
resize_column(x, field, width)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field or id of the column


width
Width in pixels (≥ 40)





Code
spec |> resize_column("study", width = 240)




update_column()
Edit properties of an existing column in place.
update_column(x, field, ...)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field or id of the column


...
Named properties to update



Top-level slots written directly: header, align, header_align, wrap, sortable, width. Any other name is merged into the column’s options list (useful for type-specific knobs such as decimals, format, colors).


Code
spec |>
  update_column("hr",
    header = "Hazard Ratio",
    align = "right",
    decimals = 3
  )

title: Data Operations
text:
Row & Data Operations

sort_rows()
Order rows by a column’s values.
sort_rows(x, by, direction = c("asc", "desc", "none"))







Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


by
Field or id of the column to sort by


direction
"asc", "desc", or "none" (clears sort on a proxy)



On static inputs, direction = "none" is a no-op; the spec data frame keeps its existing order.


Code
spec |> sort_rows("hr", direction = "desc")




filter_rows()
Keep rows that match a predicate.
filter_rows(x, field, operator = c("eq", "neq", "gt", "lt", "contains"), value)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


field
Field to filter on


operator
"eq", "neq", "gt", "lt", or "contains"


value
Scalar value to compare against





Code
spec |> filter_rows("region", "eq", "North America")
spec |> filter_rows("hr", "lt", 1)


On static inputs, rows where the predicate is NA are dropped.


clear_filters()
Remove any runtime filters.
clear_filters(x)
No-op for static inputs (there is no persisted filter state). On a proxy, sends clearFilter to reset the widget’s filter stack.


select_rows()
Replace the current row selection (proxy only).
select_rows(x, row_ids)



Argument
Description




x
A tabviz_proxy


row_ids
Character vector of row ids to select



On static inputs, warns and returns the input unchanged — selection is a runtime concept with no spec-level representation.


Code
observeEvent(input$highlight, {
  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")
  select_rows(proxy, c("Trial A", "Trial C"))
})




move_row()
Reposition a row within the data frame.
move_row(x, row_id, to)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


row_id
Row id (the value in the leftmost label/id column)


to
1-based integer index, or another row id





Code
spec |> move_row("Summary", to = 1L)




update_data()
Replace the entire underlying data frame.
update_data(x, data)



Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


data
A data frame (static inputs) or WebSpec (proxy)



For a proxy you must pass a complete WebSpec, not a raw data frame — column specs, themes, and annotations need to be re-serialized together. Build a fresh spec with tabviz() or web_spec() and pass it in.


Code
# Static: swap the data frame
spec |> update_data(new_df)

# Proxy: full re-specification
observeEvent(input$refresh, {
  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")
  fresh <- tabviz(reactive_data(), label = "study",
                  columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lo", "hi")))
  update_data(proxy, attr(fresh, "webspec"))
})

title: Data Operations
text:
Group Operations

toggle_group()
Collapse or expand a named group.
toggle_group(x, group_id, collapsed = NULL)







Argument
Description




x
A WebSpec, htmlwidget, or tabviz_proxy


group_id
Id of the group to toggle


collapsed
TRUE to collapse, FALSE to expand, NULL to flip current state



Groups are defined via the group argument of tabviz(); if a spec has none, toggle_group() warns for static inputs.


Code
spec |> toggle_group("secondary_endpoints", collapsed = TRUE)

title: Data Operations
text:
Cell Edits (Proxy Only)
These verbs carry changes directly into a running widget. They have no spec-level counterpart — on static inputs they warn and return unchanged.

set_cell()
Overwrite a single cell.
set_cell(x, row_id, field, value)


Code
set_cell(proxy, row_id = "Trial A", field = "hr", value = 0.84)




set_row_label()
Rename a row’s label.
set_row_label(x, row_id, label)


Code
set_row_label(proxy, "Trial A", "Trial A (updated)")




clear_edits()
Discard every in-widget cell and label edit applied since the last full render.
clear_edits(x)

title: Data Operations
text:
Labels and watermark
Title / subtitle / caption / footnote / watermark have dedicated single-slot verbs that mirror the tabviz() arguments. Each accepts NULL to clear the slot.

set_title() / set_subtitle() / set_caption() / set_footnote()
set_title(x, title)         # NULL clears
set_subtitle(x, subtitle)
set_caption(x, caption)
set_footnote(x, footnote)


set_watermark()
set_watermark(x, text = NULL, color = NULL, opacity = NULL)
A diagonally-rotated watermark across the full plot region. Pass text = NULL to clear. color is a CSS color (defaults to theme.colors.foreground); opacity is a number in [0, 1] (default 0.07).

title: Data Operations
text:
Painter (semantic-token stamping)
The painter applies one of the five semantic tokens (emphasis / muted / accent / bold / fill) to a row or cell — equivalent to setting the matching row_* flag for that single row, but as a fluent verb suitable for piped one-shot updates and proxy dispatch.

paint_row()
paint_row(x, row_id, token = c("emphasis", "muted", "accent", "bold", "fill"))


paint_cell()
paint_cell(x, row_id, field, token = c("emphasis", "muted", "accent", "bold", "fill"))

title: Data Operations
text:
Pagination

paginate()
Fluent modifier mirroring tabviz(paginate = ...). Useful at the end of a pipe when the pagination policy is decided after the spec has been built, or for toggling on/off without re-running the constructor.
paginate(x, ...)
... accepts either a single positional PaginateSpec / shortcut value (paginate(x, paginate_letter()), paginate(x, 50), paginate(x, TRUE), paginate(x, NULL)) or named arguments forwarded to paginate_spec() (paginate(x, rows = 20, orphan_min = 5)).
spec |> paginate(rows = 30)         # sensible defaults
spec |> paginate(paginate_letter()) # preset
spec |> paginate(NULL)              # turn off

title: Data Operations
text:
Split-by column widths

set_shared_column_widths()
set_shared_column_widths(x, enabled = TRUE)
When the input is a SplitForest (or htmlwidget over one), stamp identical per-column widths across every sub-plot so columns line up tile-to-tile when screenshots are stacked. Works through the proxy too — equivalent to flipping the Align column widths checkbox in the sidebar footer.

title: Data Operations
text:
Global controls
set_theme() and set_zoom() are documented in the styling reference — they are global presentation modifiers rather than data verbs, but they accept the same input shapes (spec / widget / proxy where applicable).

title: Data Operations
text:
Verb Reference

Columns



Function
Purpose




add_column()
Insert a new column


remove_column()
Remove a column (proxy: hide)


move_column()
Change column position


resize_column()
Set pixel width


update_column()
Edit header, alignment, options





Rows & data



Function
Purpose




sort_rows()
Order rows by a column


filter_rows()
Keep rows matching a predicate


clear_filters()
Clear runtime filters (proxy)


select_rows()
Select rows (proxy)


move_row()
Reposition a row


update_data()
Replace the data frame





Groups & cells



Function
Purpose




toggle_group()
Collapse/expand a group


set_cell()
Overwrite a cell (proxy)


set_row_label()
Rename a row (proxy)


clear_edits()
Discard in-widget edits (proxy)





Labels, watermark, painter, pagination, splits







Function
Purpose




set_title() / set_subtitle() / set_caption() / set_footnote()
Single-slot label setters; NULL clears


set_watermark()
Diagonal full-plot watermark text + color + opacity


paint_row()
Stamp a semantic token (emphasis / muted / accent / bold / fill) on one row


paint_cell()
Stamp a semantic token on one cell


paginate()
Configure / clear pagination on an existing spec


set_shared_column_widths()
Toggle aligned per-column widths across split sub-plots

title: Data Operations
text:
See Also

Styling functions
Fluent API guide
Shiny integration

title: save_plot()
text:
Export a tabviz widget, WebSpec, or SplitForest to a static SVG, PDF, or PNG.
The pipeline runs entirely in R: the spec is serialized to JSON, the bundled JavaScript SVG generator (inst/js/svg-generator.js) is run via V8, and PDF/PNG outputs are produced from that SVG via rsvg.

title: save_plot()
text:
save_plot()
save_plot(x, file,
          width = NULL, height = NULL, ratio = NULL,
          flex = TRUE, scale = 2,
          which = NULL, paginate = NULL,
          ...)



Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget / SplitForest
required
The plot to export. SplitForest is dispatched to save_split_table() unless which is set.


file
character(1)
required
Output file path (or directory for SplitForest without which). Extension determines format: .svg, .pdf, or .png.


width
numeric(1) / NULL
NULL
Display width in pixels. NULL preserves natural. With height or ratio, triggers Mode 3 relayout.


height
numeric(1) / NULL
NULL
Display height in pixels. NULL preserves natural. With width or ratio, triggers Mode 3 relayout.


ratio
numeric(1) / NULL
NULL
Target aspect ratio (width / height). When set with one of width / height, the other is computed. When set alone, target width = natural width and target height = natural width / ratio. Falls back to the spec’s target_aspect (set via set_aspect_ratio()) when NULL. Mutually exclusive with passing both width and height.


flex
logical / numeric(1)
TRUE
Lever-ladder cap for flex-column absorption. TRUE = cap of 2 (each flex column may grow / shrink within [0.5×, 2×] of natural). FALSE = disable flex absorption (cap of 1; aspect change goes entirely through row-height + non-flex column scaling). A numeric N >= 1 sets a custom cap (e.g. 1.5 is conservative; Inf removes the cap).


scale
numeric(1)
2
Raster fidelity multiplier for PNG output (2 = retina). PNG-only: passing scale to a .svg or .pdf save warns.


which
string / integer / NULL
NULL
For SplitForest only. NULL (default) writes every subview into a directory; pass a key string ("Male__Young") or 1-based integer index to extract a single subview to file and demote the path to a single-file write.


paginate
PaginateSpec / shortcut / NULL
NULL
Override the spec’s pagination at the call site. Accepts NULL (off), TRUE, a positive integer, or a PaginateSpec from paginate_spec(). When set on a .pdf save, emits a multi-page PDF; on a .png / .svg save, warns and flattens (use paginate = NULL to silence). See Pagination in the Export guide.


...
—
—
Reserved for future format-specific options.



Returns: the output file path, invisibly. For paginated PDF output without qpdf, returns the character vector of numbered-series paths.

Sizing modes










width
height
ratio
Mode
Behavior




—
—
—
Natural
Render at the spec’s natural dimensions.


set
—
—
Display-scaled
Render at natural; SVG root width = requested. Height computed from natural aspect.


—
set
—
Display-scaled
Render at natural; SVG root height = requested. Width computed from natural aspect.


set
set
—
Aspect-changed
Relayout: target aspect = width / height. Flex columns absorb width delta (capped at flex); row heights scale for height delta.


set
—
set
Aspect-changed
Target height = width / ratio.


—
set
set
Aspect-changed
Target width = height * ratio.


—
—
set
Aspect-changed
Target width = natural; target height = natural width / ratio.


set
set
set
Error
Over-specified — pick at most two.



The flex argument controls what happens only in Aspect-changed mode: it caps how far each flex-eligible column may stretch before the layout falls back to scaling non-flex columns and row heights. Default-natural and display-scaled renders are unaffected by flex.


Dependencies

SVG: V8 (always required for the generator).
PDF / PNG: also requires the rsvg package.
Multi-page PDF (paginated): also requires the qpdf package, used to merge per-page PDFs into one. If qpdf is not installed, save_plot() falls back to writing a numbered series (out_p01.pdf, out_p02.pdf, …) and emits a warning.

There is no headless-browser dependency.


Resolution guide for PNG
scale is a raster-fidelity multiplier — it grows the PNG’s pixel count without changing the layout’s logical size. Vector formats (SVG / PDF) ignore scale; pass width to make a vector output denser instead.



scale
Effective DPI
Use case




1
~72
Quick preview


2
~144
Standard web / screen (default)


3
~216
Print quality


4
~288
High-resolution print





Examples
p <- forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi", label = "study")

save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg")                          # natural
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg", width = 1200)            # display-scaled
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg", ratio = 2)               # 2:1 wide
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg", width = 1200, height = 600)  # explicit aspect
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.svg", ratio = 2, flex = FALSE)     # no flex absorption

save_plot(p, "figures/forest.png", width = 1200, scale = 3)     # 3600 px PNG

# Long table — multi-page PDF (requires qpdf in Suggests)
save_plot(p, "figures/forest.pdf", paginate = paginate_letter())

# Force a denser pagination on this render only
save_plot(p, "figures/dense.pdf",
          paginate = paginate_spec(rows = 50))

# Flatten a paginated spec for a single-image PNG without warning
save_plot(p, "figures/single.png", paginate = NULL)

# SplitForest — extract one subview to a single file
save_plot(split_p, "figures/male.svg", which = "Male")

title: save_plot()
text:
set_aspect_ratio()
Pin an aspect ratio on the spec itself, so every render — save_plot(), the htmlwidget, the interactive aspect slider — picks it up by default. Backed by two WebSpec fields:

target_aspect — the numeric ratio (width / height), or NA_real_ for natural.
target_aspect_anchor — "width" / "height" / "auto", controls how target dims fall out of ratio.

save_plot(..., ratio = X, anchor = ...) overrides the spec-level values for one render only.
set_aspect_ratio(x, ratio, anchor = c("width", "height", "auto"))








Argument
Type
Description




x
WebSpec / htmlwidget / tabviz_proxy
The spec or live widget to modify.


ratio
numeric(1) / NULL
Target aspect ratio (width / height). NULL clears the target — equivalent to “render at natural aspect”.


anchor
choice
Resolution rule when only ratio is given. "width" (default): target width = natural width, target height = natural width / ratio. "height": anchors height instead — wide ratios grow output width rather than shrinking row height. "auto": picks based on whether the requested ratio is wider or taller than the spec’s natural aspect — readability-first; recommended.



# 2:1 wide, with row height preserved (output grows wider)
spec |> set_aspect_ratio(2, anchor = "auto")
The in-widget aspect-ratio slider (in the toolbar’s zoom dropdown) drives the same fields live; its “Readable rows” toggle flips the anchor between "width" and "auto". See Interactivity for the full surface.

title: save_plot()
text:
tabviz_natural_dimensions()
Compute a spec’s natural width / height / aspect without rendering — useful for sizing Quarto / RMarkdown chunks.
tabviz_natural_dimensions(x)
Returns a named list with three numeric scalars: width, height, aspect (= width / height). Accepts a WebSpec or an htmlwidget produced by tabviz() / forest_plot().
spec <- tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(viz_forest("hr", "lo", "hi")))
dims <- tabviz_natural_dimensions(spec)

#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: !expr 8 / dims$aspect

title: save_plot()
text:
save_split_table()
Export every sub-plot in a SplitForest to a directory tree mirroring the split hierarchy. Each subview’s aspect contract is independent — width / height / ratio / flex apply per-subview.
save_split_table(x, path,
                 format = c("svg", "pdf", "png"),
                 width = NULL, height = NULL, ratio = NULL,
                 flex = TRUE, scale = 2,
                 paginate = NULL,
                 ...)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




x
SplitForest / htmlwidget
required
A split-forest container or a widget produced with split_by.


path
character(1)
required
Output directory; created if it doesn’t exist.


format
choice
"svg"
"svg", "pdf", or "png".


width / height / ratio / flex / scale
—
as in save_plot()
Forwarded to save_plot() for each sub-plot.


paginate
PaginateSpec / shortcut / NULL
NULL
Override the per-spec pagination at the call site. Cascades into every subview. With format = "pdf", each split leaf becomes one combined multi-page PDF (e.g. path/Male.pdf containing all of Male’s pages).


...
—
—
Reserved for future format-specific options.



Returns: a character vector of exported file paths, invisibly.

Output directory structure







Split
Files




split_by = "region"
path/North.svg, path/South.svg, …


split_by = c("region", "age")
path/North/North_Young.svg, path/North/North_Old.svg, …





Example
data |>
  tabviz(label = "study",
         columns = list(viz_forest(point = "or", lower = "lower", upper = "upper"))) |>
  split_table(by = c("sex", "age_group")) |>
  save_split_table("output/plots", format = "svg")
save_plot() itself accepts a SplitForest and dispatches to save_split_table() automatically — pass either a directory path or a path with an extension to choose the format.

title: Function Reference
text: Complete reference for the tabviz public API. Functions are grouped by purpose; each name links to its detail page.

title: Function Reference
text:
Entry points



Function
Description




tabviz()
Main entry point — interactive table with any mix of columns.


forest_plot()
Convenience wrapper that auto-builds a forest column from point / lower / upper (or effects).


web_spec()
Alias of tabviz(..., .spec_only = TRUE) — return a WebSpec instead of a widget.


split_table()
Build a SplitForest from a spec / widget / data frame.



# Build a table with a forest plot column
tabviz(data, label = "study", columns = list(
  viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi"),
  col_numeric("hr", "HR"),
  col_pvalue("pval")
))

# Quick forest plot
forest_plot(data, point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi", label = "study")

Troubleshooting
