title: Themes
text:
Built-in theme presets, the web_theme() custom-theme constructor, the four core modifiers (set_brand(), set_accent(), set_density(), set_header_style()), and package_themes() for listing every shipped theme. Pass any theme to tabviz(theme = ...) or apply it after the fact with set_theme().
For the conceptual model see the Themes guide and the Styling Cascade concepts page.

title: Themes
text:
Preset themes
web_theme_*() builders return a fully resolved WebTheme. They take no arguments — chain modifiers to customize. Nine presets ship, each owning a distinct expressive axis: the clinical default nejm, two editorial voices, two design movements, and four expressive moods.



Theme
Register
Identity




web_theme_nejm()
clinical
The package default — restraint-led: teal + warm-coral accent, Inter, comfortable


web_theme_ledger()
design
Color: teal + oxblood, ruled rules


web_theme_brutalist()
design
Geometry: sharp corners, thick rules, no effects


web_theme_aurora()
expressive
Effects: glass + glow on a dark canvas


web_theme_terminal()
expressive
Mono phosphor CRT


web_theme_newsprint()
editorial
Texture: warm grain, serif body


web_theme_blueprint()
expressive
Draft / grid: cyanotype


web_theme_synthwave()
expressive
Neon


web_theme_dwarven()
editorial
Fantasy: Cinzel display serif



tabviz(data, label = "study",
       columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi")),
       theme = web_theme_nejm())
The 9 committed identities — each owns one expressive axis:

web_theme_nejm()


web_theme_ledger()


web_theme_brutalist()


web_theme_aurora()


web_theme_terminal()


web_theme_newsprint()


web_theme_blueprint()


web_theme_synthwave()


web_theme_dwarven()
All preset builders take no arguments and return a fully resolved WebTheme. nejm is the default. (A previous 27-preset roster was culled to these 9; a deleted look’s brand is a set_brand() recolor away, its full personality a web_theme(…) custom build away.)

title: Themes
Themes
text:
web_theme()
Custom-theme constructor. Pass Tier-1 inputs as direct arguments; everything else derives. Resolves the theme before returning.
web_theme(
  brand = "#0099CC",          # required: identity seed
  accent = NULL,              # engagement (hover/selected/callouts); mirrors brand
  polarity = "light",         # "light" | "dark" (L-reflection)
  monochrome = FALSE,         # neutral ramp rides the brand hue (B8)
  categorical = "okabe_ito",  # data palette scheme
  sequential = "viridis",
  diverging = "rdbu",
  status_positive = NULL, status_negative = NULL,
  status_warning = NULL,  status_info = NULL,
  fonts_body = NULL, fonts_display = NULL, fonts_mono = NULL,
  density = "comfortable",    # "compact" | "comfortable" | "spacious"
  header_style = "light",     # "light" | "tint" | "bold"
  first_column_style = "default",  # "default" | "bold"
  web_fonts = NULL,
  name = "custom"
)









Argument
Type
Default
Description




brand
hex
DEFAULT_BRAND_HEX
Required identity seed. Powers the brand ramp and identity tokens.


accent
hex
NULL
Engagement layer (hover, selected, callouts). Mirrors brand when unset.


paper / ink
hex / oklch
NULL
Light-end and dark-end neutral anchors; the neutral ramp interpolates between them.


marks
list
NULL
Viz-mark identity: point_shape, interval_weight.


effects
list
NULL
Glow / gradient shell / elevation / glass / caption, header, title styles.


mode
string
"standard"
"standard" / "high-contrast" / "reduced-transparency" — accessibility, orthogonal to polarity.


polarity / monochrome
string / flag
"light" / FALSE
L-reflection axis; brand-hued neutral ramp. Use [tint_from_brand()] to nudge hues instead.


categorical / sequential / diverging
string
scheme names
Data-palette scheme references.


status_*
hex
NULL
Optional status color overrides (positive/negative/warning/info).


fonts_body / fonts_display / fonts_mono
string
NULL
Font stacks; fonts_display mirrors fonts_body when unset.


density
string
"comfortable"
"compact" / "comfortable" / "spacious".


header_style
string
"light"
Header chrome variant: "light" / "tint" / "bold".


first_column_style
string
"default"
Label-column variant: "default" / "bold".


web_fonts
list
NULL
Optional web_font() declarations to embed.


name
string
"custom"
Theme name (shown in the in-widget switcher and in source export).



To start from a preset instead of defaults, chain setters off a web_theme_*() builder (e.g. web_theme_nejm() |> set_brand("#1F3A5F")).
Example — navy editorial theme with bold headers:
custom <- web_theme(
  name = "navy-bold",
  brand        = "#1F3A5F",
  accent       = "#B08938",
  fonts_body    = "Charter, Georgia, serif",
  density      = "comfortable",
  header_style = "bold"
)

title: Themes
text:
Theme modifiers
All modifiers operate on a WebTheme and return a new (re-resolved) WebTheme. Chainable via the pipe.

set_inputs()
Batch-update Tier-1 inputs and re-resolve. Accepts any [ThemeInputs] field by name; cascade-aware (derived tokens refresh, explicit pins survive). For a single input prefer the focused setter (set_brand(), set_accent(), set_density(), …); use set_inputs() to change several at once.
set_inputs(
  theme,
  brand, accent, paper, ink, polarity, monochrome, mode,
  categorical, sequential, diverging,
  status_positive, status_negative, status_warning, status_info,
  fonts_body, fonts_display, fonts_mono,
  density
)








Argument
Type
Description




theme
WebTheme
Theme to modify


brand
hex
Identity seed (markers, identity tokens). Derives the brand ramp


accent
hex
Engagement layer — hover, selected, semantic callouts, selection edge. Mirrors brand when unset


paper / ink
hex / oklch
Light-end / dark-end neutral anchors for the neutral ramp


mode
"standard" / "high-contrast" / "reduced-transparency"
Accessibility mode


polarity / monochrome
string / flag
L-reflection axis; brand-hued neutrals


categorical / sequential / diverging
string
Data-palette scheme names


status_positive, status_negative, status_warning, status_info
hex
Semantic status colors


fonts_body
string
Primary CSS font-family


fonts_display
string
Optional title face; mirrors fonts_body when unset


fonts_mono
string
Optional monospace face


density
string
"compact" / "comfortable" / "spacious"




Identity vocabulary: brand is the input name; primary / primary_deep are the resolved surface tokens it derives (along with secondary and the per-series slot anchors). To pin a resolved token directly — e.g. a specific series fill — use set_theme_field(c("series", 1L, "fill"), "#…").



Variant setters
Single-purpose fluent setters (the former bundled set_variants() was retired in favor of one setter per knob, matching set_brand() / set_accent() / set_density()).
set_density(theme, density)               # re-resolves the cascade
set_header_style(theme, header_style)     # variant selector (assign-only)
set_first_column_style(theme, first_column_style)








Setter
Values
Description




set_density()
"compact" / "comfortable" / "spacious"
Tier-1 input — re-resolves and drives every spacing token


set_header_style()
"light" / "tint" / "bold"
Post-resolution variant: light header on muted surface, primary-tinted band, or bold header on primary_deep


set_first_column_style()
"default" / "bold"
Post-resolution variant: emphasized first (label) column





set_spacing()
Per-token spacing override on top of the active density preset. Set what you care about; the rest cascade from density.
set_spacing(
  theme,
  row_height, header_height, padding, container_padding, axis_gap,
  column_group_padding, row_group_padding, cell_padding_x,
  footer_gap, title_subtitle_gap, bottom_margin, indent_per_level
)
All values are numeric (pixels).






NoteLive aspect-driven row_height in the widget



When the in-widget aspect-ratio slider is engaged, the lever ladder scales row_height to absorb the height delta. The Spacing settings tab reads the effective (post-lever-ladder) value, banner-flagged “Row height (aspect)”. Editing the field clears the aspect target so the user-typed value pins. See Themes guide — aspect-aware spacing.
Other tokens (header_height, gaps, paddings) keep the value set_spacing() writes; only row_height is live-driven by the slider.




set_theme_field()
Generic deep setter. Walks the theme tree by path and replaces the leaf with value. Assigning under inputs re-resolves; other paths pin directly.
set_theme_field(theme, path, value)







Argument
Description




theme
A WebTheme


path
Character or mixed character/integer vector. Strings are property names; integers index list-typed properties (currently only series).


value
New value for the leaf field



Examples:
# Pin row-group L1 background regardless of theme
theme |> set_theme_field(c("row_group", "L1", "bg"), "#EEEEEE")

# Override series 2's stroke without touching anything else
theme |> set_theme_field(c("series", 2L, "stroke"), "#444444")

# Set Tier 1 inputs surgically
theme |> set_theme_field(c("inputs", "brand"), "#FF0000")

# Tweak axis config
theme |> set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE)
theme |> set_theme_field(c("axis", "tick_count"), 5)

# Pin layout config
theme |> set_theme_field(c("layout", "container_border_radius"), 4)
Re-resolution preserves explicit overrides on subsequent edits. set_inputs(brand = "...") after pinning surface.base will refresh series colors but leave surface.base pinned.

title: Themes
text:
Switching themes on a spec



Verb
Operates on
Purpose




set_theme()
WebSpec, htmlwidget, proxy
Swap to a preset name or WebTheme object


selectable_themes()
WebSpec, htmlwidget
Curate the in-widget theme switcher



# Apply by name
plot |> set_theme("nejm")

# Apply by object
custom <- web_theme(brand = "#1F3A5F")
plot |> set_theme(custom)

title: Themes
text:
Worked examples

Editorial theme with custom Tier 1 input
my_editorial <- web_theme(
  name = "my-editorial",
  brand        = "#0066CC",
  accent       = "#CC9900",
  fonts_body    = "Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif",
  fonts_display = "Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif",
  density      = "comfortable"
)

# Swap presets, then tweak
custom <- web_theme_nejm() |>
  set_brand("#0066CC") |>
  set_density("compact") |>
  set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE)


High-contrast print theme
print_theme <- web_theme(
  name = "print",
  brand        = "#000000",
  accent       = "#000000",
  fonts_body    = "'Times New Roman', serif",
  fonts_display = "'Times New Roman', serif",
  density      = "compact"
) |>
  set_theme_field(c("row_group", "L1", "text", "weight"), 700) |>
  set_theme_field(c("row_group", "L1", "border_bottom"), TRUE)


Dark dashboard theme
dark_theme <- web_theme(
  name = "dashboard-dark",
  polarity     = "dark",
  brand        = "#10B981",
  accent       = "#10B981",
  density      = "spacious",
  header_style = "bold"
)

title: Themes
text:
Theme structure (S7)
A WebTheme is an S7 object with the following top-level properties. For the conceptual model and how the cascades compose, see Styling Cascade and S7 Architecture.









Property
Type
Tier
Description




name
string
—
Theme identifier


inputs
ThemeInputs
1
Customer-facing inputs


variants
ThemeVariants
1
Per-table toggles


surface
Surfaces
2
base / muted / raised


content
Content
2
primary / secondary / muted / inverse


divider
Dividers
2
subtle / strong


accent
AccentRoles
2
default / muted / tint_subtle / tint_medium


status
StatusColors
2
positive / negative / warning / info


series
list<SlotBundle>
2
Per-anchor 7-field bundle


summary
SlotBundle
2
Pooled-effect diamond bundle


text
TextRoles
2
8 named role bundles


spacing
SpacingTokens
2
12 density-derived numerics


annotation
AnnotationCluster
3
title / subtitle / caption / footnote


header
HeaderCluster
3
light + bold variants


column_group
ColumnGroupCluster
3
Column-group header


row_group
RowGroupCluster
3
L1 / L2 / L3 tiers

RowCluster
3
base / alt / hover / selected + the five semantic bundles (emphasis, muted, accent, bold, fill)

FirstColumnCluster

PlotScaffold

Per-mark slot-bundle recipes

AxisConfig

Forest x-axis range/ticks/grid

Layout

Plot width, container border

title: LOTR (Easter Egg)
text:
NotePre-release easter egg



The theme and examples on this page (web_theme_dwarven(), plus a set of bespoke pictogram glyphs — used below: gem, ale_mug, pickaxe, sword, jar, pie, mushroom, plus more in glyph_registry_names()) are pre-release demonstrations. They are likely to be removed before CRAN submission and republished as standalone source on the package blog. Don’t build load-bearing dashboards on top of them.
These three tables exercise: the new col_pictogram() and col_ring() columns, threshold-driven col_badge() shapes, theme-declared web fonts (Cinzel, EB Garamond), and bespoke SVG glyphs designed to read at 14 px.
A late evening, an unexpected guest, three ledgers from three peoples — all dressed in the dwarven preset. Each table pairs a complete editorial-register theme — palette, typography, glyphs — with content that winks at its source material.

title: LOTR (Easter Egg)
section: Erebor — Vein Yields, Hall of Records
text:
Erebor — Vein Yields, Hall of Records
Iron-clad ledger of every shaft beneath the Lonely Mountain. By order of Thorin son of Thráin. The Drum Chamber, abandon status, has been redacted from the King’s quarterly briefing; he requests no further inquiry.
Pictograms in use: gem (gold carts pulled this quarter, count mode), ale_mug (crew songs per shift, rating mode — five mugs because the Songs of Smithing are five), pickaxe (axes dulled, scaled to a 100-axe ceiling). The Balrog risk column uses a circular col_badge() with a status threshold scale; Drum Chamber’s score of 9 lands firmly in the negative bin.


Code
dwarven_data <- data.frame(
  shaft        = c("Front Hall", "Hall of Thrór", "Black Lode", "King's Vein",
                   "Mithril Seam", "Eastern Drift", "Deep Foundry", "Drum Chamber"),
  depth_m      = c(40, 280, 540, 720, 980, 1240, 1620, 2100),
  q3_tonnage   = c(142, 318, 412, 88, 24, 488, 612, 0),
  gold_carts   = c(3, 7, 6, 9, 4, 5, 2, 0),
  songs        = c(4, 5, 4, 5, 4, 3, 2, 0),
  axes_dulled  = c(12, 18, 15, 11, 22, 28, 35, 99),
  balrog_risk  = c(0L, 0L, 1L, 1L, 2L, 3L, 4L, 9L),
  status       = c("singing", "yielding", "yielding", "yielding",
                   "watch", "watch", "hazardous", "abandon"),
  yield_ratio  = c(1.18, 1.42, 1.31, 0.92, 0.78, 1.65, 1.84, 0.04),
  yield_lo     = c(0.95, 1.20, 1.10, 0.74, 0.55, 1.32, 1.42, 0.01),
  yield_hi     = c(1.42, 1.65, 1.55, 1.12, 1.10, 2.05, 2.40, 0.18)
)

tabviz(
  dwarven_data,
  label = "shaft",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("depth_m", header = "Depth (m)", decimals = 0),
    # Bar without `color` → picks up theme primary (hammered bronze).
    col_bar("q3_tonnage", header = "Q3 tonnage"),
    # Pictograms without `color` → pick up theme secondary (warm gold).
    # No hex literals in the call — the cascade does the work.
    col_pictogram("gold_carts", header = "Gold carts", glyph = "gem"),
    col_pictogram("songs", header = "Songs / shift",
                  glyph = "ale_mug", max_glyphs = 5),
    col_pictogram("axes_dulled", header = "Axes dulled",
                  glyph = "pickaxe",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 5, min_value = 0, max_value = 100),
    # Threshold badge: 2 thresholds → status palette (positive/warning/negative).
    col_badge("balrog_risk", header = "Balrog risk",
              shape = "circle", thresholds = c(2, 5)),
    # Categorical badge with semantic VARIANTS instead of hex literals —
    # each label maps to a status token and pulls from theme.status.
    col_badge("status",
              variants = c("singing"   = "success",
                           "yielding"  = "info",
                           "watch"     = "warning",
                           "hazardous" = "error",
                           "abandon"   = "muted")),
    viz_forest(point = "yield_ratio", lower = "yield_lo", upper = "yield_hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Yield ratio (Q3 vs Q2)",
               show_header = FALSE)
  ),
  title = "Erebor — Vein Yields, Hall of Records",
  subtitle = "Iron-clad ledger of every shaft. T.A. 2941, Halimath.",
  theme = web_theme_dwarven()
)

title: LOTR (Easter Egg)
section: Rivendell — Council of Elrond, Attendee Manifest
text:
Rivendell — Council of Elrond, Attendee Manifest
Eight peoples. One ring. The minutes are in Sindarin and refreshments are at the second eagle. Boromir spoke for forty-two minutes; the minutes are redacted at the request of the chair.
Three pictogram columns each tell a different micro-story: star for wisdom (a rating), sword for valor (also a rating; Boromir is unsurprisingly five-of-five), and person for times interrupted Boromir (a count — Gimli is leading the pack at six). The col_ring() for ring temptation is keyed to 0.33 and 0.66 with no explicit colors — the cascade resolves to the theme’s status palette (positive / warning / negative). Boromir’s 86 % lands well inside the negative arc.


Code
elvish_data <- data.frame(
  delegate    = c("Elrond Half-elven", "Gandalf the Grey", "Aragorn II Elessar",
                  "Legolas Greenleaf", "Gimli son of Glóin", "Boromir of Gondor",
                  "Frodo Baggins", "Samwise Gamgee"),
  race        = c("Elf", "Maia", "Man", "Elf", "Dwarf", "Man", "Hobbit", "Hobbit"),
  wisdom      = c(5, 5, 4, 3, 3, 2, 4, 3),
  valor       = c(4, 5, 5, 5, 5, 5, 4, 5),
  tempt       = c(0.18, 0.32, 0.12, 0.08, 0.14, 0.86, 0.64, 0.22),
  words_12h   = c(1240, 890, 720, 320, 480, 1880, 380, 220),
  interrupted = c(0, 2, 1, 3, 6, 0, 1, 4),
  lembas      = c(4, 3, 6, 5, 12, 8, 9, 14),
  joins       = c("HOSTS", "YES", "YES", "YES", "YES", "YES", "BEARER", "GARDENER"),
  vote_for    = c(0.95, 1.05, 0.88, 1.10, 0.92, 1.32, 0.78, 0.85),
  vote_lo     = c(0.78, 0.85, 0.72, 0.92, 0.78, 1.10, 0.62, 0.68),
  vote_hi     = c(1.12, 1.25, 1.05, 1.30, 1.08, 1.55, 0.98, 1.05),
  # The Ring-bearer is the single row that fires the accent treatment
  # (theme.row.accent → twilight gold). Everything else stays at theme
  # default — no per-call hex literals.
  row_accent  = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE)
)

tabviz(
  elvish_data,
  label = "delegate",
  row_accent = "row_accent",
  columns = list(
    # Race as plain text — five arbitrary categories don't fit any
    # semantic palette; hardcoding five hexes would defeat the showcase.
    col_text("race", header = "Race"),
    # Pictograms drop their hardcoded `color` — pick up theme secondary
    # (mithril silver). Frodo's row, marked accent, switches the glyphs
    # to twilight gold via the row.accent semantic override.
    col_pictogram("wisdom", header = "Wisdom",
                  glyph = "star", max_glyphs = 5),
    col_pictogram("valor", header = "Valor",
                  glyph = "sword", max_glyphs = 5),
    # Ring tempt: 2 thresholds → 3 stops → status palette. Boromir's
    # 0.86 lands in the danger bin on its own; no per-call hex needed.
    col_ring("tempt", header = "Ring tempt",
             thresholds = c(0.33, 0.66)),
    col_bar("words_12h", header = "Words spoken (12h)"),
    col_pictogram("interrupted", header = "Interrupted Boromir",
                  glyph = "person"),
    col_pictogram("lembas", header = "Lembas eaten",
                  glyph = "leaf",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 8),
    # Joins as variants — info / success status, plus "default" → accent
    # for the Bearer (twilight gold), pinned to a single semantic axis.
    col_badge("joins", header = "Joins fellowship",
              variants = c("HOSTS"    = "info",
                           "YES"      = "success",
                           "BEARER"   = "default",
                           "GARDENER" = "success")),
    viz_forest(point = "vote_for", lower = "vote_lo", upper = "vote_hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Council vote weight",
               show_header = FALSE)
  ),
  title = "Rivendell — Council of Elrond, Attendee Manifest",
  subtitle = "Eight peoples. One ring. The minutes are in Sindarin.",
  theme = web_theme_dwarven()
)

title: LOTR (Easter Egg)
text:
Hobbiton — Pantry & Meal Audit, Halimath 1418
A respectable hobbit’s larder is never half-full; it is half-empty only between meals (of which there are seven). The Sackville-Baggins household, as always, is showing concerning second-breakfast deficiency. Pity tea-baskets have been dispatched.
jar (pantry stock, rating), pie (second-breakfast quality, rating), mushroom (mushroom haul, count) — three glyphs that ought to be permanently on every hobbit’s letterhead. The adventures badge uses outline mode (outline = TRUE) — a hobbit’s relationship with adventure is, after all, low-emphasis.


Code
hobbit_data <- data.frame(
  household       = c("Baggins of Bag End", "Took of Great Smials",
                      "Brandybuck of Brandy Hall", "Gamgee of Bagshot Row",
                      "Sackville-Baggins", "Cotton of Bywater",
                      "Boffin of Overhill", "Proudfoot of Westfarthing"),
  smial           = c("S.W. #1", "Tuckborough", "Buckland", "S.W. #3",
                      "Sackville", "Bywater", "Overhill", "Westfarthing"),
  pantry          = c(5, 5, 4, 4, 2, 4, 5, 4),
  pipeweed_lb     = c(3.2, 4.0, 2.6, 1.6, 0.5, 1.2, 2.8, 2.0),
  meals_per_day   = c(7.0, 7.0, 6.5, 7.0, 4.5, 6.5, 6.0, 6.5),
  second_brkfst   = c(5, 5, 5, 5, 1, 4, 4, 5),
  mushrooms       = c(8, 12, 6, 9, 0, 4, 5, 7),
  adventures      = c("YES (ONE)", "MANY", "YES (ONE)", "YES (ONE)",
                      "NONE", "NONE", "NONE", "NONE"),
  satiation       = c(1.05, 1.12, 0.98, 1.02, 0.75, 0.95, 1.08, 0.92),
  satiation_lo    = c(0.92, 1.00, 0.85, 0.88, 0.62, 0.82, 0.95, 0.78),
  satiation_hi    = c(1.18, 1.25, 1.10, 1.15, 0.88, 1.08, 1.20, 1.05),
  # Adventurous Tooks pop in accent (autumn rust); the Sackville-Bagginses
  # fade in muted (problematic relatives). Everything else at theme default.
  row_accent      = c(FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE),
  row_muted       = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE)
)

tabviz(
  hobbit_data,
  label = "household",
  row_accent = "row_accent",
  row_muted  = "row_muted",
  columns = list(
    col_text("smial", header = "Smial"),
    # Pictograms without `color` → theme secondary (garden green —
    # Sam's vegetables). Took's row picks up accent override automatically.
    col_pictogram("pantry", header = "Pantry fill",
                  glyph = "jar", max_glyphs = 5),
    # Bar without `color` → theme primary (warm clay — Bag End brick).
    col_bar("pipeweed_lb", header = "Pipe-weed (lb)"),
    col_numeric("meals_per_day", header = "Meals/day", decimals = 1),
    col_pictogram("second_brkfst", header = "2nd brkfst",
                  glyph = "pie", max_glyphs = 5),
    col_pictogram("mushrooms", header = "Mushrooms",
                  glyph = "mushroom",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 8),
    # Adventures via variants — success for the typical one-time hobbit
    # jaunt, default → accent for the unusual MANY (the Tooks), muted for
    # NONE.
    col_badge("adventures",
              variants = c("YES (ONE)" = "success",
                           "MANY"      = "default",
                           "NONE"      = "muted"),
              outline = TRUE),
    viz_forest(point = "satiation", lower = "satiation_lo", upper = "satiation_hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Satiation index",
               show_header = FALSE)
  ),
  title = "Hobbiton — Pantry & Meal Audit, Halimath 1418",
  subtitle = "A respectable hobbit's larder is never half-full.",
  theme = web_theme_dwarven()
)

title: LOTR (Easter Egg)
text:
Bring your own theme
The dwarven theme above is picked up automatically by the in-widget theme switcher (look for the editorial tab) when enable_themes = "default" — package_themes() returns it under the editorial category. To use it explicitly:
tabviz(
  data, label = "x",
  columns = list(...),
  theme = web_theme_dwarven()
)
The themes declare their Google Fonts via web_fonts = list(web_font(family, url), …). The widget injects <link rel="stylesheet"> tags on mount and dedupes across multiple widgets on the same page. rsvg/PNG export uses the system font fallback (Georgia, serif) since rsvg does not fetch web fonts at render time — install the family locally for high-fidelity offline export.
The bespoke pictogram glyphs (pickaxe, harp, mushroom, …) live in glyph_registry_names() alongside the core set (person, dot, star, …) and are usable from any theme — col_pictogram(field, glyph = "harp") works on the nejm preset just as well, it just doesn’t match.

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
Two recently-shipped column types — col_pictogram() (repeated glyphs, count or rating) and col_ring() (donut gauges with thresholds) — together cover the “small visual quantum” niche that bare numbers, percentages, and bars can’t hit cleanly. This page walks every meaningful mode of each, then composes them on a worked dashboard.
For the full argument list see the Reference; for theme cascading into glyph color see Visualization Styling.

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_pictogram() — count mode
The default. glyph repeats round(value) times per row. Use it when the metric is a small integer count and the reader benefits from visual heft per item.


Code
counts <- data.frame(
  team    = c("Backend", "Frontend", "Platform", "Data"),
  prs     = c(3, 7, 4, 2),
  reviews = c(12, 18, 9, 5)
)

tabviz(counts, label = "team",
  columns = list(
    col_pictogram("prs", header = "PRs merged",
                  glyph = "person"),
    col_pictogram("reviews", header = "Reviews",
                  glyph = "person",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer")
  ),
  title = "Sprint summary — count mode"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_pictogram() — rating mode
Pass max_glyphs to switch to rating mode: render exactly max_glyphs glyphs per row, the first value filled, the remainder muted. Use it when the metric is a bounded score.


Code
ratings <- data.frame(
  product = c("Widget Pro", "Widget Lite", "Widget Plus", "Widget Max"),
  quality = c(4.5, 3.8, 4.8, 4.1),
  service = c(5,   3,   5,   4),
  perf    = c(3,   4,   5,   3)
)

tabviz(ratings, label = "product",
  columns = list(
    col_pictogram("quality", header = "Quality",
                  glyph = "star", max_glyphs = 5,
                  half_glyphs = TRUE,
                  value_label = "trailing", label_decimals = 1),
    col_pictogram("service", header = "Service",
                  glyph = "star", max_glyphs = 5),
    col_pictogram("perf",    header = "Performance",
                  glyph = "star", max_glyphs = 5)
  ),
  title = "Product ratings — rating mode (max_glyphs = 5)"
)






half_glyphs = TRUE allows a half-filled boundary glyph for fractional values (4.5 stars renders as four full + one half). Without it, the value rounds.

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
Range remapping (rating mode + min_value / max_value)
When the input range isn’t [0, max_glyphs], pass both min_value and max_value to remap. Useful for percentages, scores on arbitrary scales, etc.


Code
risk <- data.frame(
  asset       = c("AAA Bonds", "Corp Bond", "Equity Idx", "Emerging", "Crypto"),
  risk_score  = c(8, 22, 48, 67, 92)        # 0–100 scale
)

tabviz(risk, label = "asset",
  columns = list(
    # Map [0, 100] into [0, 5] skulls
    col_pictogram("risk_score", header = "Risk",
                  glyph = "skull", max_glyphs = 5,
                  min_value = 0, max_value = 100,
                  color = "#dc2626", empty_color = "#e5e7eb",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer")
  ),
  title = "Risk score — remapped from [0, 100] into [0, 5]"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
Per-row glyph selection via glyph_field
Pass glyph as a named character vector and pair with glyph_field to pick a different glyph per row. Useful when the same metric is best read with category-specific iconography.


Code
energy <- data.frame(
  source     = c("Solar farm", "Wind farm", "Hydro plant",
                 "Geothermal", "Biomass"),
  capacity   = c(8, 12, 6, 4, 3),
  category   = c("solar", "wind", "hydro", "geothermal", "biomass")
)

tabviz(energy, label = "source",
  columns = list(
    col_pictogram("capacity", header = "Capacity (units)",
                  glyph = c(solar      = "leaf",
                            wind       = "leaf",
                            hydro      = "leaf",
                            geothermal = "flame",
                            biomass    = "leaf"),
                  glyph_field = "category",
                  color = "#16a34a")
  ),
  title = "Renewable capacity — per-row glyph selection"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
Layout — row vs stack
Default layout is row — glyphs lay horizontally. layout = "stack" piles them vertically. Best in tall rows (rows that already include a viz_* column) where horizontal real estate is at a premium and vertical space is plentiful.


Code
restaurants <- data.frame(
  spot       = c("Cafe A", "Bistro B", "Diner C", "Trattoria D"),
  meals      = c(3, 4, 2, 5),
  hr         = c(0.85, 1.05, 0.92, 0.78),
  lo         = c(0.62, 0.78, 0.66, 0.55),
  hi         = c(1.16, 1.42, 1.27, 1.10)
)

tabviz(restaurants, label = "spot",
  columns = list(
    col_pictogram("meals", header = "Meals",
                  glyph = "pie", max_glyphs = 5,
                  layout = "stack",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               null_value = 1, scale = "log",
               axis_label = "Effect"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_height = 56,                               # tall rows so stack fits
  title = "Layout = 'stack' alongside a viz column"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_ring() — single color, fraction range
Default: input range [0, 1], single color from the theme accent. Best for “% to target”-style metrics.


Code
goals <- data.frame(
  initiative = c("Onboarding", "Activation", "Retention", "Expansion", "Win-back"),
  q3_target  = c(0.85, 0.72, 0.91, 0.58, 0.31)
)

tabviz(goals, label = "initiative",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("q3_target", header = "% to Q3 target",
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0)
  ),
  title = "Single-color ring — default theme accent fill"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_ring() — one threshold (binary palette)
One thresholds value splits the range into two bins. With no explicit color, the package picks a sensible warning / safe palette automatically.


Code
budgets <- data.frame(
  team       = c("Engineering", "Marketing", "Sales", "Ops", "G&A"),
  spent      = c(0.42, 0.78, 0.91, 0.55, 0.38)
)

tabviz(budgets, label = "team",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("spent", header = "Budget spent",
             thresholds = 0.80,                # over-budget after 80%
             label_format = "percent")
  ),
  title = "One-threshold ring — auto-palette (under / over)"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_ring() — two thresholds (status palette)
Two thresholds → three bins → the cascade resolves to the theme’s status palette: positive for the lowest band, warning for the middle, negative for the highest. No hex literals needed; the colors come from the theme.


Code
risk_ring <- data.frame(
  project   = c("Apollo", "Bedrock", "Cypress", "Drift", "Echo", "Falcon"),
  risk      = c(0.12, 0.35, 0.51, 0.68, 0.78, 0.91)
)

tabviz(risk_ring, label = "project",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("risk", header = "Risk score",
             thresholds = c(0.33, 0.66),       # auto positive / warning / negative
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0)
  ),
  title = "Two-threshold ring — auto status palette"
)






Switch the theme to JAMA, Lancet, or any custom theme and the colors restyle automatically — status_* is a Tier 1 input.

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_ring() — explicit colors with thresholds
Pass an explicit color vector when you want to pin specific shades. Length must equal length(thresholds) + 1.


Code
score_card <- data.frame(
  metric  = c("DAU", "WAU", "MAU", "L7d", "L30d"),
  value   = c(0.18, 0.42, 0.71, 0.55, 0.83)
)

tabviz(score_card, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("value", header = "Engagement",
             thresholds = c(0.30, 0.60),
             color = c("#dc2626", "#f59e0b", "#16a34a"),
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0)
  ),
  title = "Explicit colors with thresholds"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
col_ring() — sizes
Three sizes are available: "sm" (≈18 px), "base" (≈24 px, default), "lg" (≈32 px). Larger rings may exceed a normal rowHeight — best used in tall rows that already contain a viz_* column or when row_height is bumped up.


Code
sizes <- data.frame(
  metric    = c("Small ring", "Base ring", "Large ring"),
  value_sm  = c(0.65, 0.65, 0.65),
  value_md  = c(0.65, 0.65, 0.65),
  value_lg  = c(0.65, 0.65, 0.65)
)

tabviz(sizes, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("value_sm", header = "sm",   size = "sm",
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0),
    col_ring("value_md", header = "base", size = "base",
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0),
    col_ring("value_lg", header = "lg",   size = "lg",
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0)
  ),
  row_height = 44,
  title = "Ring sizes side by side"
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
section: Theme cascade — semantic row tokens reach into glyphs
text:
Theme cascade — semantic row tokens reach into glyphs
row_accent, row_emphasis, row_muted, row_bold, row_fill all reach into pictogram and ring fills the same way they reach into forest markers. Tag a row with the semantic intent; the cascade resolves the color from the theme.


Code
escalations <- data.frame(
  account     = c("Acme Corp", "Bedrock LLC", "Cosmic Inc",
                  "Delta Co",  "Eclipse Ltd"),
  health      = c(0.88, 0.42, 0.65, 0.91, 0.18),
  open_tix    = c(2, 12, 6, 1, 18),
  is_critical = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE),  # row_accent
  is_top      = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE,  FALSE)  # row_fill
)

tabviz(escalations, label = "account",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("health", header = "Health",
             thresholds = c(0.40, 0.75),
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0),
    col_pictogram("open_tix", header = "Open tickets",
                  glyph = "person",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 12)
  ),
  row_accent = "is_critical",
  row_fill   = "is_top",
  title    = "Account health — semantic tokens reach into glyph cells",
  subtitle = "Eclipse Ltd carries the accent; Delta Co carries the fill."
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
Putting it together — survey-feedback dashboard
A six-row dashboard that exercises every mode covered above. Each segment shows: NPS as a ring (auto status palette), promoter / detractor counts as horizontal pictograms, CSAT as a remapped rating-mode pictogram, and a 5-quarter trend sparkline for context.


Code
feedback <- data.frame(
  segment        = c("Enterprise", "Mid-market", "SMB",
                     "Self-serve",  "Education", "Public"),
  nps            = c(0.62, 0.48, 0.31, 0.08, 0.74, 0.44),
  csat_5pt       = c(4.5,  4.1,  3.7,  3.2,  4.7,  3.9),
  promoters      = c(11,   8,    6,    3,    13,   7),
  detractors     = c(2,    3,    8,    14,   1,    4),
  trend          = I(list(
    c(78, 80, 82, 85, 87), c(72, 75, 78, 80, 82),
    c(65, 68, 70, 72, 74), c(60, 62, 64, 65, 66),
    c(82, 85, 87, 88, 89), c(70, 73, 75, 77, 78)
  )),
  is_top         = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE,  FALSE),
  is_concern     = c(FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE,  FALSE, FALSE)
)

tabviz(feedback, label = "segment",
  columns = list(
    col_ring("nps", header = "NPS",
             thresholds = c(0.20, 0.50),                  # auto status palette
             label_format = "percent", label_decimals = 0),
    col_pictogram("csat_5pt", header = "CSAT (5pt)",
                  glyph = "star", max_glyphs = 5,
                  half_glyphs = TRUE,
                  value_label = "trailing", label_decimals = 1),
    col_pictogram("promoters", header = "Promoters",
                  glyph = "person",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 14),
    col_pictogram("detractors", header = "Detractors",
                  glyph = "person",
                  value_label = "trailing", label_format = "integer",
                  max_glyphs = 14,
                  color = "#dc2626"),
    col_sparkline("trend", "5q trend")
  ),
  row_fill   = "is_top",
  row_accent = "is_concern",
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title    = "Customer feedback — pictograms + rings end-to-end",
  subtitle = "Education leads (row_fill); Self-serve flagged for response-rate concern (row_accent)."
)

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
When to reach for what







Metric shape
Reach for




Small integer count (1–15)
col_pictogram(...) count mode


Bounded rating (1–5, 0–10)
col_pictogram(..., max_glyphs = ...)


Fractional metric in [0, 1]
col_ring(...)


[0, 1] metric with “good / bad” cutoff
col_ring(..., thresholds = X)


[0, 1] metric with “low / mid / high” bands
col_ring(..., thresholds = c(X, Y)) (auto status palette)


Anything outside [0, 1]
Add min_value / max_value


Want category-specific iconography
col_pictogram(glyph = c(...), glyph_field = "...")


Need the value next to the glyph
value_label = "trailing" (or "leading")


Glyph piles in a tall row
layout = "stack"

title: Pictograms & Rings
text:
See also

Reference → col_pictogram() and col_ring() — every argument.
Domain Dashboards — sports / survey / portfolio examples that use these in context.
LOTR (Easter Egg) — three full-page demos using the bespoke glyph set.

title: Feature Showcases
text: Spotlights for individual tabviz features. Each example highlights one feature family — column types, statistical models, distribution viz, theming, conditional styling, hierarchical splits — and combines them in the closing “Full Monty” smoke test.

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Rich Column Types
A comprehensive showcase of column types — heatmaps, progress bars, currency, dates, badges, stars, sparklines, bars, ranges, and icons — using a project management dataset:


Code
project_data <- data.frame(
  task = c("Backend API", "Frontend UI", "Database Migration",
           "Auth System", "Deployment Pipeline", "Documentation"),
  status = c("Active", "Active", "Completed", "At Risk", "On Hold", "Active"),
  priority = c(5, 4, 3, 5, 2, 3),
  budget = c(85000, 62000, 41000, 55000, 28000, 15000),
  spent_pct = c(0.65, 0.42, 1.00, 0.88, 0.15, 0.30),
  health = c(88, 75, 100, 45, 60, 82),
  start_date = as.Date(c("2025-01-15", "2025-02-01", "2024-11-01",
                          "2025-03-10", "2025-04-01", "2025-03-15")),
  est_low = c(30, 20, 15, 25, 10, 8),
  est_high = c(45, 35, 20, 40, 18, 12),
  velocity = I(list(
    c(8, 10, 12, 14, 15, 18, 20, 22),
    c(5, 8, 10, 12, 10, 8, 12, 14),
    c(15, 18, 20, 22, 25, 28, 30, 32),
    c(12, 10, 8, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2),
    c(2, 3, 4, 3, 2, 1, 1, 0),
    c(3, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 15)
  )),
  flag = c("ok", "ok", "done", "blocked", "paused", "ok")
)

tabviz(project_data, label = "task",
  columns = list(
    col_badge("status", "Status",
      variants = list(
        Active = "success", Completed = "info",
        `At Risk` = "error", `On Hold` = "warning"
      )),
    col_stars("priority", "Priority", max_stars = 5),
    col_currency("budget", "Budget", decimals = 0),
    col_progress("spent_pct", "Spent", max_value = 1),
    col_heatmap("health", "Health",
      palette = c("#fee2e2", "#dcfce7"),
      min_value = 0, max_value = 100, decimals = 0),
    col_date("start_date", "Started", format = "%b %Y"),
    col_range("est_low", "est_high", header = "Est. Days"),
    col_sparkline("velocity", "Velocity"),
    col_icon("flag", "Flag",
      mapping = list(ok = "\u2714", done = "\u2605", blocked = "\u2716", paused = "\u23f8"))
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Rich Column Types Showcase"
)

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Statistical Models
Forest plots aren’t just for hazard ratios — any point estimate with a confidence interval fits. Here we visualize regression coefficients from a linear model of fuel economy on mtcars, with null_value = 0 and a linear scale:


Code
model <- lm(mpg ~ cyl + disp + hp + wt + am, data = mtcars)
coefs <- summary(model)$coefficients
ci <- confint(model)

reg_data <- data.frame(
  term = rownames(coefs),
  estimate = coefs[, "Estimate"],
  std_error = coefs[, "Std. Error"],
  conf.low = ci[, 1],
  conf.high = ci[, 2],
  p.value = coefs[, "Pr(>|t|)"],
  row.names = NULL
) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)")

tabviz(reg_data,
  label = "term",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("estimate", "Estimate", decimals = 3),
    col_numeric("std_error", "SE", decimals = 3),
    viz_forest(point = "estimate", lower = "conf.low", upper = "conf.high",
               scale = "linear", null_value = 0,
               axis_label = "Coefficient (95% CI)"),
    col_interval("estimate", "conf.low", "conf.high", header = "95% CI"),
    col_pvalue("p.value", "P", stars = TRUE)
  ),
  marker_color = ~ case_when(
    conf.high < 0 ~ "#dc2626",
    conf.low > 0 ~ "#16a34a",
    TRUE ~ "#64748b"
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Linear Regression Coefficients",
  subtitle = "lm(mpg ~ cyl + disp + hp + wt + am, data = mtcars)",
  footnote = "Red = negative effect, green = positive effect, gray = not significant at alpha = 0.05."
)

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Distribution Visualizations
All three visualization types — viz_bar, viz_boxplot, and viz_violin — in one combined clinical site dashboard:


Code
set.seed(654)
clinical_dashboard <- data.frame(
  site = c("Boston", "London", "Tokyo", "Sydney", "Toronto"),
  enrollment = c(245, 189, 312, 156, 198),
  completion_rate = c(0.87, 0.92, 0.78, 0.95, 0.89),
  response_times = I(list(
    rnorm(50, 5.2, 1.1),
    rnorm(50, 4.8, 0.9),
    rnorm(50, 6.1, 1.5),
    rnorm(50, 4.5, 0.8),
    rnorm(50, 5.5, 1.2)
  )),
  satisfaction = I(list(
    c(rnorm(60, 8.0, 1.0), rnorm(20, 5, 1.5)),
    rnorm(80, 8.5, 0.7),
    c(rnorm(50, 7.0, 1.5), runif(20, 3, 9)),
    rnorm(75, 9.0, 0.5),
    c(rnorm(40, 7.5, 1.2), rnorm(30, 6, 1.8))
  ))
)

tabviz(clinical_dashboard, label = "site",
  columns = list(
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("enrollment"),
      header = "Enrolled",
      axis_range = c(0, 350)
    ),
    viz_bar(
      effect_bar("completion_rate"),
      header = "Completion",
      axis_range = c(0, 1)
    ),
    viz_boxplot(
      effect_boxplot(data = "response_times"),
      header = "Response Time (days)",
      show_outliers = TRUE
    ),
    viz_violin(
      effect_violin(data = "satisfaction"),
      header = "Satisfaction (1-10)",
      show_median = TRUE,
      show_quartiles = TRUE
    )
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Clinical Site Performance Dashboard"
)

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Column Groups & Split Plots
Combine col_group() headers with split_by for faceted views with organized columns:


Code
regional_data <- data.frame(
  study = rep(paste0("Study ", 1:4), 3),
  region = rep(c("North America", "Europe", "Asia Pacific"), each = 4),
  n = rep(c(1250, 980, 1560, 1100), 3),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.68, 0.91,
         0.78, 0.82, 0.75, 0.88,
         0.65, 0.71, 0.62, 0.79),
  lo = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.52, 0.75,
         0.62, 0.68, 0.58, 0.72,
         0.48, 0.55, 0.45, 0.62),
  hi = c(0.95, 1.03, 0.89, 1.10,
         0.98, 0.99, 0.97, 1.08,
         0.88, 0.92, 0.86, 1.01)
)

tabviz(regional_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_group("Study Info",
      col_n("n")
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_group("Results",
      col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
    )
  ),
  split_by = "region",
  shared_axis = TRUE,
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Treatment Effect by Region"
)

title: Feature Showcases
section: Pagination — long tables across multiple pages
text:
Pagination — long tables across multiple pages
Long meta-analyses or multi-cohort registries often need to span more rows than fit on a printed page. paginate = paginate_spec() (or one of the presets — paginate_letter(), paginate_a4(), paginate_slide()) breaks the table into pages: the HTML viewer renders one page at a time with prev/next controls and a continuous-mode toggle, and save_plot(*.pdf) emits a true multi-page PDF.


Code
set.seed(2026)
n_studies <- 72
cohorts <- c("Phase III RCT", "Real-world evidence", "Registry")
counts  <- c(24, 30, 18)

meta <- data.frame(
  study = c(
    sprintf("Trial %02d", 1:counts[1]),
    sprintf("RWE %02d",  1:counts[2]),
    sprintf("Reg %02d",  1:counts[3])
  ),
  cohort = rep(cohorts, times = counts),
  year   = sample(2010:2024, n_studies, replace = TRUE),
  n      = sample(c(120, 240, 480, 960, 1900, 3800), n_studies, replace = TRUE),
  hr     = round(rnorm(n_studies, 0.92, 0.18), 2),
  se     = round(runif(n_studies, 0.06, 0.20), 2)
)
meta$lower <- pmax(0.20, round(meta$hr - 1.96 * meta$se, 2))
meta$upper <- round(meta$hr + 1.96 * meta$se, 2)

tabviz(
  meta,
  label = "study",
  group = "cohort",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("year", "Year", decimals = 0),
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               header = "HR (95% CI)", scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard ratio"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "GLP-1 receptor agonists — pooled cardiovascular meta-analysis",
  subtitle = "72 studies across three cohorts; paginate at 30 rows per page",
  caption = "Use the prev/next controls below the plot to walk through pages, or switch to continuous mode to scroll.",
  paginate = paginate_letter()
)






paginate cascades into every split_by subview — each subview owns its own page set, paginating independently:


Code
set.seed(404)
regions <- c("North America", "Europe", "Asia-Pacific", "Latin America")
sites_per_region <- 18
n <- length(regions) * sites_per_region

split_data <- data.frame(
  site = unlist(lapply(seq_along(regions), function(i) {
    sprintf("%s site %02d", substr(regions[i], 1, 4), seq_len(sites_per_region))
  })),
  region = rep(regions, each = sites_per_region),
  arm    = rep(c("Treatment", "Placebo"), length.out = n),
  n      = sample(c(80, 120, 240, 400), n, replace = TRUE),
  rr     = round(rnorm(n, 0.85, 0.14), 2)
)
split_data$lower <- pmax(0.30, round(split_data$rr - 0.18, 2))
split_data$upper <- round(split_data$rr + 0.18, 2)

tabviz(
  split_data,
  label = "site",
  group = "arm",
  split_by = "region",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "rr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               header = "Risk ratio (95% CI)",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Risk ratio"),
    col_interval("rr", "lower", "upper", header = "RR")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Multi-region efficacy",
  subtitle = "Each region paginates independently at 10 rows per page",
  paginate = paginate_spec(rows = 10)
)






See Export → Pagination for the full configuration surface and PDF export details.

title: Feature Showcases
text:
All Theme Variants
Compare the seven built-in themes — four journal presets and three LOTR-flavored editorial themes:

Code
sample_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("A", "B", "C"),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.91),
  lo = c(0.55, 0.70, 0.75),
  hi = c(0.95, 1.03, 1.10)
)

make_plot <- function(theme_fn, name) {
  tabviz(sample_data, label = "study",
    columns = list(viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
                             scale = "log", null_value = 1)),
    theme = theme_fn(), title = name
  )
}

# The 9 committed identities — each owns a distinct expressive axis.
make_plot(web_theme_nejm,      "NEJM · Clinical")
make_plot(web_theme_ledger,    "Ledger · Color")
make_plot(web_theme_brutalist, "Brutalist · Geometry")
make_plot(web_theme_newsprint, "Newsprint · Texture")
make_plot(web_theme_blueprint, "Blueprint · Draft")
make_plot(web_theme_dwarven,   "Dwarven · Fantasy")
make_plot(web_theme_aurora,    "Aurora · Effects")
make_plot(web_theme_terminal,  "Terminal · Aliasing")
make_plot(web_theme_synthwave, "Synthwave · Neon")

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Conditional Styling
One comprehensive example showing formulas for row_bold, row_color, marker_shape, marker_color, and cell-level color/bold on columns:


Code
styling_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("RCT Alpha", "RCT Beta", "Cohort Gamma", "Cohort Delta",
            "Registry Epsilon", "RCT Zeta", "Cohort Eta"),
  design = c("RCT", "RCT", "Cohort", "Cohort", "Registry", "RCT", "Cohort"),
  hr = c(0.65, 0.78, 0.95, 1.12, 0.88, 0.72, 1.05),
  lo = c(0.50, 0.62, 0.78, 0.95, 0.72, 0.58, 0.88),
  hi = c(0.84, 0.98, 1.15, 1.32, 1.08, 0.89, 1.25),
  pval = c(0.001, 0.02, 0.15, 0.03, 0.08, 0.005, 0.22),
  quality = c(1.0, 0.9, 0.7, 0.6, 0.5, 0.95, 0.65)
)

tabviz(styling_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_text("design", "Design"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_numeric("hr", "HR",
      color = ~ ifelse(.x < 1, "#16a34a", "#dc2626"),
      bold = ~ .x < 0.8 | .x > 1.1
    ),
    col_pvalue("pval", "P",
      stars = TRUE,
      bold = ~ .x < 0.05
    ),
    col_badge("design", "Type")
  ),
  # Row-level styling
  row_bold = ~ pval < 0.01,
  row_color = ~ case_when(
    hi < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
    lo > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
    TRUE ~ "#64748b"
  ),
  # Marker styling by study design
  marker_shape = ~ case_when(
    design == "RCT" ~ "circle",
    design == "Cohort" ~ "square",
    TRUE ~ "diamond"
  ),
  marker_color = ~ case_when(
    hi < 1 ~ "#16a34a",
    lo > 1 ~ "#dc2626",
    TRUE ~ "#64748b"
  ),
  marker_opacity = "quality",
  marker_size = ~ ifelse(design == "RCT", 1.2, 1),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Conditional Styling Showcase",
  subtitle = "Row, cell, and marker styling with formulas"
)

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Fluent Theme Customization
Build a fully customized theme by chaining v2 modifiers off a base preset:


Code
custom_theme <- web_theme(
  name         = "warm",
  brand        = "#CA8A04",
  accent       = "#CA8A04",
  fonts_body   = "Georgia, serif",
  density      = "spacious"
) |>
  set_spacing(row_height = 32, cell_padding_x = 12) |>
  set_theme_field(c("plot", "point_size"), 8) |>
  set_theme_field(c("plot", "line_width"), 2) |>
  set_theme_field(c("layout", "container_border_radius"), 8) |>
  set_theme_field(c("row", "banding"), "row")

theme_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("PIONEER-1", "SUMMIT-2", "HORIZON-3", "ELEVATE-4", "APEX-5"),
  n = c(2450, 1890, 3200, 1560, 2100),
  hr = c(0.72, 0.85, 0.68, 0.91, 0.78),
  lo = c(0.58, 0.70, 0.52, 0.75, 0.62),
  hi = c(0.89, 1.03, 0.89, 1.10, 0.98),
  status = c("Significant", "Non-significant", "Significant",
             "Non-significant", "Significant")
)

tabviz(theme_data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_badge("status", "Result",
      variants = list(
        Significant = "success",
        `Non-significant` = "warning"
      ))
  ),
  marker_color = ~ ifelse(hi < 1, "#16a34a", "#64748b"),
  theme = custom_theme,
  title = "Custom Warm Theme via the v2 Inputs API",
  subtitle = "web_theme(brand = ...) + set_spacing() + set_theme_field()"
)












TipTheme modifiers (v2)



Four modifiers cover the surface: set_inputs() (Tier 1 inputs), set_density() / set_header_style() (per-table variants), set_spacing() (per-token spacing), and set_theme_field(theme, path, value) (generic deep setter). See Fluent API and Themes.

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Publication Templates
A single drop-in journal-style template demonstrating a row-grouped, column-grouped efficacy figure for a Lancet-style submission. To produce a JAMA / Cochrane / dark / LOTR variant, swap the theme = web_theme_nejm() line — every other element survives unchanged.


Code
lancet_data <- tibble(
  outcome = c(
    "CV death or HF hospitalization",
    "Cardiovascular death",
    "HF hospitalization",
    "All-cause mortality",
    "Change in KCCQ score"
  ),
  category = c("Primary", "Components", "Components", "Secondary", "Secondary"),
  hr = c(0.74, 0.82, 0.70, 0.88, 0.85),
  lower = c(0.66, 0.72, 0.61, 0.76, 0.74),
  upper = c(0.83, 0.94, 0.81, 1.02, 0.98),
  events_tx = c(447, 156, 291, 222, NA),
  events_ctrl = c(606, 199, 407, 253, NA),
  n_tx = c(2373, 2373, 2373, 2373, 2200),
  n_ctrl = c(2371, 2371, 2371, 2371, 2180)
)

tabviz(
  lancet_data,
  label = "outcome",
  group = "category",
  columns = list(
    col_group("Treatment",
      col_n("events_tx", "Events"),
      col_n("n_tx", "N")
    ),
    col_group("Control",
      col_n("events_ctrl", "Events"),
      col_n("n_ctrl", "N")
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title    = "Figure 3: Efficacy Outcomes",
  subtitle = "Row groups by endpoint type + column groups by treatment arm",
  caption  = "HR<1 favours treatment.",
  footnote = "Cox proportional hazards model stratified by region."
)






For a worked walkthrough of building a custom branded theme from scratch — neutrals → primary → accent → series anchors → fonts → variants → field pins → wrapped function — see Compose Your Own Brand. For a full long-table treatment with pagination and pooled summaries see Long Meta-Analysis.

title: Feature Showcases
text:
Hierarchical Split Navigation
split_by with multiple variables turns the sidebar into an expandable tree (Outcome > Treatment here), letting you drill into nested subgroups without cluttering the main view.


Code
data(effect_sizes)

tabviz(
  effect_sizes,
  label = "study",
  split_by = c("outcome", "treatment"),
  columns = list(
    col_text("region", "Region"),
    col_text("phase", "Phase"),
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Hierarchical Subgroup Analysis",
  subtitle = "Outcome > Treatment navigation tree",
  caption = "Expand tree nodes to drill into subgroups"
)

title: Feature Showcases
text:
The Full Monty
Everything at once — nested row groups, three stacked effects, sparklines, weight bars, annotations, badges, and a custom dark theme. Useful as a smoke test and as a reference for how features compose.


Code
full_monty <- tibble(
  study = c(
    "ALPHA-01", "ALPHA-02",
    "BETA-01", "BETA-02", "BETA-03",
    "GAMMA-01", "GAMMA-02"
  ),
  program = c(rep("program_a", 2), rep("program_b", 3), rep("program_c", 2)),
  phase = c("Phase_II", "Phase_II", "Phase_III", "Phase_III", "Phase_III", "Phase_II", "Phase_III"),
  # Three effects
  primary_hr = c(0.68, 0.72, 0.65, 0.71, 0.74, 0.78, 0.69),
  primary_lo = c(0.52, 0.56, 0.50, 0.55, 0.58, 0.62, 0.53),
  primary_hi = c(0.89, 0.93, 0.85, 0.92, 0.95, 0.98, 0.90),
  secondary_hr = c(0.72, 0.76, 0.69, 0.75, 0.78, 0.82, 0.73),
  secondary_lo = c(0.56, 0.60, 0.53, 0.59, 0.62, 0.66, 0.57),
  secondary_hi = c(0.93, 0.96, 0.90, 0.95, 0.98, 1.02, 0.94),
  safety_hr = c(0.85, 0.88, 0.82, 0.86, 0.89, 0.92, 0.84),
  safety_lo = c(0.68, 0.72, 0.65, 0.69, 0.72, 0.75, 0.67),
  safety_hi = c(1.06, 1.08, 1.04, 1.07, 1.10, 1.13, 1.05),
  n = c(420, 380, 1250, 980, 1100, 560, 890),
  weight = c(8, 7, 22, 18, 20, 10, 15),
  pvalue = c(0.008, 0.015, 0.001, 0.004, 0.012, 0.042, 0.003),
  trend = list(
    c(0.85, 0.78, 0.72, 0.68), c(0.88, 0.82, 0.76, 0.72),
    c(0.82, 0.75, 0.69, 0.65), c(0.86, 0.80, 0.75, 0.71),
    c(0.88, 0.82, 0.78, 0.74), c(0.92, 0.88, 0.84, 0.78),
    c(0.84, 0.78, 0.73, 0.69)
  ),
  badge = c("Lead", NA, "Pivotal", NA, NA, NA, "Fast Track")
)

# Custom theme
monty_theme <- web_theme_aurora() |>
  set_inputs(brand = "#f59e0b") |>
  set_spacing(row_height = 38) |>
  set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridlines"), TRUE) |>
  set_theme_field(c("axis", "gridline_style"), "dotted")

tabviz(
  full_monty,
  label = "study", group = c("program", "phase"),
  columns = list(
    col_n("n"),
    col_bar("weight"),
    viz_forest(
      effects = list(
        effect_forest("primary_hr", "primary_lo", "primary_hi", label = "Primary", color = "#22c55e"),
        effect_forest("secondary_hr", "secondary_lo", "secondary_hi", label = "Secondary", color = "#3b82f6"),
        effect_forest("safety_hr", "safety_lo", "safety_hi", label = "Safety", color = "#f59e0b")
      ),
      scale = "log", null_value = 1,
      axis_range = c(0.4, 1.2),
      axis_label = "Hazard Ratio",
      annotations = list(
        refline(0.75, label = "Target", style = "dashed", color = "#a855f7")
      )
    ),
    col_group("Results",
      col_interval("primary_hr", "primary_lo", "primary_hi", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
      col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
    ),
    col_sparkline("trend", "Trend")
  ),
  row_badge = "badge",
  theme = monty_theme,
  title = "The Full Monty",
  subtitle = "Nested groups + 3 effects + sparklines + weights + annotations + custom theme",
  caption = "Every major feature combined in one visualization",
  footnote = "Green=Primary, Blue=Secondary, Orange=Safety. Purple line=Target."
)

title: Long Meta-Analysis
text:
A real meta-analysis often has 50–100+ studies, several cohorts, pooled estimates per cohort, and an overall summary. That’s the case tabviz() was built for: declarative columns, semantic row tokens, and pagination that lets the same spec render as a paginated HTML viewer and as a multi-page PDF without rewriting anything.
This page builds the canonical meta-analysis display step by step. We end up with a 70-row paginated forest plot that breaks across three pages by cohort.

title: Long Meta-Analysis
text:
A 70-study cardiovascular meta-analysis
Three cohorts, each with its own pooled summary plus an overall pooled diamond:


Code
set.seed(2026)

# Per-cohort row counts; each cohort gets one pooled summary at the end.
cohorts <- c("Phase III RCT", "Real-world evidence", "Registry")
counts  <- c(22, 26, 18)
n_data  <- sum(counts)

# Synthetic data — designed so each cohort lands in the budget when we
# paginate at 30 rows per page later.
trials <- data.frame(
  study  = c(sprintf("Trial %02d", seq_len(counts[1])),
             sprintf("RWE %02d",   seq_len(counts[2])),
             sprintf("Reg %02d",   seq_len(counts[3]))),
  cohort = rep(cohorts, times = counts),
  year   = sample(2010:2024, n_data, replace = TRUE),
  n      = sample(c(120, 240, 480, 960, 1900, 3800), n_data, replace = TRUE),
  hr     = round(rnorm(n_data, 0.92, 0.18), 2),
  se     = round(runif(n_data, 0.06, 0.20), 2)
)
trials$lower <- pmax(0.30, round(trials$hr - 1.96 * trials$se, 2))
trials$upper <- round(trials$hr + 1.96 * trials$se, 2)
trials$row_type <- "data"

# Pooled summary per cohort (diamond marker via row_type = "summary").
pooled <- trials |>
  group_by(cohort) |>
  summarize(
    study  = paste0("Pooled — ", dplyr::first(cohort)),
    year   = NA_integer_,
    n      = sum(n),
    hr     = round(exp(mean(log(hr))), 2),
    se     = NA_real_,
    lower  = round(exp(mean(log(hr)) - 1.96 * 0.04), 2),
    upper  = round(exp(mean(log(hr)) + 1.96 * 0.04), 2),
    row_type = "summary",
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Overall pooled diamond (one row at the very end of each cohort? overall here)
overall <- data.frame(
  study  = "Overall",
  cohort = "Registry",                       # last cohort in display order
  year   = NA_integer_,
  n      = sum(trials$n),
  hr     = round(exp(mean(log(trials$hr))), 2),
  se     = NA_real_,
  lower  = round(exp(mean(log(trials$hr)) - 1.96 * 0.03), 2),
  upper  = round(exp(mean(log(trials$hr)) + 1.96 * 0.03), 2),
  row_type = "summary"
)

# Assemble in display order: each cohort's data → its pooled → next cohort,
# with the overall pooled at the very bottom.
meta <- bind_rows(
  trials  |> filter(cohort == cohorts[1]),
  pooled  |> filter(cohort == cohorts[1]),
  trials  |> filter(cohort == cohorts[2]),
  pooled  |> filter(cohort == cohorts[2]),
  trials  |> filter(cohort == cohorts[3]),
  pooled  |> filter(cohort == cohorts[3]),
  overall
) |>
  mutate(
    is_significant = !is.na(upper) & upper < 1,   # entire CI below 1
    is_overall     = study == "Overall"
  )




Code
tabviz(
  meta,
  label = "study",
  group = "cohort",
  columns = list(
    col_numeric("year", "Year", decimals = 0),
    col_n("n"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "Hazard ratio (95% CI)"),
    col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  row_type   = "row_type",
  row_accent = "is_significant",      # bold + theme accent on significant rows
  row_fill   = "is_overall",          # strong fill on the overall pooled row
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title    = "GLP-1 receptor agonists — pooled cardiovascular meta-analysis",
  subtitle = "70 studies across three cohorts, with cohort-level and overall pooled estimates",
  caption  = "Significant studies (CI strictly below 1) are highlighted with the accent token.",
  paginate = paginate_letter()
)












TipWhat’s happening on this plot




Three collapsible cohorts via group = "cohort" — click any header to fold a section.
Pooled diamonds at the bottom of each cohort and at the very end of the table, driven by row_type = "summary" (no diamond on data rows; pooled rows render as diamonds via the marker dispatch).
row_accent on the rows whose CI is strictly below 1 — bold + theme accent color. Themable: changing the theme restyles the highlight automatically.
row_fill on the single Overall pooled row — strong row-fill background with auto-contrasting foreground. The fifth semantic token in the RowCluster set; use it when a row needs to be the conclusion, not just stand out.
Pagination at 30 rows/page via paginate_letter(). Page count appears below the plot (“Page 1 of 3”); use the prev / next buttons or toggle continuous mode for scrolling.
Group integrity keeps each cohort intact across page breaks — keep_groups = TRUE is the default, so a cohort either fits on the current page or moves to the next.

title: Long Meta-Analysis
text:
Saving as a multi-page PDF
The same spec emits a multi-page PDF where each logical page becomes one PDF page:
save_plot(meta_table, "meta-analysis.pdf", width = 800)
# Multi-page PDF merged via qpdf::pdf_combine()
For the rendering pipeline (V8 → SVG → rsvg → qpdf), the qpdf-missing fallback (numbered series), and the per-render override surface, see Export → Pagination.

title: Long Meta-Analysis
text:
Adjusting the pagination for slides
The same spec at slide density (16 rows per page) — useful when each cohort needs its own slide deck:


Code
tabviz(meta, ..., paginate = paginate_slide())   # 16 rows / page


Or fold pagination off for a single-image PNG without warning:


Code
save_plot(meta_table, "wall.png", paginate = NULL)

text:

title: Interactivity Showcase
text: Every widget below is live. Sort, filter, drag, edit — any change flows through to the SVG/PNG export at the click of the download button (WYSIWYG).

title: Interactivity Showcase
text:
The whole kitchen sink
Sort, filter, column reorder, row reorder, double-click edit, hover tooltips — one plot, every feature on.


Code
data(glp1_trials)

tabviz(
  glp1_trials,
  label = "study",
  group = "group",
  columns = list(
    col_group("Study Info",
      col_text("drug", "Drug"),
      col_n("year", "Year"),
      col_n("n"),
      col_events("events", "n", "Events")
    ),
    viz_forest(
      point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
      scale = "log", null_value = 1,
      axis_label = "Hazard Ratio (95% CI)",
      axis_range = c(0.4, 1.5),
      axis_ticks = c(0.5, 0.75, 1.0, 1.25),
      axis_gridlines = TRUE,
      annotations = list(
        refline(0.85, label = "Pooled HR", style = "dashed", color = "#00407a")
      )
    ),
    col_group("Results",
      col_interval("hr", "lower", "upper", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
      col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE)
    )
  ),
  row_type = "row_type",
  row_bold = "row_bold",
  interaction = web_interaction_full(),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "GLP-1 Agonist Cardiovascular Outcomes",
  subtitle = "All interactivity on — try everything"
)












TipHow to try each feature




Sort → click a column header. Click again for descending, again to clear.
Filter → click the funnel icon next to a header. You’ll get a text / numeric / categorical UI depending on the column.
Reorder rows → hover a row, grab the drag handle on the left, drop within the same group.
Reorder columns → hover a header, grab its drag handle. Columns inside “Study Info” stay inside “Study Info”.
Edit → double-click an editable cell (text, numeric, percent, p-value, date, interval) to edit inline, or double-click a column-group header to rename the span.
Export → download SVG or PNG; the file reflects your current state exactly.

title: Interactivity Showcase
text:
Filter + sort on a dashboard
Large-ish dashboard where filters really pay off — apply any combination of filters and the export matches the filtered view.


Code
data(airline_delays)

tabviz(airline_delays, label = "carrier",
  group = "month",
  columns = list(
    col_group("Carrier",
      col_badge("carrier_type", "Type",
        variants = list(
          Legacy = "info",
          `Low-Cost` = "success",
          `Ultra Low-Cost` = "warning"
        )),
      col_n("flights", "Flights", abbreviate = TRUE)
    ),
    col_group("Performance",
      col_heatmap("on_time_pct", "On-Time",
        palette = c("#fee2e2", "#dcfce7"),
        min_value = 0.65, max_value = 0.95,
        decimals = 2, show_value = TRUE),
      col_progress("satisfaction", "Satisfaction", max_value = 5),
      col_numeric("delay_vs_avg", "Delay", decimals = 1)
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "delay_vs_avg", lower = "delay_lower", upper = "delay_upper",
               null_value = 0,
               axis_label = "Delay vs avg (min)"),
    col_sparkline("trend", "12-month trend")
  ),
  interaction = web_interaction_full(),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Airline Performance by Month",
  subtitle = "Try filtering by carrier_type or on_time_pct; export matches the view"
)

title: Interactivity Showcase
section: Drag to reorder within column groups
text:
Drag to reorder within column groups
Columns inside “Baseline” and “Efficacy” swap cleanly; the forest column stays where it is. Whole column groups can also be dragged among top-level siblings.


Code
set.seed(42)
n_studies <- 8
trial_data <- data.frame(
  study = paste0("Trial-", LETTERS[1:n_studies]),
  arm = rep(c("Active", "Control"), length.out = n_studies),
  n_baseline = sample(200:800, n_studies),
  age_mean = round(runif(n_studies, 55, 72), 1),
  female_pct = round(runif(n_studies, 0.35, 0.65), 2),
  response_rate = round(runif(n_studies, 0.3, 0.8), 2),
  median_duration_months = round(runif(n_studies, 8, 24), 1),
  hr = round(runif(n_studies, 0.55, 1.05), 2)
) |>
  mutate(
    lo = pmax(0.3, hr - 0.2),
    hi = pmin(1.4, hr + 0.2)
  )

tabviz(trial_data, label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_badge("arm", "Arm",
      variants = list(Active = "success", Control = "muted")),
    col_group("Baseline",
      col_n("n_baseline", "N"),
      col_numeric("age_mean", "Age"),
      col_percent("female_pct", "Female")
    ),
    col_group("Efficacy",
      col_percent("response_rate", "Response"),
      col_numeric("median_duration_months", "Dur (mo)", decimals = 1)
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_label = "HR"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)")
  ),
  interaction = web_interaction_full(),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Reorder within column groups",
  subtitle = "Try dragging columns inside \"Baseline\" — they stay inside"
)

title: Interactivity Showcase
section: Edit forest cell values in place
text:
Edit forest cell values in place
Double-click any marker to pop up the estimate / lower / upper editor. The marker moves, and the export picks up the edit.


Code
set.seed(11)
effects <- data.frame(
  metric = c("Mortality", "MI", "Stroke", "HF Hospitalization",
             "CV Death", "All-cause Death"),
  hr = c(0.74, 0.81, 0.92, 0.68, 0.71, 0.85),
  lo = c(0.62, 0.68, 0.77, 0.54, 0.58, 0.72),
  hi = c(0.88, 0.95, 1.10, 0.85, 0.87, 0.99),
  events = c(412, 289, 174, 356, 298, 512),
  n = c(12800, 12800, 12800, 12800, 12800, 12800),
  pvalue = c(0.001, 0.02, 0.22, 0.0004, 0.002, 0.04)
)

tabviz(effects, label = "metric",
  columns = list(
    col_n("n", "N"),
    col_events("events", "n", "Events"),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1,
               axis_range = c(0.4, 1.4),
               axis_label = "Hazard Ratio"),
    col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
    col_pvalue("pvalue", "P", stars = TRUE)
  ),
  interaction = web_interaction_full(),
  theme = web_theme_nejm(),
  title = "Double-click a forest marker to edit",
  subtitle = "Estimate / Lower / Upper popover; export reflects edits"
)

title: Interactivity Showcase
text:
Proxy-Driven Updates
Inside a Shiny app, tabviz_proxy() exposes a fluent verb API — add_column(), sort_rows(), filter_rows(), set_theme(), set_zoom(), and more — that mutates the live widget without triggering a full re-render. The snippet below is excerpted from inst/examples/fluent_shiny_demo.R; Shiny apps can’t execute inside Quarto, so the code is shown for reference only.


Code
library(shiny)
library(tabviz)

trial_data <- data.frame(
  study = c("EMPA-REG", "CANVAS", "DECLARE", "CREDENCE", "LEADER", "SUSTAIN-6"),
  hr    = c(0.86, 0.86, 0.93, 0.80, 0.87, 0.74),
  lower = c(0.74, 0.75, 0.84, 0.67, 0.78, 0.58),
  upper = c(0.99, 0.97, 1.03, 0.95, 0.97, 0.95),
  n     = c(7020, 10142, 17160, 4401, 9340, 3297),
  year  = c(2015, 2017, 2019, 2019, 2016, 2016)
)

ui <- fluidPage(
  fluidRow(
    column(3,
      actionButton("sort_desc",    "Sort HR desc"),
      actionButton("filter_hr",    "Filter HR < 0.9"),
      actionButton("clear_flt",    "Clear filter"),
      actionButton("theme_jama",   "Theme: JAMA"),
      actionButton("theme_lancet", "Theme: Lancet"),
      actionButton("add_col",      "Add 'year' column"),
      actionButton("rename_hr",    "Rename HR to 'Hazard'")
    ),
    column(9, tabvizOutput("plot", height = "500px"))
  )
)

server <- function(input, output, session) {
  output$plot <- renderTabviz({
    tabviz(trial_data, label = "study",
      columns = list(
        col_numeric("hr", "HR", decimals = 2),
        col_numeric("n",  "N"),
        viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lower", upper = "upper",
                   header = "HR (95% CI)",
                   scale = "log", null_value = 1)
      )
    )
  })

  proxy <- tabviz_proxy("plot")

  # Each verb sends a custom message to the live widget — no re-render.
  observeEvent(input$sort_desc,    sort_rows(proxy, "hr", direction = "desc"))
  observeEvent(input$filter_hr,    filter_rows(proxy, "hr", operator = "lt", value = 0.9))
  observeEvent(input$clear_flt,    clear_filters(proxy))
  observeEvent(input$theme_jama,   set_theme(proxy, "jama"))
  observeEvent(input$theme_lancet, set_theme(proxy, "lancet"))
  observeEvent(input$add_col,      add_column(proxy, col_numeric("year", "Year"), after = "n"))
  observeEvent(input$rename_hr,    update_column(proxy, "hr", header = "Hazard"))
}

shinyApp(ui, server)


What the demo shows:

Sort / filter / clear → row operations applied client-side via the proxy; the ${id}_filtered_rows input updates so the server knows which rows are visible.
Theme switch → swaps colors, typography, and spacing in place without losing scroll or edit state.
Add / rename / resize columns → structural mutations with no full render.
Zoom → live scale change (0.8, 1.0, …) on the whole widget.

The same verbs (add_column, sort_rows, set_theme, …) work on static WebSpec and htmlwidget objects too; on a proxy they dispatch a custom message, otherwise they mutate the spec in place.
To run locally:
shiny::runApp(system.file("examples/fluent_shiny_demo.R", package = "tabviz"))

title: Interactivity Showcase
text:
Enable only what you need
web_interaction() takes any combination of flags. Use web_interaction_full() for everything on, web_interaction_minimal() for a read-only widget, web_interaction_publication() for no UI chrome at all, or dial it in yourself:
web_interaction(
  enable_sort          = TRUE,
  enable_filters       = TRUE,
  enable_reorder_rows  = TRUE,   # drag rows within their group
  enable_reorder_columns = FALSE,
  enable_edit          = TRUE,   # double-click to edit
  enable_select        = TRUE,
  enable_hover         = TRUE,
  enable_resize        = TRUE,
  enable_export        = TRUE
)
All reorder / edit state lives in the browser session. SVG/PNG exports capture the current view, but a page reload returns to the server-supplied data. For Shiny round-trips, the ${id}_selected and ${id}_hover inputs fire on selection/hover changes, and the tabviz-proxy message handler accepts applyFilter, clearFilter, sortBy, and toggleGroup methods.

title: Gallery
text: Live tabviz() widgets across nine pages. Every figure renders from the same data-frame-plus-columns API — sort columns, click filter funnels, drag rows or columns, double-click cells to edit, switch themes from the gear icon. Pick the page closest to what you want to build.

title: Gallery
text:
Flagship galleries


Long Meta-Analysis
70 studies across three cohorts with cohort-level and overall pooled summaries, paginated across three pages. Exercises the most-recently-shipped surface end-to-end: pagination, the five-token semantic surface (row_fill on the overall row, row_accent on significant), row_type = "summary" for pooled diamonds.


Domain Dashboards
Information-dense dashboards across sports analytics, customer feedback, portfolio performance, product metrics, airline ops, and climate science. Rings, pictograms, sparklines, heatmaps, badges — each lead picks the column types that fit its domain.


Compose Your Own Brand
End-to-end build of a fictional pharma brand (Acme Cardio Research) one named input at a time. Eight progressive steps from neutrals to a wrapped web_theme_acme() constructor, each with the rendered table at that stage so you see exactly which input controls what.


Pictograms & Rings
Every mode of col_pictogram() and col_ring() with realistic per-mode examples — count vs rating, range remapping, per-row glyph selection, layout = “stack”, auto status-palette thresholds, and the row-token cascade reaching into glyph cells. Closes with a six-row survey-feedback dashboard putting them all together.

title: Gallery
text:
More galleries


Clinical Examples
JAMA / Lancet-style forest plots, subgroup analyses, multi-arm dose-response, nested hierarchical groups.


Interactivity Tour
Live-behavior showcase: sort, filter, drag-and-drop, double-click editing, theme switcher, WYSIWYG SVG/PNG export.


Feature Showcases
Statistical models, distribution viz, conditional styling, fluent customization, publication templates, and the full-monty smoke test.


LOTR (Easter Egg)
Three editorial-register themes (dwarven, elvish, hobbit) with bespoke pictogram glyphs and tongue-in-cheek source data.

title: Gallery
text:
Example sources
Every gallery example is also available as a runnable R script in the package:
# List the curated gallery examples shipped with the package
list.files(system.file("examples", package = "tabviz"), pattern = "^gallery_")

# Run one
source(system.file("examples", "gallery_26_paginate_meta_analysis.R",
                   package = "tabviz"))

text:
Where to read first

Domain Dashboards.


Designing a branded theme
Compose Your Own Brand and the Themes guide.


Picking column types for new data

title: tabviz
text: tabviz turns a data frame into a publication-quality, fully interactive htmlwidget — sortable, filterable, draggable, editable — with SVG / PNG / PDF export that captures exactly what you see on screen.

title: tabviz
text:
Hero example












TipTry the interactivity




Sort — click any column header.
Filter — click the funnel icon and pick a text / numeric / categorical filter.
Reorder rows — hover a row, grab the drag handle, drop within the same group.
Reorder columns — drag column headers; columns stay within their column group.
Edit values — double-click a cell (or a column-group header) to edit inline.
Export — the download button writes SVG / PNG matching your current view exactly.

title: tabviz
text:
Selling points


Declarative columns. The columns = list(...) API mirrors what you see — column types, groups, and visualizations compose with no hidden state.


WYSIWYG export. The same SVG engine drives both the browser and headless export, so PDFs match the screen pixel-for-pixel after sort / filter / edit.


Built for science. Forest plots, multi-arm effects, p-values, intervals, a curated theme roster (the clinical nejm default plus editorial, design, and expressive presets), and multi-page PDF pagination for long meta-analyses — all first-class.


Fully interactive. Sorting, filtering, drag-to-reorder, inline editing, and Shiny proxy updates ship in the box.

title: tabviz
section: Build any table in a few lines
text:
Build any table in a few lines
library(tabviz)

tabviz(data,
  label = "study",
  columns = list(
    col_group("Demographics",
      col_text("site"),
      col_n("n")
    ),
    viz_forest(point = "hr", lower = "lo", upper = "hi",
               scale = "log", null_value = 1),
    col_group("Results",
      col_interval("hr", "lo", "hi", header = "HR (95% CI)"),
      col_pvalue("pvalue", "P")
    )
  ),
  interaction = web_interaction_full()
)
